contents: Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me I will say though, i think it takes a really good physio to make that link. i'm lucky that mine is rather amazing and able to make the links like one hip is a tiny bit further forward than the other = muscles trying to pull it back = tight muscles which also pull on the bladder. i get pain internally in this line that we established is due to one particular muscles with a knot up on my hip bone...i just got a fancy tool for internal trigger points to use when it hurts and is really helping reduce that pain good luck with that!

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me Originally posted by KikiUK Oh Chrissy, i totally get it! it's so frustrating to not do things we want. i asked my physic about swimming as the house we are renting has a pool, she said the urologists she works with tend to say avoid it when super sensitive as usually irritates. I'd love to swim int he pool with my kids! i know, changes all...travel really needs to be self catering with good shops near by. eating out--can i suggest you find a couple decent restaurants you can order to your needs? Gluten free is actually pretty easy i find (been that for 14 years), most chains now do gluten free. problem is all the other stuff we can't have... my solution has been finding a few nice restaurants that will do totally plain fish + veg for me. not exciting, but means we can eat out. granted is more expensive, but then we barely eat out now. pain is that we can't just pop in somewhere if we are out all day. i have a day without kids next weekend, really looking forward to playing tourist--but as always means i have to pack food for the day. a argh! however if you have a Pret a Manger near you they now do pots with just boiled eggs and spinach--would at least keep you going ;-) good luck with the manager, hope that they are nice about it. re exercise, i got some cheap weights (Decathlon for those in UK great) and i just try to use them a couple times a day. put them in the kitchen to do whilst cooking in those waiting moments. at least my arms are getting some exercise and not losing muscles. thinking some squats as well, plus walking. just don't want to lose more muscle....any other do at hoe suggestions welcome by us all i'm sure! I tend to walk on a treadmill and do low impact weight exercises to tone. Sometimes I do fast paced walking and even though I get a small flare from that I do it to get my heart beating and b.c walking super slow isn't going to help me loose weight. Though family and friends tell me I don't need to loose weight, which is besides the point. KikiUK - I have that same issue with not eating out. I never hangout with anyone b.c either they want to go out drinking or eating and I have such a strict diet I haven't felt brave enough to eat out. It has been 3 years since I ate out. Do you just call the restaurants and see if they deal in gluten free foods? I really want to have a social life again.

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me Originally posted by mhalin01 KikiUK - I have that same issue with not eating out. I never hangout with anyone b.c either they want to go out drinking or eating and I have such a strict diet I haven't felt brave enough to eat out. It has been 3 years since I ate out. Do you just call the restaurants and see if they deal in gluten free foods? I really want to have a social life again. Mhalin, I have been gluten free for 14 years. For a long time, this was my only trigger for bladder symptoms, so eating out was easy--just find stuff that was gluten free. i would check out menus, call restaurants, or go places with allergy menus. truth is gluten free is easy--most places can do that. you just need to check they don't put flour on fish etc etc... however, now it's trickier. are you very diet sensitive? i am super duper diet sensitive, i.e. i can eat meat / fish / pears / raspberries / coconut / almonds / pine nuts / pumpkin seeds / and about 15 vegetables. that is it so far. so eating out is really tough. my strategy is: 1. i won't let this stop me going out with friends / family. if i can eat great, if not i enjoy the company and just drink some water. 2. if i go to friends' houses i explain what i can eat that is easy--roast meat nothing on it + my safe veg. worked so far. Donna suggests bring something you can eat to share & eat 3. Restaurants. I eat out a lot less but I do eat out. I go for nice restaurants that will cook me my safe food. Eating out i go for plain fish (NOTHING on it) + green veg that are safe for me nothing on them. I explain i have severe food allergies and everyone has been lovely. I do take bicarb before i eat out to be extra safe, but no problem so far though once reacted to the water--i think there had been lemon in it (metal jug, couldn't see...). so we eat out less as this is more expensive, & i miss gorging myself on too much food or a curry, but means we still go out. In fact when my dad came last year we ate out most days--i became the salmon expert as i ate it every day we were out! not exciting, but meant i shared in family meals out which was more important. so be brave! find a few places you can eat out. what is your safe foods that might be doable? my great excitement was my husband and i were out the other day, and went into a chain of cafes here to eat. I was able to get a little to of boiled eggs & spinach, & some coconut water, and eat with him--without any planning! was great ;-)

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me KikiUK, I am very diet sensitive. I have a very limited array of foods I can eat. I tried fish once upon a time but I think it was one of my triggers so I stopped eating it. It is so hard to figure out what my triggers are and sometimes I may get a flare from something else and think it was another thing I ate. I eat sweet potatoes (which I make into potato chips and fries, I eat chicken a lot. I believe turkey is a trigger for me believe it or not. So I always eat chicken....which gets tiring after awhile. I am too afraid to try something new and have a terrible flare. I can also have bosc pears, coconut, kind bars, lara bars, so far I can only have a few types of green veggies, no corn or carrots though. I eat a special type of bread as well. I try to mix it up by creating different dishes with the same foods. If only I could have pasta. I wish I had a friend who was going through the same thing as me in my own home town...it would make this easier I think. I need to try harder...I am letting my depression get the better of me. I really don't ever talk to my friends...lives get in the way I guess. So the only support system I have is my mom. This is so frustrating. But it is nice to have a forum like this to be able to talk to others who have IC too. Maybe I should try fish again....I just know that not everyone with IC has the same reactions to foods... So safe foods: sweet potatoes, natural chicken (with no added solution), green beans, broccoli (sometimes), coconut, dates, honey, organic pear jam, bosc pears, at the moment that is really all. That and I have a special type pf gluten free tapioca bread that I eat. I can't have cereals, rice (which baffles me), oats, pasta (even gluten free), fish (which has been a year or more since I tried-thinking about trying it again), etc...you name it and I can't have it. That is why I want to go through NAET treatments if I could find someone locally or at least not more than 2 hours away from where I live. Thanks for the advice

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me Why we can't have stuff is odd i know. rice and grains also flare me. i think it's because they convert to sugar and in some of us sugar feeds this, plus it is inflammatory. carrots are also sugary... funny thought what we can have--ie that you are managing ready made bars which have a lot of ingredients and sugar. have you looked at what is in them, and then gone for those ingredients individually to widen your diet? what about greens like kale (cooked) can you do that? cucumbers and celery are on the very safe list for a lot of people... i'm reading great book called Whole 30 It all Starts with Food. It'll make you feel ok about not having grains. I got a Julienne peeler and make Zucchini spaghetti and fry it in oil. replaces pasta. you ightw ant to try fish again--make sure it's totally plain. fish is anti inflammatory, so great if you can do it! sounds like you could use with some more support. you mention about your depression. are you getting help? stress really affects IC and our pelvis bladders...managing that might really help. if i get anxious bladder gets awful! can you get some support around that? I have found that really useful, plus listening to meditations daily to help me stay calm. really recommend it.

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me I did not know that rice and grains can convert to sugar...that is strange. I am glad to know that. An carrots too! That flabbergasts me. I don't know why I can't have corn though....hum. And to answer your question no I haven't looked into the ingredients in those bars to widen my diet. But there isn't much in them. In the Lara Bars I believe is coconut which I know I can have, dates and almonds...I can have all of those. And at the moment I can't think of all what is in the Kind Bar, honey I know for sure which in small amounts I can have. I will look into that. Are the authors of that book Dallas and Melissa Hartwig? I was trying to find the book. I may get that book and read up on it. It sounds like a very interesting read. Thanks for telling me. I tried Zucchini before but because I don't remember to write things down I don't remember if it was an allergen food or not. Sheesh I need to organize my IC life better. But that dish sounds tasty. Yeah I will def try fish again. What types of fish are you able to eat? I was eating Tilapia before. And no I am not getting help. Most of the time when I am reading or watching anime or movies I don't have time to think about my bladder and I am happy. But other times I feel self-pity and I know that isn't what I should be doing but I can't help it. Oh I know how you feel. I get anxious a lot b.c I am a nervous person especially when people are fighting (I hate conflict) or if I have to go in front of people and speak...I get bladder spasms and need to pee urgently. So I understand that. I am better then I was when I was younger but it is still there. Meditations? I will have to try that. I wish I had a better support system. I tried to find support groups where I live but they are like 4 or more hours away. I guess I could try to find other people in Owosso with IC (not sure how) and start up a support group.

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me There's a few things you might find useful to research... anti inflamatory diet gives info on foods that are inflammatory overall. one site i really like that you might find useful is http://healinginterstitialcystitis.com she does a free online 4 week course around nutrition that you might find useful a lot of things become sure sugar in our body. research low GI diets but also about candida diets--not to say you have to follow that, but you'll get a sense of it. carrots are really sugary, as are potatoes, squash, so maybe you are sensitive. the bars will have a lot of sugar, so check what your reaction to those is. couple years ago i took a long time to heal following a bladder infection--now realize it was a low grade IC flare. but, sugar / yeast / starch all made it worse... try foods slowly over a few days. a couple bites of fish day 1, 5 bites day 2, then whole piece...go slowly.that way if you react, it won't be so major. i eat all fish. usually i poach it, so maybe poach some trout (freshwater) for starters as freshwater fish (not farmed) is the least inflammatory. you'll get there, have faith. Find support if not for this, then just for life! i think relaxing is so important, and friends are a great way to do that if you have good ones. I go out and drink well, water, but i'm with my friends! btw yes, they are the authors of the book. it's not an IC book but great understanding of food science! i'm signing off for a couple week as going on holiday, and tomorrow need to finish packing and plant seeds ;-) hope to hear that things are looking up when i get back ;-)

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me I just found that website and was reading about the 4 week course before you messaged back. Scary cool. LOL. I am going to try it. Thanks again for the advice. Have a great holiday. I might message you sometime if you don't mind about how my progress is going. -Meghan
Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me would be great to hear! Kiki

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me I'm 63. For years, I've walked 2-3 miles on a treadmill almost every day and done a PT program that was developed because of my IC every day. Had to stop upper body weights because of bad wrists. I started to do Pilates classes a little over 3 months ago--mostly Reformer and Tower. I'm up to 5/6 classes a week. If I do a Pilates class, I don't do my PT program because a lot of the exercises are similar.

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me Originally posted by kathymack I'm 63. For years, I've walked 2-3 miles on a treadmill almost every day and done a PT program that was developed because of my IC every day. Had to stop upper body weights because of bad wrists. I started to do Pilates classes a little over 3 months ago--mostly Reformer and Tower. I'm up to 5/6 classes a week. If I do a Pilates class, I don't do my PT program because a lot of the exercises are similar. I have started to do Pilates too as this a recommended exercise to do. I found that even if I am in pain doing a Pilates class can easy the pain. I think it is because it stretch out pelvic muscles. I would love to try other forms of exercise too but don't know what to do. I would love to go swimming. I have tried swimming with a non petroleum barrier cream as recommend by some. I think it works. I was seeing a physio specialist today who didn't know much about IC. I was going about my knee I have Patellae Femoral Joint pain on my left knee. So I am a bit wary of doing anything to irritate my knee until I have had physio for it. Thoughts Ladies and gentlemen

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me Originally posted by elanoali I have started to do Pilates too as this a recommended exercise to do. I found that even if I am in pain doing a Pilates class can easy the pain. I think it is because it stretch out pelvic muscles. I would love to try other forms of exercise too but don't know what to do. I would love to go swimming. I have tried swimming with a non petroleum barrier cream as recommend by some. I think it works. I was seeing a physio specialist today who didn't know much about IC. I was going about my knee I have Patellae Femoral Joint pain on my left knee. So I am a bit wary of doing anything to irritate my knee until I have had physio for it. Thoughts Ladies and gentlemen Have you ever heard of Peter Egoscue?? He's an exercise therapist. I had his book, Pain Free. Ended up getting the DVDs http://www.amazon.com/Egoscue-Pain-F...ct_top?ie=UTF8 It turns out that the beginners one had a lot of the exercises, or similar ones, that I did for my PT program. It's really nice. They explain the exact positioning you need to do. I recommend it all of the time. If I'm not doing any other exercise for the day, I'll do the advanced DVD.

Hi thanks for the suggestion I have the new dawn pilates by Jenny Lelwica Buttaccio which I have found to be very good.

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me Originally posted by elanoali Hi thanks for the suggestion I have the new dawn pilates by Jenny Lelwica Buttaccio which I have found to be very good. The Egoscue material isn't Pilates. He's an exercise therapist who believes that pain is caused by misalignment of the body. When your shoulder, hips, knees and ankles are out of alignment, it causes pain. I did PT and then this program almost every day for 2 1/2 years when I needed major abdominal surgery. They felt that I came through the surgery so well because my pelvic floor muscles were so strong. I started back to exercising after three weeks. I think it really helps with my bladder issues by maintaining the work I did with the physical therapist to get my pelvic floor muscles to work correctly.

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me Originally posted by kathymack The Egoscue material isn't Pilates. He's an exercise therapist who believes that pain is caused by misalignment of the body. When your shoulder, hips, knees and ankles are out of alignment, it causes pain. I did PT and then this program almost every day for 2 1/2 years when I needed major abdominal surgery. They felt that I came through the surgery so well because my pelvic floor muscles were so strong. I started back to exercising after three weeks. I think it really helps with my bladder issues by maintaining the work I did with the physical therapist to get my pelvic floor muscles to work correctly. So what you are saying is that this is very good for pelvic pain such as ic. It sounds really good. I don't have to spare money to buy it at the moment. Is there any other suggestions people have for exercise?

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me I got a DVD of yoga for pelvic pain, and do that + stretches + lots and lots of walking. i also have some lightish weights at home that i do a few arm exercises with to keep a few muscles there!

Re: Can't seem to find an exercise that doesn't flare me Anytime "movement' triggers more symptoms, I always look to a muscle origin. Sitting to standing, standing to sitting, etc. etc. Yes, of course, repetitive bouncing is certainly going to irritate a tender bladder... but if you keep things smooth it should work well. Beware weight lifting as you could be accidentally putting some extra strain on those muscles. Sometimes just taking a break is the way to go, especially if you're already in pain. Hard but true. I'm a gym rat... I love working out but I had to cry uncle a few times too! Rowing is literally the perfect exercise.. the pelvis is still... arms and torso and legs get the workout. And it's an awesome workout. You might try that! Jill

contents: Has anyone ever tried Zumba? I been taking Zumba now for a month and seeing results. My body is so toned and tight. I take the class for 2 hours a week. Mon nights is a combined Latin and Hip Hop music. Sat mornings is just Latin music. I love love Pitbull too! With having learning disabilities and having been bullied most of my life, Pitbull's music has made me happier. I actually wrote to him and did get a personal response back from the fan club! I'm so happy. I listen to Pitbull's music all day long at work, in my car, on the metro on the way to work and back home. I never thought that a rap singer could make me happier about myself. I just got into his music in March. I already memorized 7 songs that the instructor has taught us in class. So I practice at home too. We have danced to: Justin Timberlake-Sexy Back Pitbull-Bon Bon Pitbull-Maldito Alcohol Shakira Feat. Pitbull-Rabiosa Wisin & Yandel Feat. J Lo- Follow the Leader Usher-Scream Daddy Yankee-Pose Daddy Yankee-Limbo PSY-Gangnam Style Will Smith-Gettin Jiggy With It Pussycat Dolls-Jai Ho (You Are My Destiny) Get Cool-Shawty Got Moves LMFAO-Sexy and I Know It Juan Magan Feat. Pitbull and El Cata - Bailando Por El Mundo The other songs I don't know their names because its of groups I don't know of. Someone asked me since I like Pitbull so much if I too like PSY. I nearly threw up in my mouth! Pitbull is way better than PSY especially in the looks department. Any good results from anyone who did Zumba? Did it irritate the bladder more?

That is ok though. The difference between when I was diagnosed and now, is that I have embraced that fact. Me and the loo, we are one.

contents: Re: Zumba There is also a version called Zumba Gold. I think its a little slower paced. It's designed for seniors but I assume anyone can take it, regardless of age. I love my Zumba classes and used to go 5-6 days a week, now more like 3-4 simply because it's hard to find the time. To make it IC friendly I take a lot of the impact out of it, so when the instructor is jumping I am usually just stepping. It's still a great workout. I always do extra stretching after class, too, because one song with a bunch of 30 second mini stretches will not get the job done for me and my tight pelvic floor muscles. I am a sucker for the Latin music and dance styles like salsa/bachata/cha cha, so I probably know all of those artists better than the hip hop ones. I also enjoy musicians like Pitbull and Shakira who blur music's cultural lines. What I like best about Zumba is that for an entire hour I stop thinking about my medical problems, my insurance woes, my other stressors -- I'm just too busy having fun. Usually that natural dance high carries over for quite a while after class ends.
signature: History: IC diagnosis in 1995, then 10+ years remission, flared up again in 2009, now well controlled Medications: Elmiron, Nortriptyline, Hyophen, Singulair, Zyrtec, Tizanidine, Prelief, Flector, Vicodin, Valium suppositories, quercetin, glucosamine, probiotics Self Care: Meditation, massage, acupuncture, dancing, walking, stretching, avoiding triggers, staying positive and spending time with my amazingly supportive fiancé

contents: Re: Zumba I loved zumba but it did not love me back. Set my IC recovery back by over a decade in just six weeks. I now cannot sit down for the pain and have been going to pelvic therapy 3x a week for over a month with minimal improvement. Zumba is bad for anyone with high tone pelvic muscles which is frequently an aggravator or those with IC. Please monitor and be mindful. Don't want you in my shoes (I was going 3x a week so once might be ok). Last edited by lauraads; 06-03-2014, 03:14 PM. Reason: Accidentally published before done

contents: Re: Zumba I used to do Zumba 3 or 4 times a week, until my bladder pain got so bad in October of 2013 (started in February 2013). I haven't done it since then because I hoped if I did everything right my pain would go away. It hasn't and I've gained more than 20 lbs. So I decided if I was going to be in pain either way, I'm going to try Zumba again. I'm going tonight. Wish me luck and I'll report back how it's going after a few classes.

Re: Zumba Oh, I am cringing at this. I hope you are OK. I can tell you after 18 pelvic floor therapy sessions (3 a week) I am still struggling from my zumba marathon. Many of us with IC have pelvic floor disorder. For me I do and a major component is high tone. Classes like zumba can effect that tone. If you have still been in pain since you last did zumba, there is a chance it could be that you too reactivated (or started) high pelvic muscle tone as one of the issues of your disorder. This sort of thing rarely gets better on its own - requires actual physical therapy, relaxation sessions, sometimes biofreedback because of the reflex arc (or pain cycle) - your muscles squeeze so your bladder hurts so your muscles squeeze which makes your bladder hurt more. Pretty soon your muscles stay squeezing and your bladder stays mad. Anyway, taken to extremes like me (and it crept right up on me) you can squeeze those muscles so much that you end up irritating the pudendal nerve. Now, after almost 3 months I still cannot sit down without pain. Working at a standing desk and lying down whenever I can (since continued standing irritates my bladder). What can you do when you are self employed and rely on your work to make your income?? So, please be careful. I cannot recommend enough looking for a pelvic floor therapist to check into your pelvic floor muscles. Zumba could very well make your situation much worse as it did mine. I zoomed past years of pelvic therapy to go back to being as bad as I was 10 years ago (except then I could sit) in just six weeks of zumba going three times a week. I never saw the serious pain coming. One day little flares and the next living hell.

contents: Re: Zumba Well...I drove to the wrong place and then locked my keys in the car, so zumba wasn't meant for me last night anyway. Your comments have me worried though, since I too have major pelvic floor dysfunction. I just started with a physical therapist so I will ask her about it before I jump back in. I'm just so sick of being fat. I have put on nearly 25 pounds since the pain started. It isn't helping my overall disposition at all. I'm barely eating, but I just can't lose weight without burning calories. I appreciate your advice, though. You may have saved me from misery. Catherine

Re: Zumba :/ I never thought that Zumba would cause IC symptoms more worse. I just feel it in my knees the next day. But I don't jump with high intensity like some of the other women in class. I had bunion and hammer toe surgery on right foot 4 months ago and still recovering. So for now I'm not jumping like PSY does in his music video Gangnam Style. If something requires skipping or jumping, I just march. Pony is kinda painful so I just move my hips around. What is that pelvic floor dysfunction I read about on the boards? I thought that cardio like Zumba would make IC feel better because I thought cardio itself stretches the bladder. I haven't noticed my IC getting worse or better during Zumba. I understand that Yoga is great for IC patients.

contents: Re: Zumba You may have dodged a bullet! But I hear you on the body image/fat thing. I struggle with my thyroid so that even when I was doing 12 hours of cardio a week and a little weight training I struggled to lose even part of a pound. In three months of watching calorie intake, eating healthy, and getting plenty of exercise I lost 9 pounds. In the 8 weeks I have been back in pain I have gained 7 pounds back. I was crying to my pelvic therapist today about it. Of course she does marathons and is absolutely gorgeous. She said, "That is secondary to getting you better and out of pain." You can worry about your weight then. We are particularly tough on ourselves for what many of us are suffering with! But, it doesn't help that I feel bad about how i look and feel physically excluding pelvic/bladder! Hang in there! Laura

contents: Re: Zumba I have done Zumba about twice a week for the last month, but I am worried about it. So far, it's been ok. It hurts for a while after, but is usually better within about 24 hours. I do have some fear that my IC could have been caused or seriously exacerbated by zumba. It's the only thing I did differently before the IC began. I got really into Zumba and did it 3-4 times a week for about a year and a half before the pain started. My pelvic floor muscles are destroyed...all knotted and toned, too shortened to really relax. Is it crazy to blame Zumba?

Re: Zumba I just looked at a couple of Zumba exercises on u-tube --- to me it looks like a lot of "jarring" movements. I have to stick with smooth move exercises, such as walking (outdoors in good weather, treadmill other times). I can also tolerate an exercycle if I don't stay on too long. Donna

contents: Re: Zumba If you are not seeing a pelvic floor therapist while doing Zumba you are endangering yourself. A YEAR after my zumba-related injury I am still in horrible pain and cannot sit down AT ALL. Many of us with IC have developed (or had first) pelvic floor dysfunction. If your muscles get too tight as a result of zumba, you can -- LIKE I DID -- damage your pudendal nerve. I have seen a pelvic floor therapist 1-3 times a week for an entire year. Not sitting and having to work standing up at my desk is a nightmare. I think we can do zumba with IC but we have to be monitored. Trust me that it is not worth the risk without PT. I managed to go from a January 2014 PT checkup with a fine pelvic floor to doing this nerve damage in two months (end of March). It happens fast but in the interim it just feels a little uncomfortable. It is like putting a frog in room temp water and slowly raising the heat until he boils -- you don't notice it until it is too late. I all but beg anyone doing zumba to see a pelvic floor therapist regularly (like at least once a month) so you don't end up like me. I have horrible regrets about doing zumba. If I had any idea that there was something SO FAR WORSE than I ever experienced with 20 years of IC, I would have been more careful. But I had no one to tell me. Just imagine the worst of what you experience in your bladder being spread throughout your entire pelvis, then add in continued sexual arousal, feeling like your vulvar skin is being rubbed off, stinging, BURNING FLAMES in the tissues of your pelvis... If I thought I had quality of life issues before, I was crazy. IC at its worst is a cake walk compared to this. I can't say enough that those of us with some kind of pelvic dysfunction such as IC to take precautions by getting your pelvic floor checked out regularly or just avoiding this type of activity. I belong to a FB community where there are a whole bunch of us who have developed Vulvodynia and Pudendal Neuralgia symptoms by 'trying to get physically fit'. Rant done -- I just want to save others from my hell!

contents: Re: waterproef swim shorts Thanks, I've emailed them to ask questions ;-) i thought there were little shorts that looked like swim shorts, but maybe I imagined it… I'd swum early on in this ongoing "flare" (if this is what it is) and felt worse the next day. assumed it was the peanut butter as never occurred to me that swimming pool could affect me, but reading on the forum made me wonder otherwise, so i'm avoiding swimming with the kids which is a shame! but i'm doing everything i can at the moment, whilst I wait to see the doctor in a few weeks...

Re: waterproef swim shorts Hi, I bought the SoSecure and have been able to swim in our pool and swam in the ocean in the Key West. Just know that if you have frequency problems make sure that you empty them out before rinsing off. I am so happy that I found these!!!

contents: Re: waterproef swim shorts that is great to know! so no water gets in? i emailed them and they said it might get down the back… i would have thought the ocean would be ok…we're going on holiday in April and will have a pool and the beach. does anyone flare from sea water?
Re: waterproef swim shorts Sea water is fine from my experience and everything I've read on these forums
signature: Diagnosed Nov 2012 after birth of my daughter. I feel like IVF hormone injections triggered my debilitating IC symptoms, but that I had it mildly even before that and didn't realize it. Currently taking daily: probiotics, licorice root, vitex, vitaminB12, thyraxis, D3, Calcium/Magnesium, permeability factors, digestive enzymes. Nightly castor oil packs on my abdomen. I follow the IC diet, anti-inflammatory diet, gluten and dairy-free (most of the time), with as much organic, local food as possible. My approach is to try to get to the root of the problem, which according to everything I've read, is probably centered in the gut (read, chronic constipation my whole adult life). I have a list of things to try and keep checking things off my list. My inspiration has been Wendy Cohan's Better Bladder Book.

contents: I find exercise a good way to deal with IC pain but the exercise I miss the most is swimming. I find that some pool chemicals cause flare ups. I am an avid swimmer and am limited to lakes and ocean swimming. Has anyone found a way around this problem? Like a special swimsuit or? I do an exercise like Zumba five times a week for an hour. Even if it doesn't take my pain away I feel like I have done Something to help myself. We will be going to a beautiful resort next week with 5 pools. I would love to swim!

Re: anyone had any luck dealing with pool chemicals? Ideas? There was a post a while back about bathing suit bottoms or undergarments that were waterproof that were used for swimming in chlorinated water. I can't remember what the brand name of them were though- darn. Maybe someone else here remembers.

Re: anyone had any luck dealing with pool chemicals? Ideas? There have been some posts by people who use a thick layer of Vaseline to help protect from chlorine. Some places don't use it in their pools, but those can be hard to find. There's a pool in my area with a salt filter system --- or you could check to see if there is anywhere that they use something different. If you do try for a dip in the pool, please be sure to change out of that wet swim suit immediately after you swim. Sitting around in a wet suit can be very irritating. You might check with a sporting goods store about the possibility of a waterproof suit. Good luck! Donna

Reusable Swim Diaper http://www.sosecureproducts.com An effective and discreet swimming undergarment for adults and teens and children. Sosecure Swim Diapers are an effective and discreet reusable containment swim diaper for adults, youth, or children dealing with incontinence. So secure swim diapers are recognized to be the most effective containment swim diapers available. i emailed the company and they said that some people water will leak in at the back due to curves, but others find it works very well. i really want to swim too, but am afraid to go!

I ordered a DVD online called "Core Fusion" and it is a Bar workout. I thought I would try it because it's low impact. I have been doing it for the last few days (coincidentally I also started a flare). It's basically a dancer's type workout using a chair or a bar. It's very small movements but it hurts like crazy! I felt like it was safe because it didn't seem to affect my core and there was no cardio. Just curious if anyone has tried it.
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Hello, Just wondering if anyone has had any success with exercising. I find this limitation very frustrating part of my IC. I have tried rowing, recumbent bicycle, joining the gym and having a PT help me by writing a program that would avoid moving or working my core muscles as when I do any of those exercises the pain is horrible. I just want to move because for one it makes me feel better mentally and two I have put on weight since being diagnosed with IC and I would love to get back to a more healthy weight (my PT said my body fat ratio was too high). Any successful workouts you have done with no pain consequence would be lovely to hear about, Many thanks, Stacey.

Re: I want to exercise so badly Stacy I feel your pain. I have gained a few pounds, certain areas got bigger too. My family says I am still tiny, but I have fat in those hard to get rid of regions (buttocks & stomach-core area). Not a lot thank goodness but I feel unhealthy. I feel gross. With IC it really limits the types of activities and exercise you can do. Again--everyone is individual so what one person can do in exercise another may not be able to tolerate. When I had free time over the summer I walked (fast and slow paced) and did low impact aerobics and at the time it was working for me. Then life got in the way and I got too busy to exercise, now I am back to walking on an incline on the treadmill and exercising on the elliptical at low pace and it works for me. But I also have been trying to incorporate low impact aerobics again into my exercise. I use weights too but I try not to strain my bladder in any way. You really just have to try things and see if they work for you. If you can find a good dance aerobics video it can make exercise fun again. I hope this helps <Meghan
signature: I've had IC I guess my whole life, however, I was diagnosed in Spring of 2011. I've tried colostrum, Cystoprotek, and other herbs and meds. I drink Marshmallow Root Tea & Raspberry Leaf Tea --- sometimes I drink Chamomile Tea too! My urologist just put me on myrbetriq for OAB. Hoping to be prescribed Hydroxyzine to decrease night time voiding, daytime frequency, pain (burning and pressure) You're right: I don't look ill. Interstitial Cystitis is an invisible disease. And see, sweetheart: you don't look stupid either! "Everyday is a battle, everyday is a struggle and I refuse to be defeated! Interstitial Cystitis will not beat me! Depression will not concur me! I will fight like a girl!" -Author to me unknown

contents: Re: I want to exercise so badly About the only exercise I can do is low impact, such as walking. I can also do an exercycle if I'm cautious about it and don't overdo. Donna

contents: Re: I want to exercise so badly What about an elliptical? Or yoga? Are these okay?

Re: I want to exercise so badly In the beginning when I was pretty bad the only thing I could do was walk - either on a treadmill or outside. I was also able to do many of the pelvic floor exercises. After my bladder started to calm down I was able to do the stationary bike again but only for short periods at a time. For some reason the recumbent bike put too much strain on my pelvic area. I know how frustrating it is not to be able to exercise when you know how badly you need to. There are DVDs here in the ICN shop for exercise for people with IC. You could try one of these. Several people have said yoga and pilates were ok for them. My core muscles had gotten so weak from lying around with a heating pad I thought I would never get back to the way I was before but I'm slowly getting back there. There was an exercise my pt showed me to help the core I could do while standing up and bending forward from the waist with knees bent. It did help, and didn't seem to put much pressure on my bladder.

Re: I want to exercise so badly Thank you Meghan for replying. I have tried fast pace walking and it hurts. I have not tried low impact aerobics though or dance so I will look around the net for something. I was thinking of buying some weights and trying to make up my own routine at home also. I appreciate you taking the time to answer. Thank you Stacey Originally posted by Mhalin1990 Stacy I feel your pain. I have gained a few pounds, certain areas got bigger too. My family says I am still tiny, but I have fat in those hard to get rid of regions (buttocks & stomach-core area). Not a lot thank goodness but I feel unhealthy. I feel gross. With IC it really limits the types of activities and exercise you can do. Again--everyone is individual so what one person can do in exercise another may not be able to tolerate. When I had free time over the summer I walked (fast and slow paced) and did low impact aerobics and at the time it was working for me. Then life got in the way and I got too busy to exercise, now I am back to walking on an incline on the treadmill and exercising on the elliptical at low pace and it works for me. But I also have been trying to incorporate low impact aerobics again into my exercise. I use weights too but I try not to strain my bladder in any way. You really just have to try things and see if they work for you. If you can find a good dance aerobics video it can make exercise fun again. I hope this helps <Meghan
Re: I want to exercise so badly Thanks Donna. Yes it is very limiting. Especially when exercise can tip me into a flare. I will keep experimenting. Stacey

Re: I want to exercise so badly Hi There, Tried the eplliptical with no luck cause pain. Yoga I have not mainly because I am wanting something that will help with weight loss as well as mental benefits of exercise. Many thanks, Stacey. Originally posted by HRJ What about an elliptical? Or yoga? Are these okay?

Re: I want to exercise so badly I can still bike, but I do not do it sportive, and I go to back exercises and yoga classes. I lost extremely lot weight on sugar-free diet for a month for fructose intolerance, and need to do it this month for 2 months again. If you have good discipline, 100 % sugar free, but eating everything else normally, makes a great difference.

Lately, my IC has been doing very well, so I've been changing my workout routine to one that burns more calories and includes abdominal exercises. The other day, I did my usual strength training, but added in the cardio and abs that I usually skip. It involved quite a bit of jumping around. And the abs were mostly crunches and bicycle type exercises. I felt fine afterwards for about an hour. Then, I noticed a sharp increase in my frequency for many hours that day. It did resolve later on, but it kinda freaked me out. It may have nothing to do with the exercises, I don't know. I have been feeling good again all weekend, so I am going to work out again this morning. I was wondering what types of exercises cause pain for you? Are abs worse? Or jumping exercises? It aggravates me that I can't burn more calories and I haven't done abs for so long, and I need them! Any help is appreciated - thanks!
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Re: Which is worse for you - abdominal exercises or cardio (jumping)? Any high impact cardio, especially with abdominal, is a trigger for me. As a matter of fact, I'm convinced that my daily workouts helped put me into the "flare of a lifetime" and lead to my diagnosis...but I've got PFD which factors in. My PT has instructed me to do very low impact aerobics, preferably walking or the elliptical trainer and I've not yet been able to return to any kind of abdominal work as it triggers spasms every, single time. It's such a Catch 22 because my abs have gotten so weak that it's exacerbated my herniated, lumbar spine disks. I guess if I were you, I'd do the workout with the added ab work a couple more times and see if you get the same increase in frequency. If you do, then you'll have your answer.
signature: ************************************************************** Experienced first symptoms in 2007 Diagnosed with IC 6/13 Diagnosed with PFD (Pelvic Floor Dysfunction) 8/13 Medications: .5 mg. Ativan, occasionally for spasms Supplements: DH Aloe Vera capsules, 3 per day for maintenance and 6-12 per day during flares Following Strict IC Diet

contents: Re: Which is worse for you - abdominal exercises or cardio (jumping)? Other than walking it was hard to find something that would burn calories and for me to get enough cardio. My pt told me to power walk but after a long time of power walking it just about killed my ankles! Maybe you could just do the cardio and see what happens and skip the abs. If that feels ok then you would know it was the ab work that caused the increase in frequency. I'm able to do the stationary bike now at the gym but there are still many exercises that target the core that I stay away from. Unfortunately my core is now getting quite weak so I have to find something from the pt that will help.

Re: Which is worse for you - abdominal exercises or cardio (jumping)? Thanks ladies. I know what you both mean about getting weak around the middle/core. It's so frustrating. I'm 43 years old and I need to exercise on a regular basis to keep myself strong. I used to exercise all the time, and for the last year, I've not been able to and I have become so weak, it's terrible. I remember I used to be able to do full pushups during my workouts. This morning, I could barely do the modified (knees on the floor). Lorisue, I believe I have some PFD issues as well. That's why I think the crunches bother me. earthlady, I don't mind walking, but I feel like the rest of my body is getting so pathetically weak. I can do weights, so that's good, but I miss the cardio as well. This morning, I skipped the abs (but I did do some planks), but still did the cardio. Guess we'll see what happens. I hate that IC has robbed me of my workouts too. :-(
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Re: Which is worse for you - abdominal exercises or cardio (jumping)? IK suggest you start out very slowly --- only a very few minutes at first, gradually building up. I can't do any exercises that are jarring or that put stress on my abdomen or my back --- that pretty much says walking is about the only exercise I can do.

contents: Re: Which is worse for you - abdominal exercises or cardio (jumping)? I know what you mean. I miss my Zumba dance classes and just walking doesn't make me feel I'm getting an all over body workout that uses all my muscles.

Saw this on Doc Oz today. Was wondering if anyone has tried this and it helped. I tried his green tea first thing the morning to help you get started and such. Didn't think about all the acid in it and my bladder was sooooooooooo upset for a week. But I can't stop snacking.

I see a couple of people have posted questions about exercise but I'm still not positive about what is safe. I was going to a personal trainer once per week before this started. I stopped because I wasn't feeling great but think I may be able to try again. I need to know what kind of exercises I should stay away from. I'm assuming any jumping or high impact. What about ab exercises? Are they all pretty safe? Any other suggestions? Thanks, Amy
Re: Exercise question Everyone is different, but for me, any abdominals cause me to flare. I can't jump at all either. Strength training and weight lifting (and walking) are all I can do.

contents: Re: Exercise question Thank you. I feel like there has to be some way to get those abs!

Re: Exercise question Originally posted by AmyGig I see a couple of people have posted questions about exercise but I'm still not positive about what is safe. I was going to a personal trainer once per week before this started. I stopped because I wasn't feeling great but think I may be able to try again. Thanks, Amy I had the same question. I am in remission but am still scared. I would like to know if gentle yoga and palaties are ok.
signature: First diagnosed June, 2014 by MD Confirmed diagnosis July, 2014 by specialist Uro. Current medications: Amitriptyline 150mg Omeprazole 40mg Hydrocodone 10/325 as needed (4x day) Pyridium 200mg as needed (4x day) Armour Thyroid 30mg Diagnosed with IC, Acid reflux and Hypothyroidism. I have mainly urethral burning and pain as well as vaginal pain. No history of bladder infections and did not experience any symptoms of IC until June, 2014 when I was diagnosed. On an elimination diet, found that I do not tolerate preservatives well. I do not eat any prepackaged food unless it is organic and made w/o preservatives. God Bless, Julie

contents: Re: Exercise question The exercising is different for everyone in what they can do. When I was having full flares all I could do was walk ( walking had a positive effect on my pelvic muscles) and upper body strengthening with some weights but only if I did them sitting down. It seemed any pressure at all on my lower body bothered me. When the flares stopped and I was feeling better I was able to use the stationary bike at the gym but only if I didn't over do it in length of time. Since I've been in remission I'm still careful about overdoing the stationary bike but I am able to do a lot more than I could before. My rule of thumb was that if I felt any pressure or irritation in the pelvic area I stopped what I was doing. There are CD's here in the ICN shop for exercise that may be worth looking into. Several people here have mentioned that yoga has helped them and haven't read (so far) any negative comments about it making them worse.
Re: Exercise question I do best with what I call smooth exercises --- walking, especially walking in a swimming pool, some swimming, gentle stretches. Donna

contents: I just got my diagnosis this week and let me tell you I am overwhelmed I love biking so I'm definitely feeling sad about saying goodbye to that... but my biggest passion is hiking and backpacking. A few years ago I hiked the John Muir Trail in California and it has always been a dream of mine to do the PCT. I was just wondering if there were any hikers or backpackers out there who could offer me some hope. Have people been able to do strenuous hiking without flares?

Re: Any hikers/backpackers out there? Hi there, I know that biking can be painful with IC. I have heard of people getting special seats for their bikes, so they are still able to ride. As far as hiking and backpacking, if you can get your IC under control (which does take some time and trial and error), I don't see why you would not be able to do it. I was diagnosed in January and prior to that, was in a 3 month flare that basically ruled my life. It was awful. But I did get better with treatments and diet. I was in an 8 month "remission" where I only had mini flares during ovulation and my period. I did everything I wanted to do. I even went to Cedar Point Amusement Park (one of the biggest in the US) and rode all the big rides with no problem. I am currently in a week long flare that is calming down now, so I'll just take it easy until I start to feel better again. IC doesn't have to end your passions. It may make it harder for you, but I do think it could be doable once you get your symptoms under control. By the way, what are your symptoms and have you started any treatment yet?
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Re: Any hikers/backpackers out there? It really depended on if I was in a flare or not. I did do a day long hike with a group of other hikers and I have to say I was pretty miserable. The trail had a lot of climbing and it did seem to put a lot of pressure on my pelvic area. My symptoms at the time were burning and irritation and as the day went on it got worse for me. The ride to the hiking area was pretty bumpy which did not help at all. Other times if my symptoms were cramping ones without burning I was actually ok and the cramping actually got better. If the trail was pretty flat and most of it was level walking it was ok but if there was strenuous climbing I wasn't. For me it depended on what kind of symptoms I was having at the time and how difficult the trail was. Biking was pretty hard on my bladder but when I went to remission I was able to use a stationary bike with no problem.

Re: Any hikers/backpackers out there? Originally posted by my2boys99 Hi there, I know that biking can be painful with IC. I have heard of people getting special seats for their bikes, so they are still able to ride. As far as hiking and backpacking, if you can get your IC under control (which does take some time and trial and error), I don't see why you would not be able to do it. I was diagnosed in January and prior to that, was in a 3 month flare that basically ruled my life. It was awful. But I did get better with treatments and diet. I was in an 8 month "remission" where I only had mini flares during ovulation and my period. I did everything I wanted to do. I even went to Cedar Point Amusement Park (one of the biggest in the US) and rode all the big rides with no problem. I am currently in a week long flare that is calming down now, so I'll just take it easy until I start to feel better again. IC doesn't have to end your passions. It may make it harder for you, but I do think it could be doable once you get your symptoms under control. By the way, what are your symptoms and have you started any treatment yet? Thanks for the advice! I will look into getting a special seat for biking. My symptoms began as urinary urgency and frequency, but in small increments. Then difficult urination (difficulty starting stream, etc). Then UTI like symptoms (pain, burning, stinging). Then pelvic aching/pain that radiated to my back and thighs. I'm currently feeling okay, just some pain due to urgency. (I think I'm out of the "flare"). I have not started any treatment yet I've started the diet, but it is challenging, and I went to my first PT appointment today for PFD which was very helpful but have much work to do. I have taken advil and amitriptaline (at night). Definitely still looking for what treatments will work for me. Suggestions appreciated! (Especially alternatives to medications). My urologist was sympathetic but not particularly knowledgeable. I haven't exercised at all in the past 3 weeks of this flare but am definitely ready to start working out again. Am certainly a little afraid about what exercises are going to trigger more flares.

Hi everyone. Well, I really need to do some excersising. Before IC I used to run 3 miles 3 to 4 times a week. But that all came to an end. Last year I became very ill with not only IC but I had to have major surgery to remove Pelvic Floor Mesh that had been in for 5 years. I had to be out of work for 5 months and could barely get out of bed. After healing in May I started having all kinds of probelms and was diagnosed in JUne with IC. I have not excersised in over a year. I hear that it is not good to run because of the pounding motion. What about an Elliptical machine? Has anyone had any luck with that. I have always been a small woman and now I have gained 16 pounds over the las year already. I know some weight gain is due to some of the meds I am on but I am hating it and want to excersize. I still have bladder and pelvic pain everyday but I am learning that this is the new normal for now. I just wanted to see who else may use the Eliiptical. Thanks.

(Romans‬ 12‬:12‬ NIV) God Bless: Lorrie

contents: Just wondering what kind of exercise everyone does. I try to go to yoga class a couple times a week. The only exercise machine I use is the elliptical, & I'm wondering if anything that's higher impact would actually do more harm than good. Does anyone do any form of aerobics or does that tend to make things worse? I was considering trying a step or cardio class but I feel like the intensity will irritate my bladder & pelvic floor.

Re: Exercise Hi Domv, I may not be typical among ICers, but I find that moderate to high impact exercise is very tolerable. In particular, I love running. The first few miles always feel like "oh no, I'm about to pee my pants" but then after a while the urgency fades and I can immerse in the activity. When I first was diagnosed 2 years ago, I did cut out everything other than yoga and walking. After slowly adding in exercises again, I found what exercises are actually intolerable for me: jumping (e.g. jumping rope, jump squats, jumping jacks) is the worst. But I was able to resume my daily bike commute and mountain biking with my husband. One thing that I've found is that riding up steep hills requires a good deal of abdominal tension, which causes some horrific flares. So my rule riding up hills is that I either stand up the whole time, or get off my bike and walk. Of course, if I'm really in horrible shape I don't get on a bike at all, even the seat pressure can be too much. I think the best thing you can do is just try it and see. I took a boot camp class a while back and actually DID pee my pants a little bit on one of the high-impact exercises. I confessed this to my pelvic floor therapist and she said "oh everyone does, just wear tights with shorts over them and don't sweat it!"

Re: Exercise I can't tolerate any high impact exercises. Running is one thing that is totally out for me. I can walk --- in summer I love to swim and just walk in the pool. I have a treadmill to walk if the weather is awful. Donna

contents: Hello everyone! I'm really interested in trying yoga-- I used to do it fairly frequently before the IC, though always at a beginner level-- but I'm worried about poses that require lying face down. I found a study online that listed yoga poses that are helpful to people with interstitial cystitis and other pelvic disorders (http://choicespc.net/wp-content/uplo.../04/Yoga-2.png), but I can't imagine it being comfortable to lie face down, which is required for some of them. I can do child's pose, since the pelvis is elevated enough that the bladder/belly stays away from the mat, but some poses like cobra pose seem like they'd be incredibly uncomfortable. I can't stand to lie face down on my stomach. (If I'm lying out at the beach, I either don't lie face down or I dig a hole in the sand to give my belly some room.) I'm interested in going to a yoga class, but I'm worried about those poses. Has anyone had that experience? And does anyone have advice about modifications? Thanks in advance for any input you may have!
signature: I wish lots of love, support, understanding, and good medical research to everyone who has chronic pain conditions! Current treatment: IC diet, fish oil capsules, independent physical therapy for pelvic floor dysfunction, marshmallow root tea, dilator

contents: Re: Want to try yoga, but am worried about poses lying down Originally posted by MsGulia Hello everyone! I'm really interested in trying yoga-- I used to do it fairly frequently before the IC, though always at a beginner level-- but I'm worried about poses that require lying face down. I found a study online that listed yoga poses that are helpful to people with interstitial cystitis and other pelvic disorders (http://choicespc.net/wp-content/uplo.../04/Yoga-2.png), but I can't imagine it being comfortable to lie face down, which is required for some of them. I can do child's pose, since the pelvis is elevated enough that the bladder/belly stays away from the mat, but some poses like cobra pose seem like they'd be incredibly uncomfortable. I can't stand to lie face down on my stomach. (If I'm lying out at the beach, I either don't lie face down or I dig a hole in the sand to give my belly some room.) I'm interested in going to a yoga class, but I'm worried about those poses. Has anyone had that experience? And does anyone have advice about modifications? Thanks in advance for any input you may have! I don't have the same symptoms as you because lying down doesn't cause me pain. The great thing about yoga is that they often stress doing your own modifications if necessary. If and when there is a pose that you are not comfortable trying, go into child's pose. Child's pose is the default pose to go into if you're "skipping" a pose... or you could even sit with your legs crossed.. Maybe mention it prior to class? Good luck! Yoga is a great exercise and I hope it helps your IC.

Hi there, been on these boards for ages, it seems but have been lurking for quite some time now. I have done a search for weight loss surgey or bariatric surgery & only found a few posts about ladies who underwent the procedures back in 2011. I am looking to see if there is anyone out there with a diagnosis of IC who has had this surgery? I have been thru the wringer in my attempts to lose weight. This is my last resport & it has come down to it. My docs are agreeing that my weight is at a point that is very un healthy. I have NOT spoken to my urogyn or my rhum to hear their opinion, but what I need to hear now is real life stories of people who had it done. Be it bad experience or good, I would be so thrilled if anyone shared their story. my biggest fear is that this complex of a surgery will cause other auto immune issues that are dormant to come to the surface or aggreavate my bladder even more than it is already is. I have a history of rhum issues, that currently are at bay. My ic is the biggest problem, I have a pretty severe case & have been on disable for about 3 years. I must do something about losing weight, I cannot deal with diabetes or heart disease or the myriad of other issues that can come about from obesity. I am at 36% body fat, so I def qualify. Any feed back is most appreciated. Feel free to private message me also, if needed. I am also posting this to a few other boards, as I see some are more read & active than others. Thanks so much for reading this!!
signature: Karen Currently Taking: 20 mg Celexa fentanyl patch 12.5 mcg chgd every 3 days morphine IR 15 MG, up to 3x daily Xanax 1mg as needed (not taken daily) 900 MG Oxycarbizine (mood disorder) 300 MG Welbutrin Tizanidine 4 mg up to 20 mg daily as need Prevacid 30 mg Ambien as needed my heating pad is my best friend, use daily on my lower back & pelvic area blessed to be able to receive massage & Amma , along with Avazzia therapy 3x weekly Been diagnosed for 10 years now, consider myself pretty well versed in this disease & would love to help out with new IC sisters or brothers, feel free to message me.

contents: Hey everyone, I was diagnosed with IC in August and am currently still learning what works and what doesn't for me. I am a yoga teacher and have been seriously practicing yoga for over 10 years. However, in the last few months I have had to stop teaching and even stop my normal practice because I feel like it makes my symptoms worse. With the help of diet and medication I feel like my bladder pain is improving, but I have a lot of trouble with urgency and frequency and notice that this gets worse with most exercise and the car!!! I am thinking it has to do with my pelvic floor muscles being too tight and core work etc can make that worse.... I am starting physical therapy on Friday... I hear a lot about how yoga has helped people with IC, but I am curious what yoga you are actually doing, and what modifications are made to accommodate to the needs of an IC patient! I am trying to up my meditation and restorative practices but would love some advice if anyone has any! Thanks!

Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool I know next to nothing about yoga, but I think you might find Amy Stein's book helpful. It really explains the inter workings of PFD and related muscle function-which activities are good and not so good and why. PT will give you lots of insight too.

Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool I was a Yoga teacher too. I was about to open a studio when I got frozen shoulder the first time. Later, IC stopped me from practicing Yoga. I can't do any floor exercises right now because I'm in huge pain, however, when I'm better I use "NewDawn Pilates; A Pilates-Inspired Workout adapted for people with Pelvic Pain" by Jenny Buttaccio. I bought it from the ICN shop...I don't know if they still have it. I've tried all kinds of Yoga since getting IC 2 years ago. I just can't do it without pain. I can only walk a little and even that makes me worse. BUT, the relaxation I learned from practicing Yoga for over 30 years has come in handy. All of that practice is there now that I need it. Car rides are bad for me too. I went through a few months of pelvic floor PT too. Above all else you have to be gentle to yourself. Accept things the way that they are and gently do what you can. My main IC symptom is pain but urgency and frequency are also a problem. Take your time and adapt all of your teaching knowledge like you were your own client. You wouldn't be pushy with a student, would you?

Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool Can you please tell me who you are going to for physical therapy? I live 2 hours away from Denver-up in Wyoming. Being in such a rural area means we have to travel for better medical care. Also, what doctor do you go to? Thank you!

Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool Hey Everyone! Sorry for my delayed response, I went into a big flair and have been having some neuro pain too, so I've been in TLC focus mode Thank you for all of the positive feedback! My meeting with my PT was hugely successful. I was a dancer before getting into yoga and she said I have a classic case of hyper-mobility with out enough strength. I also have some neuro-muscular issues and have cause me to over work and grip my core (and in turn pelvis) to keep myself stable. It turns out all my years on rotating my thighs in, engaging my core and tucking my tail bone have made my pelvis closed in on itself and over engaged. haha the irony is ridiculous! But I am ready for the challenge of "undoing" this training, and learning how to stabilize my body in a way that supports the conditions I have. I am sure I am not the only yogi with this issue! smd: I will look into that book by Amy Stein, I am a huge research nerd about all of this stuff so anything I can get my hands on to read I am excited about! Clevsea: Thank you so much for the kind and supportive words! I have never done pilates but my pt said that once we get some of my basic issues dealt with I will probably start. It has been really hard giving up my practice but you are right that the meditation and lifestyle practices help so much when dealing with IC! I have a lot of training in restorative yoga and adaptive yoga and am trying to focus on those styles to help myself heal, and help with my anxiety! I am excited to see the progress I made with my PT, she is also a yogi so she knows what my body habits are on which ones are hurting my poor little bladder and pelvic floor WymoningIC I have some great docs here in Denver. My urogynocologist is Terry Dunn at Foot Hills Urogynocology. She is AMAZING and her staff are all wonderful! My PT is through Rocky Mountain Osteopathic Medicine and Physical Therapy. The woman I am going to is named Nikki and she specializes in women's health. If you are in need of a good doc I highly recommend both of them! The two offices also work together so any notes from either office are shared. Which is great! If you have any other questions please let me know! I will continue to keep you all updated on what I learn about movement while in PT. It's pretty cool stuff and hopefully it can help more of us ICers get some relief!

Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool It's the same with me. I started in ballet at 5 years old and I've always been super bendy, well, hyper-mobile. I still am. So I had all the flexibility and I did have some strength but that was outmatched by my flexibility. I wish that all IC people had something in common so we could figure out the "why" of all this. Certainly not all IC-ers are extremely flexible, right? All of the spinal twists I did (in the name of good body health) have backfired on me too. My sacrum doesn't know if it's coming or going and is very hyper-mobile. Now I have to work on stability and not on twisting. Last edited by Clevsea; 04-23-2014, 12:41 PM.

Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool I just started taking yoga classes and I go on Mondays. I am taking ashtanga yoga, and mostly I'm just doing sun salutation A & B and standing sequence. I do ok with it, and haven't noticed any types of flares with it. But, I just started taking yoga and are not anywhere near the noodle that my instructor is.
signature: ---------------------------------------------------- My blog on IC, adoption, and academia: the garlic whisperer Diagnosed Jan 2013. Currently taking: 200mg Elmiron morning and night 25mg Elavil at night (no drowsiness or weight gain) culturelle in the morning SLIT drops for allergies (environmental) 3 drops/night under the tongue zyrtec 24 hour nightly (recommended by uro) Instillations 3x/week: Parson's Solution uribel and prelief as needed Also took Desert Harvest Aloe (3 in the morning and 3 at night) for a year and had great luck. No longer need them, so I only take them during flares.

contents: Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool Hi Sargie- I have been to Terry Dunn and you are right-she is amazing. I finally felt like a doctor cared about what I was going through. I just saw her once but am hoping to get back down this spring. Thank you so much for your help.
Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool Has physical therapy helped you guys???
Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool My main symptom is pain.. And I wounder if physical therapy will help this symptom???
Re: Advice on how to modify and use yoga as a tool I've never tried PT, but my yoga instructor talks about pelvic floor muscles all the time. I kind of look at my yoga practice as PT

contents: I've had mixed messages about if its good for IC or not. I love to workout and have a gym membership. I haven't been able to do much with it for several months due to something going wrong in my hip. Is exercise good for IC? Its a dress reducer for me but don't want it to flare. I usually spend a decent amount of time on the treadmill and weight lifting. Any thought?

Re: exercise friend or foe? Gosh, I hear ya. I've always exercised and I can't figure out if it helps me or hurts me. I know one thing for sure - stretching out the pelvic area ALWAYS helps me. I can lift weights with no problem. But sometimes even walking bothers my bladder some days. I guess it's the movement. I like to do Jilian Michaels Ripped in 30, but I fast forward the cardio (or do it low impact with no jumping), and limit the abdominals, which also seem to bother me some days. It's still a very hard DVD with doing only that!
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Re: exercise friend or foe? I will try to stay away from anything targeting the stomach. I've been dealing with a lot of pain due to a hip injury that's still not healed and just got the diagnosis of IC last week. Ive been feeling really down and hoping going to the gym will help. I'm afraid to do anything to cause a flare. I had one yesterday that almost sent me to the hospital.

Re: exercise friend or foe? I have to stick with what I call smooth exercise --- walking is my primary one. I can't do anything that involves running, jumping --- anything jarring. I also can't lift. Donna

contents: Re: exercise friend or foe? Walking was my best friend when my IC was active. Some days the cramping was pretty bad for the first 5 or 10 minutes but if I continued walking the pain went away and I felt a whole lot better. I always thought it might have something to do with getting more blood flow to the area and relaxing the muscles. That was about all I could do at first, but later I was able to do other things at the gym. The upright bike put too much pressure on my pelvic floor so I used the recumbent bike on a low tension for short periods of time. Some machines at the gym were ok as long as I wasn't jarring the abdominal area.

A number of years back before my second Hydrodistention/cystoscopy i was doing zumba often and i was fine with it. Mainly just urgency frequency then and it didnt make that much worse. I stopped zumba for awhile when i had arotator cuff problem and had to heal that. Now since second hydro i have pain much more often and pain doing zumba at times. I can do zumba but within 30 have to pee cause of pain and into the next 30 i start to hurt and peeing helps just a tiny bit. I dont want to give up Zumba cause i love it so much. Any tips to help with the pain during or after Zumba? Also i now have 2 new small kidney stones on right side. Could this be the pain from the kidney stones doing zumba affecting my IC? My kidney stone doc wants me to work out. Im guessing to see if that helps the stones to pass. I had a stone before which didnt pass for months and i had to have it blasted. btw i also have Ulcerative Proctitus which gives me bowl problems. Oh i also do Curves machines.
signature: IC ulcerative proctitus lower back pain uterine fibroid allergies A friend is a person who reaches for your hand and touches your heart There are no rules. That is how art is born, how breakthroughs happen. Go against the rules or ignore the rules. That is what invention is about. Helen Frankenthaler :woohoo:

contents: Re: Zumba with IC Oh be careful. Like you I did zumba and started having little issues with discomfort, frequency, etc. I worked around it because I loved the classes. What I didn't know was that the high pelvic tone I had worked so hard to correct with my pelvic therapist was inching back. Suddenly my little flare turned into a blaze of the worst IC experience in 22 years. I cannot sit down without terrible pain, so I don't. I have given up all physical activity just to survive my intense pain and suffering. I have been standing/lying down and going to pelvic therapy 3x a week for over a month. I am sloooowly getting better. So please be careful and pay attention to your symptoms.

Then I'll try it at the back of the class, I think, so I can slip out if it's too much. I'm also giving myself permission to only do half the class to start with.

contents: I did Zumba class before and I have to tell you, they really work your abdominals so be prepared for a little jostling around that area. It was loads of fun though and I think I was in a mini remission at the time because I don't recall having any IC issues after it. I don't recommend doing it during your time of the month though, you'll get tired so much quicker and your body won't do what your mine wants it to LOL.

contents: zumba I take zumba every saturday morning.It doesnt bother me but, the only exersize that does a little is runnning.

I tried it - It wasn't bad for me, I did have to slip out and "go" a few times but I think it depends on the routine they're doing also - some were easier than others on your abdominals. I'd say give it a try, with a talk to the instructor - and don't get upset if you have to stop to use the restroom. It was a lot of fun but definately a workout!

contents: Nina --Zumba Search for posts-- mainly on the Dex forum- by Nina. She's a Zumba instructor now. She's been in classes for awhile I'd say. She's actually doing so well that she hasn't posted here in a over 90 days. I see her on FB talking about Zumba. It's on my list soon.

Re: anyone tried zumba? This is just to caution -- I have had IC for 22 years. Thanks to pelvic floor therapy, herbs, supplements, and following an IC diet I started living mostly pain free. After a year of hula dancing I decided to add in zumba. I had no trouble and loved it so upped it slowly but surely to 3x a week plus my hula. I started having little flares after about six weeks but they would go away. Then they didn't and in two weeks I was in worse pain than I have ever experienced. I went back to my therapist and she said my pelvic muscles were tighter than she had ever seen them. For the last five weeks I have not been able to sit down - I stand at my computer to work and I lie down. I am going to pelvic therapy 3x a week and have stopped all activity except puttering and going to bathroom. It has also activated all my GI problems. Anyway, recovery is sooooo very slow and my pain is so intense shifting from burning internal pelvic muscle pain, bladder spasm, urethral spasm, urgency, frequency, etc. I dream about being able to sit! Yes, it was from the frequent zumba and tightening my muscles. I do suffer from high tone and I just went right back there! I would say once a week would be ok but please be mindful!!! Last edited by lauraads; 06-03-2014, 03:00 PM. Reason: Accidentally published before done writing

contents: I have been in a remission for 3.5 months. I am currently going through a small, hormonal flare right now - related to ovulation. It's not horrible, just some increased frequency. However, I do not want to do anything to make it worse. The problem is we are going up north this weekend for Memorial Day. We go every year with the same group of people. Every year, we all bring our bikes and do a lot of biking. It's really something I look forward to doing, but I am really scared I am going to aggravate my IC. If I bounce too much while exercising, my bladder is sometimes affected. Do any of you ride bikes? Do you only do it if you're in remission (or flare-free)? Thanks, Jane

contents: Re: To ride a bike or not?? I couldn't even sit on a bike seat, let alone ride one. I love bike riding and used to do a lot of it. My urethra/bladder pain has me standing, or reclining. If I sit it has to be a very cushion-y seat. But you might do great and it would be a shame to miss out on something so fun!!

Re: To ride a bike or not?? I have been afraid to try...lately. I biked many years with IC, 3 to be exact, as I had only frequency and urgency. Since 6 months I had cramps as well, now lately I do not have them (probably Uracyst instill is helping), but I am scared, as until lately even walking made me cramps. But maybe I will try. After I recover 2 months ovaries infection, which is now, today extremely bad still....

Re: To ride a bike or not?? I can't ride a bike any more, but it's due to back problems. However, I've had diagnosed IC for 39 years now and most of that time I have been able to do it. I do think you should give it a try ahead of time so you'll know how you react. If you star out with a short distance that could tell the story. Good luck! Donna

contents: Re: To ride a bike or not?? No need to feel embarrassed. Everybody's got something. Some people have cancer. Some have warts. Some have one leg (like my husband). Some have dandruff and bad breath. You just happen to have IC. Enjoy the things you know you can do over the weekend, and don't worry about the things you can't. If there's anybody not understanding about your limitations... well, bully for them. Just spend that time doing something different. About the cookies, I meant... bake them before you leave home and take them with you!!
