contents: Re: To ride a bike or not?? Cushy seat, cushy bike shorts, maybe a seat that is hollow in the center. If you see a bump try to stand a little, stand when you can other times, ice the area when you are done...I used to bike alot (when my bladder behaved all those years), but I stopped because of safety (stupid cars!). Sometimes I miss it. I am glad to hear many people can ride-makes me think I can try again. Let us know how it went. Have a great time anyway (great comments Bladderella). Sounds like a cool trip.

Re: To ride a bike or not?? Thanks sdm. I was thinking the same thing - about standing up over bumps. If the road is pretty smooth, it shouldn't be too bad. I think I'll give it a shot. I'll never know unless I try, right?

contents: Re: To ride a bike or not?? Have any of you guys had any luck with this specialized gel seat called a Lithia Gel seat? It's a seat made for women to take pressure off the soft tissue of the pelvis, so that's the vulva area I believe (I'm not positive about that though) and although it has a nose, it's got a slit down the middle that is much longer than normal bike seats. A physio recommended it to me instead of the noseless seat, claiming that a noseless seat would leave me off-balance and teetering and that this bike has helped many of her patients with pelvic pain. I really, really want to hope that this seat might work for me-I miss my bike something terrible after not riding it all last year because I was in so much pain, and I'd really rather not have to buy a new upright bike if I don't have to (those are danged expensive and I love my current bike very much). Any of you guys heard of this seat/had any luck with it? My physio told me that there's really no guarantee that anything (upright bike, recumbent, noseless seat, lithia gel seat etc) will or won't work, which isn't exactly comforting, but it's also not discouraging either.

I'm planning to purchase a rowing machine for my home. I used to be a big runner, but thanks to IC / PFD, I've pretty much stopped running and am looking for new ways to get in some cardio. I heard the rowing machine was good. Is there a particular type (I see a few different variants on amazon - ones with a straight bar you pull to move you towards the machine, others with actuals oar like arms, etc.). For example: Amazon.com http://www.amazon.com/Stamina-Air-Rower-Black-Chrome/dp/B003YPZWT2/ref=sr_1_3?s=sporting-goods&ie=UTF8&qid=1401396785&sr=1-3&keywords=rowing+machine Amazon.com : STAMINA Precision Hydraulic Rower 1205 - Smart Workout App, No Subscription Required - Compact Full Motion Rowing Machine for Home : Exercise Rowers : Sports & Outdoors http://www.amazon.com/Stamina-35-1205-1205-Precision-Rower/dp/B000AMRN44/ref=sr_1_10?s=sporting-goods&ie=UTF8&qid=1401396785&sr=1-10&keywords=rowing+machine Amazon.com : STAMINA Precision Hydraulic Rower 1205 - Smart Workout App, No Subscription Required - Compact Full Motion Rowing Machine for Home : Exercise Rowers : Sports & Outdoors Any advice on which one is less likely to flare me? I know it would probably make the most sense to just try these out at the gym, but where I live (NYC), a gym initiation fee plus monthly membership would be easily far more expensive than just purchasing one of these machines and trying it. Thank you!

Re: Rowing machine advice How about going to a store that sells fitness equipment and trying it out there? It might be helpful to have a salesman there, too, who could answer any additional questions you would have about it. I have a recumbent bike stored in the basement that my PT told me to start using since it's not jarring to the bladder. I did see a post on a blog from an ICer who said this "As a former mega athlete now with IC, I have a very similar routine. Running, stairmaster just don't work because they aggravate my pelvic floor so I stick with exercises that keep my hips level and are fairly smooth and non traumatic to the pelvis. The rower, for example, works beautifully!" Last edited by Bladderella; 05-29-2014, 10:09 AM.
signature: "When you gotta go, you gotta go!"

contents: Re: Rowing machine advice Thanks! That's an interesting idea, although I think it might be tough to get in a long 30 minute workout and then wait and see how I feel the next day on a gym store's fitness machines (do they even let you do this?!). I could literally buy both of these for less than the initiation fee at most NYC gyms, so I guess that's my last resort (or, move somewhere less expensive). Thanks!

It's that time of year where I am supposed to make plans and begin registering for my open water swim events, and I am very afraid. Last year at this time, I was just about to sign up for my first event when my symptoms returned with a vengeance. So, I am almost a little "superstitious." The way I have been feeling lately, I am able to do most everything-even if I am not feeling great. Also, things are going to be alot different due to the diet limitations. All my old rituals of favorite eating places the night before, food and drinks provided in goody bags and at pre and post race refreshment tables, big victory meals afterwards...are all out the window. Will have to totally revise these, and they won't be as much fun. My performance won't be what is was (not that I was ever very fast) either-but that's the least of my worries. There are enough worries without additionally needing to worry about how I might feel that day. Last summer, I wasn't able to do anything, and I went from really missing it to being relieved. Family and friends, who are always interested in my adventures, regularly asked me what I was doing-some knew what was going on-others didn't. I was really hoping things would be better this year, but I am scared! So, I kinda don't know what to do...

Re: What To Do??? Here's what I would do (you will have to make the decision for you!) --- I would register, especially if there's a deadline for registering. You can always cancel if you have to, but it would be sad if you were able to participate and were too late to get your name in the pot. There are lots of people with diet restrictions, whether it be diabetes, allergies, medications, or IC --- so you're not alone there. For me eating out is for the fun of going and seeing friends; the food isn't even the most important thing --- it's the fun of being with family and/or friends. Hope you have a wonderful summer. Donna

contents: Re: What To Do??? Thank you. I know I need to do these things. As far as the food, I am sure I will be able to figure it out. Mostly I will just need to bring my own stuff. It's just another aspect of that whole "year of firsts" thing. And another reminder of how my life has changed. I have found your ability to enjoy the social aspects of eating over the food aspects admirable. I have never been overly social by nature, and my favorite part of socializing had always been the food. So that is another change I have been working on. Just got an email today that my first event is now open for registration!...

Re: What To Do??? SDM, I am in the same boat. I do several 5ks and obstacle courses and I have the same concern. I've been flaring a lot this year and it is keeping me on the couch and out of the gym. I am afraid if I don't get it under control soon I won't be able to train and will not be in good enough shape to perform. I feel like I'm losing muscle tone. So I'm trying really hard to find different things to treat my symptoms and I'm struggling to stay positive. I signed up for the events anyway. I have one tomorrow in fact. I'm having a mild flare today but I will be darned if IC is going to make me miss out. So I'm scouring the forum today and hitting up the drugstore tonight. Good luck!
Re: What To Do??? How did it go? I am still on the fence about registering for anything yet (another unrelated conflict has come up re my first event).

Re: What To Do??? It went pretty well! I had some mild abdominal discomfort but I found that the excitement of the event helped to distract me from that. Since I have been inactive I found myself short of breath fairly easily but luckily I didn't have to run the whole time so it wasn't too bad. Were you able to resolve the conflict that was keeping you on the fence about registering?
Re: What To Do??? No resolution on the other issue. May need to do a later event...

Re: What To Do??? OK OK, I just signed up for my first event (I will just work around my other issue)!! Actually feeling a little confident! Did a short (cold) training swim yesterday (for Mother's Day I chose to go jump in a lake).

I found this on Amazon a few weeks ago and have used it about 10 times...not nearly enough, but enough to show some promising results. It's called "Easy Yoga for Easing Pain" with Peggy Cappy It's around 10.00 and if you're a prime member, there is no shipping cost. It's EASY...it's mostly stretching, stretching and more stretching...and some of it's done sitting in a chair. You can also use a chair for the poses/stretches you do standing up. It's nice to have the chair there for support if you have a bad knee, or are unsteady. I highly recommend it. If I hadn't had so much going on I would have done this religiously every day of the week. It has made a difference in the short amount of time that I have used it. Since most of us with IC also have fibro, this whole series of videos looks VERY promising for us. There is also a Jane Fonda video available to prime members on Amazon that I've found to be very helpful. It's real yoga, and broken down into a morning section and a bedtime section. I'll have to look up the name of it but you can find it by searching Jane Fonda Yoga Morning and Bedtime or similar key words. Take a look, let me know what you think.
signature: *IC-- Summer 2004; PFD--October 2005 *Fibro--Fall 2000; CFS-- Fall 2000 *MPS--Fall 2000; Crohn's disease-- 1997*IBS,GERD, *Migraines, hypothyroidism, GYN problems *Degenerative Disc Disease/scoliosis Total Abdominal Hysterectomy--adenomyosis--9\08 04/17/09 Crohn's disease almost killed me with a combo of extreme constipation from pain medications. My bowel ruptured, I almost died from peritonitis and spent several days in the ICU then more in a private room on the floor. If you have any questions about severe constipation from pain meds please don't hesitate to send me a message.

contents: I want to start exercising, and am not sure what would be good to start with. Physical therapy has helped so much that I am really encouraged to start a daily regimine. I am a bit hesitant though, as I am feeling so much better since I am not doing the heavy lifting and do not have the physical demands that my old job required. So, I would like to start out light. I am considering the balance ball, or maybe some yoga...not exactly sure. I would appreciate some advise, or some input on what has worked for any of you. I would be great to have some input before I just run out and by some exercise program that is to hard on me. Thanks in advance. Lisa

Walking is the best! After that, yoga. I do videos at home sometimes. You can get yoga videos very inexpensively through amazon.com It's a good idea to scroll down & read the comments other customers have left about the videos. Better yet, if you have netflix or another place to rent videos, rent the videos that sound good & try them out that way first.

contents: Thank you Kadi, I will check out amazon. What a great idea, I hadn't even thought of that. I do quite a bit of walking at work, but probably not enough. And, when I start my new position, I will not be walking much at all. Thanks again, Lisa

Since your new, I would second walking. Pedometers are fun and cheap. They show you steps walked, calories burned and other fun extras. Its great becuase once you get a baseline for how much you walk on average, try adding one hundred steps a day. Seeing your progress will keep you motivated! Erika

contents: I agree. Walking is a low impact exercise that burns calories, yet doesn't put extra strain on the abdomen. Donna

contents: I am also new to IC and wanting to exercise more. However, walking for me alot sometimes flares me. Any other types of exercise? Would you suggest taking a yoga class to learn yoga first or just rent/buy yoga videos to do yoga?
signature: IC Diagnosed: January 3, 2007 Pelvic Floor Dysfunction Vulvodynia Meds: Estrostep FE Urocit K Prelief (as needed) Urelle (as needed) Darvocet (as needed for pain) Singulair (for seasonal allergies affecting the IC) Cymbalta (for the pain) Have been on and off too many to list because of adverse reactions...

contents: I began yoga a year ago. I used to be the biking, weight lifting, running, power walking kind of exerciser. Then I couldn't do any of that! I thought yoga would be a joke when my PT suggested that. Not only is it not whimpy (you actually can break a sweat) but the breathing techniques and mental focus are great for pain control any time of the day. I have to be really careful if the class is heavy on twisting moves as that totally sets off my PFD. My instructor has lovingly reminded me over and over to listen to "my" body and do what it needs. I don't have to do exactly what is being demonstrated. I would suggest taking a beginners class so you can ask questions and learn the basic poses. Have fun!
Tai chi is also good. It is composed of a series of slow steady movements.
Re: What do you recommend for beginning exercises? For the people that did physical therapy.. How long did it take you to see a difference in symptoms... Please help????

I have been using Baquacil for many years and have had no problems with it and my IC. A critter got in the pool and destroyed my liner so I will be starting from scratch with the water. I have been told that Baquacil and Aquasilk are made by the same company and are really the same products, just that Aquasilk is much cheaper. Has anyone had any experiance with Aquasilk? Is it just as safe for an IC patient as Baquacil? I would hate to switch and find out that it just doesn't work for me. And I just love my pool-great for PT and mental health

Re: Baquacil or Aquasilk Originally posted by pskasper I have been using Baquacil for many years and have had no problems with it and my IC. A critter got in the pool and destroyed my liner so I will be starting from scratch with the water. And I just love my pool-great for PT and mental health I don't know about Aquasilk, but we used to use Baquacil in our pool --- until a generic became available --- ours is Revacil (much cheaper) and we can even interchange when we add to the pool. I suggest you compare the labels for the two just to be certain they're the same. That's what we did. Summer is coming --- Summer is coming --- Summer is coming --- Summer is coming --- YIPPEE!!! Donna

contents: Re: Baquacil or Aquasilk Thanks for the reply! I checked the labels and found that 50% of 2 of them were "other" ingredients. So I dug deeper on the internet and called the company which makes both. They were very helpful and they are in fact the very same products, same production line even. Just different labels. So I'm picking up the much cheaper chemical for the pool. We finally hit 60 here today. We were in the top 5 coldest and snowiest winters here. I still have a huge ice cube in the pool. Thanks for helping so quickly! Enjoy your day to the fullest!

I have been doing strength training at home with Jilian Michaels this week. I have also noticed an increase in my frequency and bladder sensitivity. I don't know if the movements are too hard on my bladder (especially the abdominal ones). I have been doing the cardio all low impact, but I'm still moving around a lot. I was thinking about trying yoga. I've only done it once many, many years ago. I remember downward dog causing me to get a headache, so I quit. But I am interested in trying it again because it's gentler on my bladder. Do any of you know of a good yoga dvd? I don't want to commit to a class because I never know how I'll feel that day. I'd rather do it at home alone. Thanks, Jane

Re: Questions about Yoga... No, we don't have cable, which stinks! We live out in the country and so we have Dish Network for tv. I ended up buying a yoga DVD online called "Hatha Yoga for IC". I'm looking forward to trying it!

contents: Re: Questions about Yoga... I take yoga every Monday and I try to do it wed and fri too. I don't always, but I try. I have found that the stretching really helps and my yoga instructor is always talking about pelvic floor muscles. I take ashtanga, which is very stretchy, and I like it. I also have a Gaim (sp?) yoga for beginners DVD that is very helpful, and you can find lots of how to videos online. Hope you like it!!

contents: Re: Questions about Yoga... i try to do yoga on a regular basis if i am able to. If you have an internet connection there are some great yoga sites that offer free videos. One of my favourites is yoga journal (I'd include the link but not sure if it's allowed). Also the Canadian Institute for the Relief of Pain and Disability has a great webinar on yoga and chronic pain.

Ok I have a few key points here, and as the title states, its mostly about keeping active during a flare. My challenge for the past year has been trying to lose weight and get to a point where I am healthy and happy and have a good self esteem again. I try to do at least 20 minutes of exercise every day, if not more...which is obviously a challenge with IC and you feel miserable. So I was hoping to maybe trade some ideas? Personally on days when I feel miserable and have a flare, all I want to do is curl up and sleep till I feel better. However, I also know its probably better for me in the long run to push past that feeling. Anyone else experience this? On days like that, I normally break out the weights and do arm exercises from a comfy position that doesn't cause the flare any additional irritation. Sometimes I find it more uncomfortable to be sitting so I will turn my treadmill on to slow and walk for 10-20 minutes, anything to get up and move. I also find that sometimes moving helps the flare (whether its because im just distracted, or the endorphin or whatnot, i have no idea!) Anyone else ever notice that they feel fine UNTIL they begin to do a workout? Ive had moments where I will be doing a workout and suddenly it will trigger a flare. What specific workouts were they? Ive found that too much jogging tends to do this for me.

Re: Keeping active while having a flare? Activity doesn't usually make me feel worse. When I feel like crap, trying to something is generally a good idea. I agree that slow walking is good. I don't know what your weather is like currently, but if it isn't too bad, I would lose the treadmill and go outside. Running doesn't usually bother me (even though I have to be careful about over doing it). I feel like my momentum is more forward than up and down. Do you like to swim?

Re: Keeping active while having a flare? I find walking at a leisurely pace works best for me. I do like my treadmill, but my back has kept me away from it for a while now. I'm hoping to get back to it soon. Before the back problem, I could plug in a DVD and just walk on the treadmill while I watched an episode of "As Time Goes By" --- my very favorite Britcom. Donna

contents: Re: Keeping active while having a flare? Hi sdm I can't really lose my treadmill. About 10 months of the year where I live is cold and rainy. Plus I live out in the middle of nowhere, so if I wanted to go for a walk, I would need to drive into town a ways. Don't get me wrong, I LOVE walking outside when given the opportunity, and the few times the weather is actually nice, I take advantage and I try to go outside. Thank you for the suggestion though.

Re: Keeping active while having a flare? Hi Donna, I like my treadmill too And I agree about walking at a leisurely pace. I personally choose to lose myself in music when I'm on the treadmill. I love turning on my favorite station of plugging into Pandora. Thanks for the response.

Re: Keeping active while having a flare? I've been taking yoga, and I find that the stretching really helps me in flares. I guess that makes sense, because my yoga instructor talks about the pelvic floor a lot.

contents: Re: Keeping active while having a flare? That does make a lot of sense! I took yoga through my college for awhile and I felt quite better while doing so. Perhaps I will start doing yoga at home a few days a week again.

Re: Keeping active while having a flare? I have always been an active person who likes working out. But since my last flare, I am scared to start any exercise program. I have noticed that walking, especially outside, makes me feel better almost every time. I love Jilian Michaels workout dvds, so i was thinking about starting them again. Except I will skip the cardio part and just walk in place. Jumping around bothers my bladder. I also think just working out with weights is a great idea.
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Re: Keeping active while having a flare? Jumping around too much hurts my bladder too...too much fast paced cardio in general, like heavy running will trigger flares...so I agree I think light walking, or walking in place is a great idea.

Re: Keeping active while having a flare? A lot of what you can or cannot do depends on whether you have PFD along with the IC. I have PFD and must be careful not do anything that tightens my abdominal/pelvic floor muscles because they are already in knots. Stretching and walking are the safest or anything low impact. I have an elliptical trainer. My PFD therapist said that was fine but only on the lowest setting. I think getting outside and walking is not only good for the body, but it's good for the soul. When we're in pain all the time it's easy to get trapped inside...not particularly wanting to get involved in anything or with anyone. We need to give those endorphins a chance to kick in on a regular basis. I can't wait for the piles of snow that have accumulated where I live to melt. I feel like I live in Antarctica.

Ativan, occasionally for spasms Supplements: DH Aloe Vera capsules, 3 per day for maintenance and 6-12 per day during flares Following Strict IC Diet

contents: Re: Keeping active while having a flare? Originally posted by LoriSue A lot of what you can or cannot do depends on whether you have PFD along with the IC. I have PFD and must be careful not do anything that tightens my abdominal/pelvic floor muscles because they are already in knots. Stretching and walking are the safest or anything low impact. I have an elliptical trainer. My PFD therapist said that was fine but only on the lowest setting. I think getting outside and walking is not only good for the body, but it's good for the soul. When we're in pain all the time it's easy to get trapped inside...not particularly wanting to get involved in anything or with anyone. We need to give those endorphins a chance to kick in on a regular basis. I can't wait for the piles of snow that have accumulated where I live to melt. I feel like I live in Antarctica. You and I must both live in Antarctica! I live in Michigan and this is the snowiest, coldest winter I can remember! We just got 4 more inches last night...sigh. I am really looking forward to spring so I can walk outside again. I very much agree with you about the endorphins. Very important to feeling better. I tried doing my Jilian Michaels DVD this morning. I did all the cardio low impact with no jumping. I did all the weights, strength training and abdominal. Boy am I out of shape. I can tell I haven't done anything but sit for 3 months.
signature: Jane Diagnosed 1/7/14 via cysto/hydro Current Treatment: 10 mg amitriptyline, DH Aloe Vera Used During Flares: Uribel, extra DH Aloe Vera, Klonopin and Tramadol (as needed) Practicing bladder training and stress control (hot baths, pelvic stretching) Recently tried PT, do not appear to have PFD Things that have not helped in the past: Oxybutynin, Vesicare, Pyridium, Myrbetriq, Vagifem Also have migraines and IBS

contents: Re: Keeping active while having a flare? I agree, I miss walking outside as well. I live in such a pretty part of the country with views of Mt. St. Helens, and Mt. Hood, evergreens, and rivers and lakes, I love driving out to hiking grounds and taking it all in during the summertime! Our snow finally melted just the other day, but we've been having crazy wind storms, as well as constant cold rain. But I believe a breath of fresh air now and again is good for a person, I can't wait for spring to arrive either!

I got an email today from my running magazine concerning the usual "goals for the New Year." Most years I'm anxious to get going on new exciting adventures for the New Year. However, this past year really sucked, and it was all I could do get back on my feet. So a reminder of setting new goals hit me kinda hard. I feel like I went backwards instead. How do I set goals when I am actually behind where I usually am? I suppose I should give myself credit for getting back on my feet at all with all this adversity. I began walking, then slowly incorporated more running while increasing the time. I am back to the same amount of time I used to go, with mostly running. But, there is still a fair amount of walking, and I am probably running a little slower than I used to (if that's possible). So the total distance in reduced. Not sure where to go from here.

Re: A Little Bummed I set little goals like "stay positive" or "try new things". Things I know I can do. For instance, I decided that 2014 was all about trying to stay positive. I'm also going to try one new things each month. January is yoga. Haven't decided what to do for Feb, but I don't have to worry about that. I'm also going to start tackling my try it list of foods, and I'm going to take it slow. I'm not in any hurry. I'm also giving up processed foods 100% this year. I do a pretty good job of it, but I sneak sometimes. Maybe set a goal like "walk one mile a day" and then if you feel better one week then run. We can only do what we can do, and it took me a long time to be ok with that.

No longer need them, so I only take them during flares.

contents: Re: A Little Bummed How about a goal of progressing within your physical limitations --- I was never able to actually run --- it's too much jarring in my joints. I can walk, but not long distances. My goal is to take a daily walk. Sending encouraging hugs, Donna

contents: Re: A Little Bummed Goals..New Year.. I see how this is hitting us. I decided not to get myself caught in the too many wrong resolutions thing. I got up and made myself go to the gym. I told myself if you walk on the treadmill for 30 minutes thats good and then I stayed longer and used some new equipment. I hope that helped especially ICNDonna she supports me. My plate is overflowing with chronic health needs. Exercise will support me when done consistently. Only 4 more months to warmer temperatures!

Re: A Little Bummed Thanks everyone. You have given me plenty of food for thought. Speaking of food. Tackling the try it list sounds brave. I find it discouraging. It seems whenever I try something, I tend to feel a little worse (not a full blown flare-exept polish sausage and caffeinated low acid coffee), sometimes starting later the next day (not sure what the time frame should be), sometimes lingering longer (shouldn't it be out of my system by now?). Since symptoms tend to vary anyway, I end up not knowing if the food was the problem or not. So I go back to bladder friendly only. This is one reason I am glad the Christmas season is about over. Back to fitness goals...Thinking about doing a very cold open ocean swim in the next few weekends. Wondering about a half marathon in March. Maybe I can create a "post IC" personal record. Then I won't feel so bad about how pathetic my time will be. I am also afraid of getting worse. I can almost handle where I am now. But if I make plans...

Re: A Little Bummed Yay, I did my open water ocean swim this weekend! And I felt better than good afterwards! Seems cold water and endogenous morphine is pretty good for the bladder. Then again, open water swimming almost always cures what ails me (even temporarily).

Re: A Little Bummed that's awesome! I started yoga this month, and I've been to 2 classes and have started doing it at home. The stretching seems to be helping me. I also find it really relaxing (although it's so hard!!) Someday I will be the flexible noodle that my instructor is.

Re: A Little Bummed I think its great that you're back to running! I have always exercised with walking and my Jilian Michaels DVDs, but since this hit me last October, I haven't done anything! It was all I could do get off the couch! But I have been feeling better lately and contemplating starting a new exercise routine. I thought I would do my DVDs which is strength training, abdominal, and cardio circuits. I think Ill do all the cardio low impact for a while because I'm afraid to jump around. Walking always makes me feel better. I agree, the endorphins help our bladders!

I'm going to stick with the yoga (I really like it although my instructor is a noodle who can just bend and do whatever on a whim). I'm going to crochet in Feb. I think it will help me clear my head after school and give me something to do to relax while I work on this big publishing deadline that I've got looming overhead. Something mindless that I can do at night. I've also started reading one new book for fun every month.

contents: Re: Using kettlebells while experiencing bad IC symptoms? My physical therapist waited a year before having me touch a weight. It was all stretching and stabilizing exercises. Even now, I don't use heavy weights - i'm that weird dude in the gym lifting 10 lbs I think the biggest challenge with kettleballs and other weights is that the body will naturally engage your pelvic floor and your core (abdominal, etc.) to help lift and stabilize but if those muscles aren't working well or are painfully tense - the entire weight-lifting exercise could make things worse. However, it's weird you've been using kettleballs for three months without issues until now. Maybe its all coincidental and your recent flare isn't related to your exercise regime at all??
signature: Please see some of my forum postings about my experience My positive experience at the Wise Anderson Weekend Interesting Reads out on the Internet: A Case Study by Harvard of Pelvic Floor Pain in the Boston-area (nope this definitely isn't me but I've met some of the practitioners in my travels)

contents: I had horrible bladder issues all summer long after a uti that began in May. After the infection was no longer, my pain, burning, and frequency continued. After months of dilatations, a bunch of different meds & a cysto, I threw my hands up and began doing yoga again. It hurt like hell, but I pushed through it. And for 4 months I was just about symptom free. My bladder would spaz a bit before my period or when I ovulated, but nothing reallly bad. I got off all the meds & was doing so well But, leave it to me to think I was better & then began slacking, as in not doing the yoga program every night Over two weeks ago I had all the symptoms return, but with a uti again. Went through 3 different antibiotics & finally after no infection, my bladder is just going crazy...oh, the pain I know yoga isn't a cure for IC, but I honestly do believe in it's benefits...but stick with it!! Even though this is one of my bad nights, I'm rolling out the yoga mat right now....NAMASTE Alisha xoxo

Uh, yeah, 10 minutes of a more advanced yoga practice can be quite challenging & turned out to be over my limit. So, back to my Rodney Yee beginner dvd or my Lilias Folan vhs tape tomorrow

signature: [SIZE="1"][B]Be well, Alyssa :hi:[/B] [

contents: Girls: I just found a 3-dvd set of beginners yoga (Rodney Yee & Suzanne Deason). Totally forgot I bought this months ago at Target....oops. One is for Upper Body, One for Abs and One for Lower Body. All are beginner so its just perfect for me...I'm excited to hear that those who have tried it have had good outcomes.... Kadi is this the set you have? Do you recc something else that you like? I just wanted to try something different...I already go 2x/week to the gym and meet the trainer to kick my butt....on off days I do cardio on my own (or try to). I figured I'd try it on days that are to crappy to drive to the gym--or I want to do something but maybe a different pace, this might be a good fit for me. Born to Swim: I'm thinkin' Yoga might be a good thing for you...if you do some reading up on yoga and similar disciplines....sometimes just really focusing on the breathing aspects (forget the physical poses for awhile) can really do wonders. I know I asked one of Jill's guests here on an ICN chat reagrding this....and she confirmed that focusing on the breathing can really be helpful (especically those of us who are struggling with excercise--I've been there too...Just walking was making me miserable with my IC) But getting more oxygen in your body helps in all sorts of ways...If you do try it....let us know how you make out, k? y.

contents: I also love yoga, the one I don't (well I haven't for a few weeks) but when I can is Kundaliuni yoga. I got into it by reading Gurmukh's book "The 8 Human Talents", I highly recomend it. I found a Kundalini yoga class in town that I go sometimes, and Gurmukh's DVD on Kundalnini yoga which I do sometimes too. What I like about it is that it is very spiritual, focusses on breathing, repetitions, and you don't have to do the excersices you can't. I don't do the ones not recomened for pregnant women or the ones that comon sense juts tells me that is putting pressure on my bladder. There's another Kundalini Yoga DVD with Maya, I haven't looked into it yet but planning to buy it. Today I may do a meditation session....

contents: Re: Yoga!! yogaglo.com is a great place to try different types of yoga and you can specify time limit, difficulty etc. Free trial for 15 days and $18 a month. I am in my free trial and loving it! Yoga classes are $12 a pop around here and although I try to do that once a week simply for the "pushing myself" more and social aspect of a class, these yogaglo classes are pretty nice! I'm not very good about doing yoga at home but 3x this week...not bad

My drs are all urging me to lose weight. I have not done much more than light walking in flat neighborhoods but it's not burning enough calories. I have stuck with it because it's the only "safe" exercise I have been able to do. It helps my pelvic and back muscles feel better but I'm not burning enough calories. So today I used my treadmill and did some intervals with the incline and went no higher than 3 mph. As soon as I was done I felt my bladder burning, and a frequency feeling even though I had just gone. I got dressed and ran out on errands and had the twisting/pulling in my urethra again too. I know it was not diet related because I got up this morning and drank water only and my usual bowl of cereal. Is anyone else extremely frustrated with exercise flaring them? My DH bless his heart tries to understand but he just doesn't get it. His response was that I needed to just keep at it and that my muscles would adjust. I disagree with him but maybe someone here can chime in with experience? Does your bladder ever adjust? In my experience with Zumba, water aerobics, some yoga poses, any sort of squat or ab work.. all these things flare me so bad. And I used to be a religious hard core exercise. My bladder intolerance to exercise came on literally overnight back when my IC reared it's ugly head for good. It doesn't feel like it's something my body just needs to strengthen itself against because it feels like it's injury to the bladder versus just the muscles around the bladder?
signature: The combo of things that brought me out of remission were artificial sweetners, liquid vitamins with citric acid, tight pants, lower body workouts and stopping all my allergy meds cold turkey during height of allergy season. I wish I knew back then what I know now about IC First IC symptoms Spring of 2000; In-office cystoscopy 2002 -negative; Remission until 2007 (yeah!!); Negative PST spring of 2007; IC diagnosed via Cystoscopy and Hydrodistention 7/24/07; Another In-office cystoscopy 4/9/10 with biopsy showed inflammation Treatments for my IC/PFD: Elmiron 400 mg per day; walking, Zyrtec, Claritin, Singulair, Neurontin 100 mg per day; Ice packs and laying down; About to try Cytotec Tried and Failed: Heat (always makes me worse!), Heparin instills -too painful for my urethra; Atarax (interfered with my quality of sleep first time, second time dried my bladder out; Elavil makes me sick, lidocaine patches (just plain don't work), Cystoprotek (only tried a couple months but didn't seem to do much), Aloe Vera caps, pelvic floor therapy, trigger point work flared me worse, Lyrica (bad stomach pain) Other Diagnoses: PFD 2009; Fibromyalgia 2006, Osteopenia 2012, GERD 2006, Gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying) Anxiety: Other medications: Ambien, Dexilant, Ibuprofren, Necon 1/35, Voltaren gel for tailbone BABY GIRL 10/28/08 *** BABY BOY 7/8/11

contents: I have a problem with exercise and flares. My physical therapist thinks that my exercise regimen before I was diagnosed might have led to the "big" flare. I have pelvic floor dysfunction and any exercise that tightens my abdominal and pelvic floor muscles is a no-no. I've been told to only walk and on flat surfaces (inside at the mall or the local school track). It's tough to lose weight without exercise, unless you enjoy eating next to nothing! Initially, after I was diagnosed I lost 10 pounds but that was while I was in mega pain and afraid to eat anything. I quickly gained it back (plus 5 for good measure). I was diagnosed only 4 months ago and I want to give my bladder and my pelvic floor a good chance to get better before I begin the exercise again.
signature: ************************************************************** Experienced first symptoms in 2007 Diagnosed with IC 6/13 Diagnosed with PFD (Pelvic Floor Dysfunction) 8/13 Medications: .5 mg. Ativan, occasionally for spasms Supplements: DH Aloe Vera capsules, 3 per day for maintenance and 6-12 per day during flares Following Strict IC Diet

contents: Fifteen minutes on an incline at 3 MPH is a lot if you're not used to it. You might want to start out with a flat setting and a little slower. When I got my treadmill I started with 5 minutes, then gradually increased to 45 minutes --- then I had back surgery and complications and I still can't do the treadmill for more than a few minutes. I hope eventually to get back to the 45 minute routine. You may need to reduce calorie intake to be able to lose. --- not easy, I know. Warm hugs, Donna

contents: Hi Charisse, I've just restarted exercise after a year of being sedentary. I found that I can do 10 minutes of treadmill, no incline, 2.5-3.0 right now, much more than that & my bladder complains. So, my routine at the gym right now is flat treadmill 10-12 minutes, armcycle on easy setting for 1 song - usually about 3-4 minutes, and then exercises my physical therapist gave me (stretches). If you don't have a physical therapist, I really like "Heal Pelvic Pain" by Amy Stein for a good home program. It's only about $16 here in the ICN shop or a little less on amazon. My urologist (IC researcher) told me not to do any exercises that strain the abdomen directly = no crunches, no weight machines for abs/adductors/abductors. He advised walking, some elliptical machines (the ones that have some electrical help are ok, ones that are powered just by me - forget it), rowing machine and then the PT stretches. I've been through this before though, and I know that if I continue to regularly do the small amount of exercise, then I can gradually increase it without a problem over time. It's just hard to be patient & I have to be careful not to compare what I'm doing now with what I used to do before. My counselor calls it "self compassion". I call it, "not yelling at myself". I'm also battling weight issues and am rather worried. I'm 5'4" and 186 pounds (down 3 from 2 weeks ago--yay!). I'm on Elavil and while that medicine really helps my IC, it also really increases my appetite especially for sweets. I'm going to have to be super careful now because my doctor and I just met on Friday and my IC has gotten worse, so I'm going to need to increase my Elavil dosage from 50 to 80mg. Starting with 60mg this week, then increasing by 10mg per week until at 80mg for 2 weeks, then to see the dr. again. I'm keeping a food diary, symptom diary, and medicine diary. Trying to keep myself honest about what I eat, and clear about what's happening with my IC and meds. Not fun. Hate it. Angry. Feel like I've got a "new normal" and it's worse than my last "new normal". I really recommend keeping a food diary, writing down everything you eat, and keeping anything tempting out of the house. There are just some foods I really can't control once I start on them. But fortunately, I'm usually too lazy to go out & get them! Hang in there! IC is a really crappy disease, but at least here on the forums, we know we're not alone!
signature: Kadi ------------------------------------------------------------- I am not a medical authority nor do I offer medical advice. In all cases, I strongly encourage you to discuss your medical treatment with your personal medical care provider. Only they can, and should, give medical recommendations to you. ------------------------------------------------------ New favorite quote: "God gives us only what we can handle. Apparently God thinks I'm a bad-ass" ~Author Unknown Source - Pinterest " Current treatments: -IC diet -Elavil 50mg at night -Continuous use birth control pills (4-5 periods/year) -Heparin/Marcaine/Sodium Bicarb home instills at night 3-4x per week, more often if needed -Pyridium if needed, -Pain medicine at bedtime daily, as needed during the day several times per week -Antibiotic when doing an instillation to prevent UTI -Colace & SmartFiber to treat chronic constipation from meds, Fleet enema as needed -Dye Free Benadryl 50 mg at bedtime -"Your Pace Yoga: Relieving Pelvic Pain" dvd, walking, treadmill at gym -Managing stress= VERY important! -Fur therapy: Hugging the cat!

contents: Thank you everyone for the kind and helpful responses. Lori I was actually in PT for fibromyalgia and doing a lot of "ab" work on machines right when my IC started.. maybe not a coincidence. My experience has been that exercise gives me a temporary high and then makes me feel worse because I don't know how to "under-do" it lol. As soon as I start exercising, the endorphins kick in and I just want to keep going.. it's like a drug! If only it was my just being lazy. I'm actually feeling fortunate that I've been able to keep up with my housework better this past year.. that was a major struggle for a long time but I had to learn I can only vacuum one room every couple of days.. no more vacuuming the whole house at once. I lost 8 lbs in June when I cut my calories down to 1000 -1200 per day but then I flared and I just can't diet and flare at the same time. I'm stable now so I need to start back up with counting my calories again. I will take everyday as a blessing that I am able to walk.. I remind myself not everyone has that ability! Donna my daughter just started preschool and I walk her to and from every morning. I have no problems walking outside but something about my treadmill does not agree with me. But I need to figure it out because it's getting too cold to walk outside and my allergies get so bad somedays that I can't be outdoors that long. It is frustrating though for sure and I know you understand this. Kadi I'm about the same weight right now.. I'm having to shop in the Plus sized section for the first time in my life, as if finding the right clothes for IC isn't hard enough! It is very depressing. I can't say my IC is any worse now that I'm heavier although I know that it's not healthy for me anyway.
signature: The combo of things that brought me out of remission were artificial sweetners, liquid vitamins with citric acid, tight pants, lower body workouts and stopping all my allergy meds cold turkey during height of allergy season. I wish I knew back then what I know now about IC First IC symptoms Spring of 2000; In-office cystoscopy 2002 -negative; Remission until 2007 (yeah!!); Negative PST spring of 2007; IC diagnosed via Cystoscopy and Hydrodistention 7/24/07; Another In-office cystoscopy 4/9/10 with biopsy showed inflammation Treatments for my IC/PFD: Elmiron 400 mg per day; walking, Zyrtec, Claritin, Singulair, Neurontin 100 mg per day; Ice packs and laying down; About to try Cytotec Tried and Failed: Heat (always makes me worse!), Heparin instills -too painful for my urethra; Atarax (interfered with my quality of sleep first time, second time dried my bladder out; Elavil makes me sick, lidocaine patches (just plain don't work), Cystoprotek (only tried a couple months but didn't seem to do much), Aloe Vera caps, pelvic floor therapy, trigger point work flared me worse, Lyrica (bad stomach pain) Other Diagnoses: PFD 2009; Fibromyalgia 2006, Osteopenia 2012, GERD 2006, Gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying) Anxiety: Other medications: Ambien, Dexilant, Ibuprofren, Necon 1/35, Voltaren gel for tailbone BABY GIRL 10/28/08 *** BABY BOY 7/8/11

contents: I went for a brief walk around dinner time and really wanted to nap since I didn't sleep as long as needed last night. But as usual, arrived home, started my new crock-pot recipe and did the dishes. I have read that whatever is ailing a person, exercise is usually the answer. So hoorayfor me and developing a consistent exercise habit. I hope to instill this habit before the cold weather arrives but I can still go to the gym!!
Good for you! Before I retired, I did a daily walk at lunch time --- I worked in a hospital and it was a huge building so I had a regular route to walk indoors when the weather got bad. Donna

contents: There seems to be so little activity on this section of the forums, and most references to exercise in the more general sections are negative, that I am getting curious about something. Does anyone with this condition actually exercise regularly? And also, how many were already active prior to getting this or during remissions? I am wondering about the possibility that lack of activity, in addition to all the other health problems associated with an inactive lifestyle, may have contributed. Or if being active contributed. It probably has nothing to do with it, but I am wondering if there is any correlation with activity levels and types. It is easy to imagine someone who has never been active developing this condition, then getting told to go out and exercise...Yeah right. Is that where the negative vibe is coming from? What is hard for me is that I had always been so active (20 year remission), and it is really important for me to continue as much as I can. It is necessary for healing physically and emotionally. It is frustrating when I hear all the things everyone can't do. Am I the "inspiring" one who does it anyway? Or am I just in denial and haven't accepted that my life is over, and I shouldn't be trying to do any of this? Sorry. Thanks.

I was very active up to my back surgery in November 2011 --- and I'm gradually getting back to some activities. Now that it's summer, I have been spending almost daily time in our pool. I can't do much actual swimming, but I can walk in the pool and that's helping. I'm just happy that I'm doing better than last year when I spent most of my time in bed. Yes, I was very active before my IC. Back in high school (at least a century ago ) I skated (ice and roller), rode a bicycle, hiked, swam, etc., etc. --- I didn't really slow down and pretty much did the same things with my children while they were growing up --- and continued with grandchildren. I absolutely feel better when I exercise. After I swim in the evening, I relax and sleep very well. It's not easy to begin an exercise program, but I honestly think it's a good idea. Before my back surgery I spent 45 minutes on my treadmill at medium speed every day; now I can barely make it to 10, but I will get there. Sending encouraging thoughts, Donna

contents: So most of your pain is from your back rather than bladder? Same with activity modifications? Sounds like you have been through alot. You must be pretty tough. I thought I was, but I have finally met my match. Thanks for all your help.

I have had to modify a lot of what I do. I do a LOT of walking now since I cannot ride a bike or swim in pools that have chlorine. I also do a routine of stretches and movements like modified push ups etc. My big issue lately is a heart condition they discovered in 2011, so I cannot do anything too strenuous. I try to get outdoors to do ANYTHING in the summer as much as possible. :-D

contents: Originally posted by sdm So most of your pain is from your back rather than bladder? Same with activity modifications? Sounds like you have been through alot. You must be pretty tough. I thought I was, but I have finally met my match. Thanks for all your help. Right now most of the pain is from the back, but some of the activity modifications took place long before the back problems. I found that I needed to stick with what I call "smooth" exercises, such as walking and swimming. With diet, sticking with exercise, and hydrodistentions when it gets awful, my IC is pretty well controlled. I think what has helped me most is that when I feel good, I allow myself to enjoy the day. I pretty well know my exercise limitations and try to stick within them. If you're able to exercise without paying for it later, I encourage you to go for it. There are people posting here in all levels of pain --- and for every individual posting here because they are in pain, there are literally thousands out there with interstitial cystitis who aren't posting because they feel good and are busy getting on with their lives. Twenty years in remission --- that's so great! I hope you'll get back there quickly. Warm hugs, Donna

contents: I was active prior to getting IC. Now, I am usually in too much pain to exercise. There are times I feel okay, but I've only gotten in a few workouts all year long! I am always on my feet though when I am home. I dislike having a desk job. I'd rather move around more but I hurt so much of the time, there have been times when I feel sitting is all I can do, if that. I don't feel like I'm well enough to work full-time but I keep pushing myself to do it.

I'm glad you posted this. When i first got IC it was so out of control from a year of misdiagnosis i had to take narcotics for the incapacitating pain-eventually had to stop work, ended up basically confined to my recliner. I had to go through detox off all narcotics which was hell, but in the end has saved my life because now i can be active again. I life weights-heavy weights, work hard-to develop my chest, back, shoulders particularly-since my tight pelvic muscles pull me forward and down, i want to get strength to life up and back to have normal posture-i stretch my hip flexor muscles because tight hip flexors cause the belly protrusion and also pull me downward. Also muscle weakness in the upper body causes general sinking onto the pelvic floor and aggravates the dysfunction there. I work my abs but avoid a lot of crunches-i am careful of my lower body and am more conservative with working my legs but i do use nautilus machines-i have bad hip spasms especially on the right so i work at counteracting those. I walk and swim-i swim in a chlorinated pool but it is a private one so not so much chlorine, also in a pond. i work hard enough to get results but can't get too strenuous-i can't run at all! biking out of the question. the exercise did hurt at first but i persisted and now i think it helps my pain-it also lifts my spirits, makes me feel good about myself and has helped me to lose 60 pounds i gained on the narcotics! also my posture is much better and getting blood flowing to my pelvic muscles helps them. I was active before the diagnosis for which i am grateful, but when i was first diagnosed and in the year afterward til i quit work i got so bad i couldn't even walk a block. also narcotics make exercise difficult because they suppress the good endorphin feelings. i use baclofen and hydroxyzine for my pain-stopped neurontin even though it helped because of the weight gain. I still have fatigue, i still can't do a lot in the course of a day, i still can't do housework, sitting, standing without pelvic spasms. but i can do more than when i just laid in a chair! and i look forward to my exercise-especially summer when i swim. i am determined to snowshoe this winter! i am nowhere near the way i was activity wise before ic, but i am much better than 5 years ago. even sometimes when i think it does make my pelvic muscles a little sore, i still think it is worth it

This post seems like perfect timing. I recently started exercising again after being diagnosed about 8 months ago. I felt too uncomfortable to exercise in the beginning, just didn't feel like doing much. Before IC, I was extremely active. Lots of walks, Zumba, Jillian Michaels DVDs, etc. I've been feeling great the past few months...until recently. I recently started doing the harder workout again and sure enough, some of my worse symptoms are back. Sorry if TMI, but oh well--I get a feeling like my clitoris is being pinched. It's extremely uncomfortable. It almost feels like the discomforts extends to my belly button. Why the heck is this? I have tight pelvic floor muscles and I'm in physical therapy now but haven't asked this question yet since I haven't had this symptom for quite a while. I do not want to discourage anyone. I started back at Zumba almost a month ago and I felt great for awhile!! I don't know if my symptoms are tied to exercise or not. I almost have to think they are considering I've been doing much more abs and harder workouts. Has anyone else had this experience? thanks, Kristen Walking has always helped me...just didn't do it much in the winter. I don't think walking causes me to flare at all. I haven't gotten back on a bike yet, I'm scared to flare!

I swim (deep water aerobics) 3x per week and walk for an hour on other days. I stopped swimming for awhile when one uro said it could hurt me. Started back when my new urogyn said to do it. I don't stay more than an hour in the chlorine and wash with warm water immediately when I get out. It keeps me sane.
I was biking a few miles a day on a recumbant bike, and I was up to 4 miles. That lasted about a week. It put me into one of the worst flares that I have ever been in, so now I am not doing anything.
signature: ---------------------------------------------------- My blog on IC, adoption, and academia: the garlic whisperer Diagnosed Jan 2013. Currently taking: 200mg Elmiron morning and night 25mg Elavil at night (no drowsiness or weight gain) culturelle in the morning SLIT drops for allergies (environmental) 3 drops/night under the tongue zyrtec 24 hour nightly (recommended by uro) Instillations 3x/week: Parson's Solution uribel and prelief as needed Also took Desert Harvest Aloe (3 in the morning and 3 at night) for a year and had great luck. No longer need them, so I only take them during flares.

contents: Originally posted by miniguinea73 I was biking a few miles a day on a recumbant bike, and I was up to 4 miles. That lasted about a week. It put me into one of the worst flares that I have ever been in, so now I am not doing anything. I tried riding a stationary bike as part of a weight loss program (after a detox wrap) and I am hurting today. Can't really put anything in that area with pressure, but I can walk or swim. I love really cold water. It is like having my ice pack down there. Sorry about your episode.

I am much more active now in some ways, than before (I have VV, but have found it to respond much like IC). I do yoga, workout dvd's, beginning weightlifting & lots of walking. Running wasn't a good idea for me - running at the playground is fine, long distances are not. I used to practically live in the pool, but due to chlorine, need to greatly limit my time. I would love to try a salt water pool!! I used to mountain bike all the time, but for obvious reasons that is out. However, I am checking into horn free bike seats at the moment. I don't want this crap to steal more from me than absolutely necessary!!
signature: Symptoms began October 2011, VV diagnosis July 2012 Have experienced some relief with the IC Diet as well as avoiding sugar, gluten & dairy. Still trying to nail down my trigger foods! I believe diet plays a HUGE role in my VV. Current Treatments (besides diet): physical therapy & Lidocaine (as needed). Trying to beat this as naturally as possible!

contents: I haven't been diagnosed w. IC yet, but do have PFD for certain. I also seem to have clitoral pain sometimes and sometimes I have like a shooting pain that radiates to my belly button, which makes me think it is an irritated nerve. ???? It's all very debilitating.

I've had IC for about 30+ years, but have always been a very active athletic person since I was young. I play raquetball, jog, lift wts., Yoga, xc ski. I've had remissions lasting up to 4 years. Last flare was 1.5 years ago. I was up to running 9 miles (training for 1/2 marathon) before this flare came along.. but I too find that if I run, my bladder feels better after about 5-10 minutes - it's almost as if things become numb. Weird, have no explanation for it.. I also was totally in remission last summer and biked the Mickelson Trail in South Dakota - 110 miles in two days in 90 degree heat.. and nary a crotch wince?!! This syndrome is so weird. This flare I'm in now triggered by actual UTI.. sameo, sameo - take two courses of antibiotics and the infection tests negative, yet the symptoms remain. Also with flares, I get this mid to upper back pain that makes it hard to run etc. - almost everytime. Has anyone else had this? Again, no explanation or cause give from any health pratcitioners. I'm trying some mind-body things now - Dr. Richard Schubiner has an excellent book out with a DVD of relaxtion included in it. Just google his name. Even if you aren't into this kind of "stuff", it is very encouraging and helps a bit with the extreme anxiety that comes with flares. I say if you can keep exercising, do it, in whatever form works for you. Prayers and blessings to all out there.

First, I d not have an IC diagnosis. I have bladder sensitivity which increased when I entered Perimenopause. Secondly, consistent,exercise is the answer for many people. I too have increased my exercise. However, I still have not reached the goal of 30min five times a week. I am however, walking about 20min several times a week with an occasional visit to the gym for the use of weights and the elliptical machine. I am more focused on doing the exercise out of enjoyment and then necessity. I have plantar fasc. over two years in one foot making walking less desirable. I have found only two pairs of shoes to support my feet so that I can exercise consistently. I do not have a support system so I know I would exercise more if I had one. I will go for a walk soon to get my daily dose of sun and exercise.

I started doing yoga several times a week right before I was diagnosed - I had started noticing pelvic floor tightness and it was my way of trying to fix the problem on my own. I've been going to class about three times a week for a year now and it helps a lot. Three weeks ago I started lifting weights - I go to the gym three times a week and do a routine that takes about 40 minutes. I always feel so much energy and positivity right after my workout! I occasionally swim and do exercise videos too. I'm also a professional musical theatre performer, so I dance every day at work. Being active with IC is possible!

Need some advice I was actually training for a marathon when everything started. That was about 8 years ago. I know that running and biking are out of the question and swimming since I don't have a pool or belong to a gym. I actually gained 30 pounds due to inactivity. It was around 5 pounds a year. I have had times when I tried to start working out again, mainly walking and weights, and then I would go into a flare. I had a hydro in Feb. that left me pain free for months but I couldn't get over the mental barrier to try to exercise. I didn't want the "great" feeling to go away. I saw a nutritionist about 6 weeks ago to get help some with exercise and my diet to lose some weight. (Going to a family wedding in 5 more weeks and didn't want to still be bigger than my sisters!) I have lost about 12 pounds and started walking on the treadmill for 30 min/day at 3.2 pace, 5 days a week. I started walking in 10 min increments, morning, lunch time and late afternoon. I've increased it to two 15 min increments. I usually do both in the morning..with at least 15 min in-between. But I am starting to flare up. After I work out, my girlie parts feel swollen and bruised. It hurts to sit down. This eventually goes away throughout the day. I do it in the morning now so that by afternoon, I'm fairly comfortable...down there. But, I can feel stinging in my urethra. The only way to relieve it is to go to the bathroom. Sometimes it's every 10 min and only a few drops come out, but the pain is lessened for a few minutes. This too, decreases throughout the day, but doesn't go away. I'm not sure what to do. Part of me says to quit. The other part says, that if I'm going to hurt anyway, to continue and at least lose the weight and feel better about myself and the way I look. (I'm feel the stinging even on days I don't do anything. Almost missed going to the hockey game with my son on his birthday because of the pain.) I'm really tired of letting this rule my life and what I can and can't do. Thoughts?

When I first started on the treadmill, I could only do five minutes --- finally worked up to 45 minutes, but then had back surgery, followed by post surgery thoracic pain syndrome --- so back to square one again. Now that I'm nine months with my pain pump, I'm thinking I can start again on the treadmill, but only the 5 minutes at first. It's tough trying to get enough exercise. All I've done so far is walking and some swimming. Donna

contents: Hi I wanted to offer my thoughts to you. I certainly hear you and would encourage exercise to tolerance. My high school offers the pool to the public. Also, yoga seems to offer many benefits and certainly doesn't have to always be in one position. Also, you can lay on your back and exercise with legs in the air or leaning against the couch. They make video tapes, DVD exercise tapes, that can be purchased or rented from a local library. I am actively pursuing my goal of 30 minutes of daily exercise. I have yet to reach this goal BUT I am certainly exercising on a regular basis and that is key. Once it feels like a chore I am likely to stop or decrease my exercising. So I am determined to walk for 30 minutes while the weather is cooperating. Once the weather cools, I will seek a partner or go to the gym.
I found that orthotics helped when I had plantar fasc. I also get the concept of if you hurt anyway, might as well spend the time exercising.

I was just journaling that I walked 20 minutes after dinner and then realized I had also walked earlier in the day too! I plan on soaking up the autumn weather with its sunny but cool air and the splendor of the changing colors! Its a very good thing!

I listen to meditation podcasts, recording etc. while I walk. I walk very slowly, especially when my vulvar problems are bothering me. I actually am starting to enjoy it much more than running. I do about 2.5 miles daily now. I take the time to appreciate the moments for myself, and now if I don't walk, I feel weird. I'm trying to find other ways to stretch and do strength training. I'm thinking about looking up some Tai Chi on youtube.
signature: Not yet diagnosed - dr still eliminating other possibilities Worst pain of my life began August 6th, 2013. I also had terrible pain that went away after 6 weeks in Dec 2012 - Jan 2013 IC Diet and meditation have worked wonders - as soon as I stray the pain is immediately back Ibuprofen or acetaminophen Occasional Phenazopyridin AZO Benadryl and melatonin to sleep Prelief very occasionally, because it interferes with Synthroid effectiveness) Heating pad between legs or ice cold wash cloth

contents: I have a little DVD player and when I was able to spend a half hour or more on the treadmill, I would watch an episode of "As Time Goes By" --- my favorite British program. Donna

contents: I have not read all the posts here but am compelled to share my experience. I used to work out a lot! Kickboxing, weightlifting, step aerobics, boot camp, salsa, yoga, squats, abs etc. My last "real" workout was the day my IC flared up. 6 months prior I had been diagnosed with fibromyalgia. Although fibro is painful, my fibro pain is at it's best when I'm active and in shape. My IC pain is so much more intense though and I find ALL forms of exercise except walking cause me to tense up my pelvic muscles which is a natural reaction to stabilize your core when you are working out. Walking makes me feel a lot better but I really miss all my other forms of exercise if not for the calorie burn alone. IC is not an excuse not to exercise and I am extremely frustrated when a dr. tells me to exercise. It's true I'm now overweight and I'm sure that doesn't help my pelvic pain but the day this disease reared it's ugly head was a day when I was extremely fit (minus having fibro but I was still thin). I've had drs suggest I just need a qualified PT.. no offense but I was already working with a specialized fibro PT when I developed IC. I know my body and have years of experience with exercise. While not specifically trained myself, I used to go to the gym 7 days a week and have lived with personal trainers so I'm not totally clueless.
signature: The combo of things that brought me out of remission were artificial sweetners, liquid vitamins with citric acid, tight pants, lower body workouts and stopping all my allergy meds cold turkey during height of allergy season. I wish I knew back then what I know now about IC First IC symptoms Spring of 2000; In-office cystoscopy 2002 -negative; Remission until 2007 (yeah!!); Negative PST spring of 2007; IC diagnosed via Cystoscopy and Hydrodistention 7/24/07; Another In-office cystoscopy 4/9/10 with biopsy showed inflammation Treatments for my IC/PFD: Elmiron 400 mg per day; walking, Zyrtec, Claritin, Singulair, Neurontin 100 mg per day; Ice packs and laying down; About to try Cytotec Tried and Failed: Heat (always makes me worse!), Heparin instills -too painful for my urethra; Atarax (interfered with my quality of sleep first time, second time dried my bladder out; Elavil makes me sick, lidocaine patches (just plain don't work), Cystoprotek (only tried a couple months but didn't seem to do much), Aloe Vera caps, pelvic floor therapy, trigger point work flared me worse, Lyrica (bad stomach pain) Other Diagnoses: PFD 2009; Fibromyalgia 2006, Osteopenia 2012, GERD 2006, Gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying) Anxiety: Other medications: Ambien, Dexilant, Ibuprofren, Necon 1/35, Voltaren gel for tailbone BABY GIRL 10/28/08 *** BABY BOY 7/8/11

contents: Hi everyone, I have been on amitriptyline for quite some time now and ever since then I have had HUGE cravings for sweet things and my appetite has just gotten bigger. Today at the doctor I talked him into changing it and starting something new. I am already super embarrassed and self conscience about my body anyway because of the condition, but I have gained too much weight for my comfort. I was wondering if anyone had an effective exercises that do not cause flares or pain?? Thanks, Kylie
Walking is about my only exercise I can tolerate. I also like to swim, but can't do much in the pool due to back problems. I do a lot of pool walking. Donna

contents: I just walk as fast as I can (which isnt very fast) on a treadmill. Restorative yoga is very relaxing (and not hard or exhausting), I would recommend that as well

I was diagnosed with IC a couple of years ago. I have been being careful (for the most part) of what I eat. Recently, I started hiking a lot. I have noticed that my bladder leaks quite a bit, especially on the down hill. Is this normal? I am shocked that even if I just go, my bladder seems to be constantly leaking. Any advice? This really bums me out! Thanks!

If you haven't already, I suggest you let your urologist know about the leakage. I had stress incontinence pretty bad and my uro did a fairly simple surgery that got rid of that problem. I put it off for a long time, then after I had the surgery I wanted to kick myself for delaying getting attention for the problem. I still wear panty liners --- I absolutely can't stand even slight dampness in my clothing, but I don't really need them. Donna

contents: I was a pretty avid runner during my long remission. I am now having symptoms again, and I really don't want to give up running. Is there anyone who does run with IC? Running, itself, doesn't bother me. I actually feel better (endogenous morphine is great). But I often feel worse about a day and a half later. Symptoms are variable anyway, and it seems a little far removed, so I am not sure if this has anything to do with running or not. When an activity does cause a problem, when should this occur and how long should it last? Any suggestions on how to experiment.

I bought myself a treadmill but was deeply disappointed to find that it caused major flare ups, mainly frequency and urgency afterwards. I was informed it was because of the vibration going through my legs and pelvis, even just when walking fast rather than running. The movement of putting your weight heavily and continually down on the ground causes vibrations through your body. I was told I should stick to walking very gently instead but that was a bit rubbish cos going faster really boosted my endorphins and helped me keep fit and trim. I guess something like swimming would be better, but then what about the chlorine? It's very difficult to find an exercise that doesn't cause problems. I found even the 'row action exerciser' which works on the abdomen and biceps and core strength, causes problems cos of the tightening and clenching etc. in the end I had to give up. Jem x

About the only exercise I can tolerate is walking. I have a treadmill, but haven't been able to tolerate it since my back problem began. I can tolerate an exercycle. I tried again yesterday and made it for just under 5 minutes --- hardly worth it. I do plan to continue to try it every few days. When summer gets here, I will walk in our pool. We just have an above ground pool, but we use baquacil instead of clorine so I can paddle around some and it's easier to walk in the pool. I am really looking forward to summer. Donna

contents: Can anyone give me some recommendations on exercises that won't bother my ic? I need to lose about 30 pounds. I was thinking yoga but I'm worried about starting anything until I get some advice from people who have done it. Also I would love to hear yall's success stories with losing weight. It would really give me hope that I can do it too!
I can do smooth exercises, like walking or swimming --- nothing that jars my body. I never could jog or run without discomfort. In bad weather, my treadmill is an alternative. Donna
