contents: I walk lots. If you want to loose weight it helps to vary the speed, and go up and down hill. Be careful not to pound, and build up time and distance slowly
signature: My are with you all. May you all find a way to peace and joy in your lives.

contents: Couple ideas....I do yoga. Be careful as it has to be gentle, restorative yoga. Some of the yogas are very athletic with frequent pose changes and up/ down routines. Gentle yoga moves slower, with stretches but nothing extreme. I tried some other classes and ended up in pain, even though I thought I was modifying the poses.I have pain when I walk quickly or for a long period. So I only walk for 15 minutes at a time. At PT, I am using a recumbent bike. So the seat is like sitting in a chair. Seems to work well so far.

Hi, I love to ride my bicycle and used to clock approx. 100 miles a week however dealing with a flare us of IC for close to half a year has really taken all the joy out of it. I've been considering purchasing a bike saddle with no nose in the front to relive pressure on the pubic bone area but there are quite a few on the market I'm finding, all with mixed reviews. one could spend a lot of money trying to find one that would work for them so I'm wondering if anyone on here would have any advice or experience with trying to find a bike seat that will be comfortable. If I can't ride my bike I'm considering selling it and purchasing a recumbent but they sure do cost a lot! That or an elliptigo 8R, which would cost about the same as a recumbent. Decisions, decisions. Finding a comfortable seat would sure be a cheaper solution then replacing my ride!
signature: "You must master a new way to think before you can master a new way to be", Marianne Williamson.

contents: Can't speak to the bicycle riding except...I put myself into a huge flare just by walking one mile at a time, maybe 4 or 5 times a week. Doctor said it would be OK, but now in PT. PT says any jarring or pressure exercise is out, walking slow and build up over time (and that's a maybe), and she said probably never a 5 mile hike. She has me doing tons of stretches, gentle yoga, but only 10 minutes at a time on recumbent bike. Hoping to work up to more, such a bummer to be so limited. I hope you can find the right bike combo.

I ride a bicycle at work....I just use a standard seat (skinny seat) that comes on a Trek but put on a gel cover.....when I flare I just get off the bike....but the G9 seat (large and wide) seems to help I have heard.
signature: Diagnosed in 2005. Currently take Tramadol 50 mg, Amtryptiline 25 mg, and Hydroxizine 50mg at bedtime. Also take Pyridiium or Ultira-C on a rotating basis, and Gelnique as needed as well.

contents: Which seats have you been looking at? Im curious as i too am interested in finding a comfortable seat. The large seats dont offer proper positioning on my mountain bike, and the skinny/standard mountain bike seats, well, forget it!
signature: (\__/) (o.O ) (> < ) This is Bunny. He's on his way to world domination.

contents: Up date on my bike seat dilemma. I found a nose less bike seat on line and ordered it, still waiting for it to arrive so I can get it a try. There are actually a lot of nose less bike seats and I researched them thoroughly, read reviews, took another ride on my bike to determined where I'm most comfortable sitting on the seat and based on all of that I went with a seat that will be very different. It's called the spiderflex. You can check it on line but it's basically a rectangle that your sit bones rest on. The pubic bone area will come in contact with nothing but air. It has about a 10 to 15 degree tilt so that when you peddle the seat will rise and dip a little. Some reviews had folks loving that, others not so much and they figured out a way to stabilize it. Despite the dipping action most folks agreed that after purchasing an average of 6-9 other variations of nose less seats on the market this one solved their problem the best, so I'm excited and hopeful that it will be the answer to my issue also. If not I will purchase a recumbent bike, been researching those all week and if I fall in love with it (huge transition) I'll sell my upright. I shall keep y'all posted! Thanks for your feedback.
signature: "You must master a new way to think before you can master a new way to be", Marianne Williamson.

contents: I can't ride a bike ever. It hurts to bad for me.
I can take spin classes when I'm not in a flare. I hope your new seat will really help!

Due to both IC and BPH I can not ride a bicycle anymore - not the normal bicycle most of us are used to, but now I ride a recumbent bicycle and a recumbent trike. Recumbent bikes and trikes use seats that are somewhat like sitting a chair and in a position that does not result in pressure on the perineum area. Actually, recumbents are much more comfortable in general, eliminating the pressure on wrists, hands, shoulders, and neck that a standard bicycle causes. The biggest drawback of recumbents is their cost, considerably more than most bikes, but for me it has really been worth the extra cost. I have tried a no nose saddle (Easy Seat) and didn't like it. I'm sure there are better such seats, but the recumbent seating completely eliminates the problem we IC folks have with biking. I hope your new seat works for you because of cost, but if it doesn't you might look into recumbents so you won't have to give up bicycling.

thanks so much for your input. I've been studying the recumbents, so many brands and styles! Do you have a short wheel base or a long, what is the brand name of the one you ride? how many years have you been riding a recumbent and do you prefer the recumbent bike or trike better? I belong to a bike riding group, most of our members are age 45 plus... and many of them ride a recumbent, in fact I'd say it's up to about 75% riding the recumbents. Everyone says they are great for speed and comfort! A good friend of mine recently purchased a Rans rifle, it's her first recumbent. She's been a very serious bike rider for 30 some years ( unlike myself, I only average 60-100 miles most weeks) and so transitioning to the recumbent has been quite the adventure but she's had it for a month or so now and is getting the hang of it and loving it! I'd really appreciate any advice you might have in regards to what I might want to take into consideration when purchasing a recumbent. Still waiting for my nose less bike seat and will be interested to see how that goes.
signature: "You must master a new way to think before you can master a new way to be", Marianne Williamson.

contents: Hi mountainraine1, I'm much less of a bicyclist than you. I used to ride to work years ago, but now live 45 miles from workplace. A couple of years ago, I got started riding my standard bike again for exercise. It's really exercise where I live - all hills, but soon found that it hurt me in the groin area. At that time I thought I had prostate issues only. Later, when I was diagnosed with IC (I'm so much better now, with the IC diet, Elmiron, Atarax, I discovered recumbents. I purchased a Catrike (a tadpole trike - two wheels in front). With this I have no issues with prostate or bladder and also no neck, hand, wrist, or shoulder pain. Being thin, I sit on an extra cushion becuase I get a sore butt. This year I decided to try a two wheel recumbent, but not wanting to spend too much I looked for a beginner bike. I found a BikeE, whose manufacturer went out of business ten or so years ago, but sold many, so many are out there for a reasonable price. The BikeE is what is called a CLWB, compact long wheelbase bike. I have only ridden it on our local bike trail which is mostly flat, but I really like it. I had no problem adjusting at all. Balancing a recumbent is more touchy than DF (diamond frame - regular bike), but unless you have balance issues it should be no problem, just an adjustment period. For those with balance issues, the trike is the solution - can't fall over. The big adjustment with recumbents in my mind is threefold: 1. you use somewhat different leg muscles so you have to get accustomed before you can get to your normal speed and ease of cycling, 2. you can't stand on the pedals to climb hills, so it's all about your leg muscles and the correct gearing, and 3. generally you use lower gearing and a faster pedaling cadence. I have very low gearing on my trike and climb very steep hills, slowly, but I don't have to get off and push, just sit back and pedal. The Catrike is a very nice machine in every way (aluminum frame, good components, disc brakes, etc.), but cost about $2200 new. I bought mine used and still spent $1500. I bought my old BikeE for $300. I recommed both - great fun and comfort for IC folks. I recommend you try your friends bikes and trikes. Personally, I don't think I would want either an extremely low racer or high racer. The Rans Rifle sounds like a good in between style. Check out bentridersonline.com I still just ride for fun and exercise, probably only 40 miles per week weather permitting, but when I retire I would like to do more.

Thanks so much for your reply. Great insights. I have not really considered a trike, I'm going to check out what a catrike looks like. Question, if you enjoyed the trike and it was getting the job done why go to a BikeE? Reason I ask is it made me wonder if folks start off with a trike to get a feel for the recumbent ride and then once they have gotten accustomed want to challenge themselves with the two wheel version. I checked out the bentridersonline.com. terrific forum, that's for suggesting! Do you know if short wheel base bikes are easier to learn on versus short wheel base bikes? I really loves you're saying that with medications and IC diet that you are getting along good, gives me hope! Oh, new nose less seat arrived in the mail, quite different. Pretty funny looking, easy to put on the bike but is swivels a lot when your sitting on it with your sit bones, that is certainly going to take some getting use to! Ha. The makes said to give it 12 rides before forming an opinion. I shall try to keep an open mind. If it will just give me something to ride so I can take my time choosing a recumbent to purchase I'll be tickled pink.
signature: "You must master a new way to think before you can master a new way to be", Marianne Williamson.

contents: Sorry to reply so long after your post. In answer to your question, I got the BikeE just because I wanted to try a two-wheeled recumbent. I'm no expert at all. The Catrike is the only trike I've ridden and the BikeE the only 2 wheel version. I didn't even try others at a bike shop. Your riding friends would be much better advisers on recumbent. My only expertise here is testifying that recumbents are great for people with IC - and more fun and more comfortable otherwise - in my opinion. I don't think that trikes are starter recumbents. Some people like one, some the other, most like both. The main thing is to keep riding with whatever works for you. Good luck.

I'm so confused...Everything I've read says exercise helps IC. So I've been following a good, clean diet, been feeling ok, and Monday, I started out the day by walking on my treadmill for 40 min with an incline. Aerobically, I felt fine...was able to work up a decent sweat but still had energy left. However soon after I finished, I noticed my pelvic pain returned with a strong intensity and hasnt let up since. I used to be pretty active prior to IC (ran a half marathon and a few 10ks). Are these days over? I'd like to slowly build up my endurance and start lifting some weights again too. Any suggestions? Thanks!

I can't use the treadmill (causes instant flare for me), but I LOVE the elliptical trainer and the rowing machine. I'm now up to 35 minutes on the elliptical and 15 minutes rowing. That's a pretty good workout, especially when I add a little strength building and stretching with a personal trainer. (Treated myself to 5 sessions to set up a program that I can do independently.) I've had appts #1+2 and am now up to double what I started with 3 weeks ago... Hope you find something that works for you!

contents: The treadmill is what i was doing when all of my symptoms started so I'm a bit leary now (3 yrs later). Whether its a problem for me, I dont know because I havent had the nerve to step back on it. good luck diana
signature: (\__/) (o.O ) (> < ) This is Bunny. He's on his way to world domination.

contents: I have to say that in the beginning of my diagnosis of IC (2 years ago this month) I could not do the treadmill. A good year after taking my meds and following the diet, I was able to do 30 minutes no problem on an incline. Today I am doing 60 minutes daily. I think it takes time to heal and do a little trial and error in a few months from now. Until that time, plain waking for 30 minutes at a moderate pace worked for me. Good luck. liz

Sugar, as I said in my PM to you...if you start out SLOWLY, you can work your way up. 40 mins. at in incline is to much. For everyone else (again sugar...in my PM to you) start out with 0 (zero) incline and only walk at a 2-3 or 3-4 pace, for only 10-15 mins. Try that, or try the same thing on the EFX which is better for your joints and not as hard on your bladder and inner thighs. Good luck!

contents: I gave up the tredmill also. Elliptical machines and recumbent bikes (the kind that you pedal out in front of you instead of below you, bigger seats for my seat too lol). I also picked up a tai chi tape and have heard hatha yoga is good. I also have an indoor walking tape, its more like walking in place, some low kicks, knees up etc. that I do here n there. I know most of that tape so I kind of do it w/out the tape now. I can take a nice walk when the weather is good, but I dont take a very fast pace. I also have one of those yoga balls, I use it to do squats, I put it against a wall, then I lean my back on it and roll down to squat then back up. Whatever exercise you do, definately take it slow, just like what had already been suggested. I hope you find something that works for ya.

Using the treadmill for me makes the IC pain worse as well. Most I can do is 15 minutes on a small incline. Walking outside is different. I can do up to 30 minutes before the pain gets to where I have to stop.

Just this week for the very first time I said to my husband, I think the treadmill put me in a flare. I had never noticed that before. This is upsetting because I can't imagine giving that up. Giving up good food is bad enough! But, I have to say that the day this happened I was not feeling good and very fatigued. I have had a rough month. I think there are times when our IC bodies tell us enough is enough! Lyn

The treadmill isn't good for me either. I posted previously that I thought exercise was a trigger for me. I slowly started getting back to exercise by doing yoga. I currently go to a gentle intro class once a week and use a video (Yoga Zone) another 1-2 x a week. I try to get to the gym 2x a week and have finally been able to work up to 20 min on the elliptical trainer. I find it less jarring than the treadmill. It probably depends on which program you use though and I don't add extra tension. I also use the treadmill but not on an incline yet. I took baby steps with the elliptical starting at 3 min, then 5, etc. I haven't added in much with weights yet and do mostly the two machines and stretching/yoga.

Originally posted by sugar I'm so confused...Everything I've read says exercise helps IC. So I've been following a good, clean diet, been feeling ok, and Monday, I started out the day by walking on my treadmill for 40 min with an incline. Aerobically, I felt fine...was able to work up a decent sweat but still had energy left. However soon after I finished, I noticed my pelvic pain returned with a strong intensity and hasnt let up since. I used to be pretty active prior to IC (ran a half marathon and a few 10ks). Are these days over? I'd like to slowly build up my endurance and start lifting some weights again too. Any suggestions? Thanks! Hi there,walking on incline on trademill is no no for IC suffered, because most IC suffered also suffered from pelvic floor dyfunction.When walking on incline it through your pelvic floor into abnormal gait.When I started walking on trademill my Doc. said that I should start with walking slowly for 15-20 minute a day and slowly work up to 1 hour a day of flat surface not incline,and I was told not to jog because joging mean I lifted my knee.He also make me a shoe slip for me to wear due to flat feet,and that help a lot.Flat feet and high arch feet always cause problem with back,knee,hip and shinspleen after excersive that what I was told.To correct it, use specialy made slip just for individual feet.

Hi everyone, I have a question for those people who are working out. I've been walking (briskly) on my treadmill every day for about an hour, and I also take my dog out for about an hour a day, but after a while you want something a little more. I used to be really fit - I loved working out - I did weights, ran stairs, etc. Over the past few years, my workouts became less intense and yes, I have gained some weight but nothing to crazy. I've always been good with the cardio but I'm scared to start weight training again. I really want to though - my muscles look "flabby." Any good suggestions for some weight related exercises that I can throw into my workout that will not cause me to flare? I'm also looking into throwing some gentle yoga in there too...not too sure where to start with that one though - I've heard mixed reviews! Any thoughts or suggestions? Cheers! Robin
signature: Much love, Robin Started having IC symptoms suddenly after miscarriage in January 2013, no history of UTIs or bladder problems, Scheduled for Cysto/Hydro to confirm diagnosis on April 1st (great date, huh??) I decided to not take medicine for my IC since I'm trying to get pregnant, so I'm working with herbs and supplements: Pascoe pH Balance (3 tabs, 2x per day), Golden Aloe supplements (2 tabs, 2x per day), Vitanica Bladder Ease (1 tab, 3x per day - this will be increasing - I just try to start new supplements slowly!), MSM (1 tab, 2x per day), Probiotic (1 tab, 3x per day), Ashwadangha (1 tab, 1x per day), Mag Phos Tissue Salts (2x per day to help constitution), Renew Life Flax/acacia fibre (1 scoop per day because constipation sucks!), I also follow the IC diet very closely! It's very important to stay on it closely! Remission is my goal!!!

contents: Unfortunately you really have to learn this by trial and error as different people can handle different things. Some people do well with Pilates, while some can't handle much core exercise at all. I've always been fine with standard upper/lower body exercises like curls, rows, push-ups, squats, lunges, etc., but sometimes abdominal exercises like crunches hurt. Yoga can be great but you have to be really in tune with your body. I would think most standing yoga poses would be fine, but ones that require you to lay on your stomach should be done with caution. With any exercise, make sure you aren't unconsciously tensing your pelvic floor region.

Hi, i am also someone who loves to exercise and it kills me that IC has held me back from being myself in the gym but I have found things that work and things that do not. I do yoga about two or three days a week now and I go on the oliptical machines and lift light weights. I do some ab workouts but anything that requires me to bring my legs into my chest I stay away from because it irritates me. The regular bike and running are the most painful so I no longer do those activities. I hope this helps! Have u found anything that works for you yet?

Hi Kali, All I've been doing so far is walking on the treadmill, walking my dog, (those I do daily) I've done some calf raises on the edge of stairs (no shoulder weights) and I've tried some yoga, but the yoga sent me into a flare. I tried a new yoga last night (yin yoga) which is very relaxing and stretching but I had pelvic pains all last night (in addition to a bad headache!!) - it felt like a flare was coming on, but I think I was able to get it in time with some AZO. As the day progressed I ended up feeling better I'm considering trying the yin yoga again but I want to talk to my PF therapist about it. My husband is a personal trainer so I asked him to put an upper body workout together for me this weekend. I feel like I'm being so lazy....I can't run anymore and can't do any real significant weights - especially if they make me flex my abs What ab workouts do you do? I was thinking about ordering the pilates for IC CD - I loved doing pilates before and found they made such a big difference in my health and well being - that and running. I'm trying to keep in mind that healing is a slow process - I was just diagnosed in January and when I think of where I was at the beginning of January until now I have to say it's like night and day. In the past three weeks I've had way more good days than bad, which is a step in the right direction. I just want to keep the momentum going because exercise really does make me feel better - and I think it's part of the reason why in a month I've started to handle this IC thing better! I hope you are well and having more good days than bad days! Thanks so much for your reply! Robin

It's very important to stay on it closely! Remission is my goal!!!

contents: The best way which i feel for losing wait is doing a power yoga for 30 minutes everyday and it is the best way to heal and lose wait. So have a try.
signature: what is the best tan? think and then select.

contents: I wsa doing MMA (mixed martial arts) until all of this came about. From what I have read, that isn't the best thing for me to continue doing. What can I do? I've lost 42 lbs, I want to lose 34 more and I certainly don't want to gain any back. I hate exercising but I know that I need to for a multitude of reasons. Please advise.
You are the only person who can judge what is and isn't good for you to do. Does it hurt to do what you are doing now? If not, don't stop!

i am a fitness instructor and i continue to teach with my ic, but when my flares are really bad, like they are right now, then i have to pull back and get subs. sucks but i love my active live and hope to continue it as long as possible.

Hi everyone! I have been on a downward spiral since my diagnosis 7 years ago next month and capped with losing my job a year ago. I have gained a tremendous amount and am desperate to lose it. I was also diagnosed with IBS four months ago. I try to eat the green vegetables that have always worked so well for me but they are triggering my IBS! I keep hearing good things about hydroxycut and those types of things but I am afraid to take them for fear that they will trigger my bladder! Has anyone used any of these weight loss products and had any luck??? Thanks!!!!

I find the Weight Watchers point system to be helpful. But, it's important to stay positive and focus on what you CAN eat, not on their recipes or suggestions which can be frustrating. I don't go to the meetings because 90% of what I hear there, I can't do because of the IC. But, I can use their website to track my food, exercise and weight - and that does work to help me maintain or lose weight. I really like the app for my iphone, so I can track from anywhere. Sparkpeople is a free website that also lets you log in what you eat & exercise. It uses calories though and I find the points easier to keep track of. I wouldn't touch any of the weight loss "supplements" or substitution bars or drinks, they're full of chemicals and I don't believe in eating things that are not food.

In all cases, I strongly encourage you to discuss your medical treatment with your personal medical care provider. Only they can, and should, give medical recommendations to you. ------------------------------------------------------ New favorite quote: "God gives us only what we can handle. Apparently God thinks I'm a bad-ass" ~Author Unknown Source - Pinterest " Current treatments: -IC diet -Elavil 50mg at night -Continuous use birth control pills (4-5 periods/year) -Heparin/Marcaine/Sodium Bicarb home instills at night 3-4x per week, more often if needed -Pyridium if needed, -Pain medicine at bedtime daily, as needed during the day several times per week -Antibiotic when doing an instillation to prevent UTI -Colace & SmartFiber to treat chronic constipation from meds, Fleet enema as needed -Dye Free Benadryl 50 mg at bedtime -"Your Pace Yoga: Relieving Pelvic Pain" dvd, walking, treadmill at gym -Managing stress= VERY important! -Fur therapy: Hugging the cat!

contents: Positively, without a doubt, my favorite training for fat loss is circuit training.
signature: Underarm sweat

contents: Weight Watchers can work well for ICers because it's a point system --- nobody tells you WHAT to eat --- but the program helps you to stay within reasonable calorie guidelines by assigning "points" to the various foods and drinks, which is much easier to track. Good luck! Donna

contents: There's a great app called MyFitnessPal that has calorie counts for almost EVERYTHING. Works well if you are diet-sensitive because you just input the foods you are eating and your exercise.

Thanks everyone for your responses! I am currently doing WW and lost 9 pounds and then I completely stalled. I am so frustrated! I work out, eat right and nothing. smh I am quickly being reminded of why I never stick with diets. I have a large amount of weight to lose and stalling after 9 pounds when needing to lose more than 100 is really frustrating.

Anyone have flares from swimming in a chlorine swimming pool? I am going to give it a try and was just wondering if it was safe to do. I hope that this is another thing that I don't have to give up. I haven't been in a pool since I was diagnosed last year. Here's hoping!
signature: Diagnosed: November 2011 Current treatments: Elmiron 100mg 3xday; Azo as needed, Prelief as needed, IC diet, ICN support, my faith.

contents: I can't even use regular pads because of the chlorine in them, there no way I'm getting into a chlorine pool and taking that chance.

I don't use chlorine in my backyard pool because I don't want the pain that goes with it. I use Baquacil instead. I had been using it before I found out I had IC because it's easier to maintain than a chlorine pool. I swim in it 5+ times a week and never have had a problem. It's more expensive than chlorine but the ease of maintaining it was well worth the price.

I only go in chlorine pools for brief periods of time. I haven't tried to spend longer times recently. I do feel better all around, but not sure if it would hurt or not. When I was first diagnosed, it was horrible. I love to swim.........so I swim in natural water (oceans and lakes) when I can. :-)

contents: Few things cause me to flare these days as long as I take my medication but of the things that still do a Chlorine pool can be one of them...but when I do want to risk it and it is a public pool where can't add more baking soda all the time I make baking soda tea bags and put them in my suit. This does work but I will still try to minimize how long you go in and take your suit off ASAP since it is holding that Chlorine water in it and your still soaking it in from the suit. Of course things you would normally do when eating risky foods would also help too. If you don't have tea bags to make a tea bag of baking soda anything you can do to your suit so that it has some baking soda may really help. If you have your own or it is a friends that you can put the baking soda into the pool you may not have as much of a problem....there is directions on the big bags of baking soda for how much for pools.
signature: Faith, Hope, and Love, Katrina I believe God is using me. He uses me for God. Things I gain from all my suffering are meant to help others. I hope I can help you too. Email me or start a chat if you like my help or anything. I CARE!Illnesses: IC,IBS, IBD, GERD, PFD, Epilepsy, Endo, Allergies, RLM,Rapid heart beat, low blood pressure,Gastritis,Gall stones,Tendonitis,migraines, Shingles, Prolapsed pouch,ext. fatigue (current problem) I have seen periods of remission and I have seen them end and return. At this time remission is over and working on getting it back! IC Volunteers are not medical authorities nor do we offer medical advice. Only they can, and should, give medical recommendations to you. or find me on facebook http://www.facebook.com/kat671?ref=profile Be the Miracle! & Pay it Forward! [email protected] please contact me...I am here to help!

contents: I know the ICN recommends rowing for cardio. I'm curious about what kind of technique to use that won't aggravate my symptoms. I know you can do rowing arms only, but you can also slide back at varying amounts. Based on my limited knowledge, it seems like sliding back and forth would jar my bladder and aggravate my symptoms. Is this not correct? I would kill to get a good cardio workout right now.

I used to cycle 2-3 times a week and in between I used my bike to run errands and such (I don't know how to drive). However since I developed IC I get agonising pain after cycling. I am on an anti histamine for the IC now and my IC is definitely better. I haven't tried cycling since I started the anti histamine though so I don't know if I will be able to cycle. Otherwise, can anyone tell me if they have any suggestions for how to make cycling more comfortable? Any tips or ideas? I need to be able to cycle since it is my main method of transport and also because it's the only exercise I actually like.
I don't know what kind of seat you have on your bike but I found that using a large type seat helped me. I call them big butt seats and they are available at bike stores.

I have been feeling so GROSS lately!! I was a size 5 a little over a year ago when I got diagnosed. All the stress, stress eating (I'm bad about that!), and the dietary restrictions have made me gain so much weight!! I don't fit into ANY of my clothes, but I feel so bad about it that I don't want to go shopping. I wear stretchy pants now. I need to find something other than walking...because I take long walks once to twice a day with my puppy. I know that I really need to cut back on the sugar and carbs, but it's hard when I can't exercise too! Does anyone do yoga, pilates, anything else that seems to help? I tried doing some floor exercises...situps, push ups, planks, jumping jacks, lunges...they all made me have to pee more! HELP!

Hi there, Here's a video on IC and exercise. Very helpful tips I also use the yoga dvd, "Your Pace Yoga: Relieving Pelvic Pain". I got it in the ICN shop. There's also a pilates video: "New Dawn Pilates" for pelvic pain patients, including those of us with IC. The Leslie Sansone Walking dvds (I got them at a TJ Maxx or Marshalls store - can't remember which, but they were only about $5 each) are good too for days you want to do something indoors. I don't know how fast your puppy lets you walk, but those might be options to speed it up a bit if the puppy is slowing you down. Hope that helps a bit. I understand about the weight gain. I initially gained 45 pounds thanks to IC. I've lost some of it, but it's a constant battle....

contents: It is SOOO frustrating sometimes, because in the middle of everything I am trying to lose weight. So of course, everyone says to exercise, so I do, which inevitably (no matter what I do) manages to send me into horrible spasms that will put me out for the rest of the day. Even going for a walk in the neighborhood will have me doubled over by the time I get home. I have learned to completely avoid all core focused exercises and most lower body exercises, because they are a recipe for disaster. I just don't know what to do, everybody tells me that diet alone won't do, but exercise is steadily becoming less and less of an option. Sigh. I don't know if I really have a question, this is more of just a vent. Thanks for listening! -Ruth
signature: My life in a nutshell: So I and whenever I can. I spend a lot of time in and occasionally I spend a lot of time . This is my life and I take it one day at a time.

contents: Oh dear... I used to be at the gym 5 times a week and now I am so inactive. Flares and pain meds just killing energy levels. I just started something called 'geocaching' which is a real life treasure hunt using your GPS. Maybe give it a go? Getting out in the fresh air can be helpful and you can always choose locations that are in a park so you can have a rest and lay down. Somewhere with public toilets too!
signature: Current Medications: 25mg of Amitriptyline. I tried the vesicare and it SUCKS. ZERO improvement with Cephalexin.

contents: I have heard of geocaching before, I always thought it sounded kind of fun, maybe I will have to look into it. It is frustrating, because I used to be heavy, but lost weight and in the process became a real workout addict, I have always loved lifting weights, biking, walking, running, kickboxing, dancing, anything active, but when my IC got worse that all changed. Sigh. Anyway, thanks for the response and the idea!
signature: My life in a nutshell: So I and whenever I can. I spend a lot of time in and occasionally I spend a lot of time . This is my life and I take it one day at a time.

contents: I've been losing weight, but also am in treatment for cancer so that pretty well explains it. My appetite isn't good, which is the primary thing --- plus I'm drinking an adequate amount of water. I do short walks daily as ordered by physician --- only five to ten minutes, but I actually think that little bit of exercise is working for me. Donna

It is frustrating, because I used to be heavy, but lost weight and in the process became a real workout addict, I have always loved lifting weights, biking, walking, running, kickboxing, dancing, anything active, but when my IC got worse that all changed. Sigh. Anyway, thanks for the response and the idea! Same here, I was doing kickboxing and weights etc. Loved it. Now it's hard to do anything at all, which is why I like atleast getting out for walks and to the park. Geocaching is just a fun hobby and it feels so good to get outside. The walk to work in the morning kills me now and I used to go on such long runs!
signature: Current Medications: 25mg of Amitriptyline. I tried the vesicare and it SUCKS. ZERO improvement with Cephalexin.

contents: Originally posted by Claire_A Loosing weight is 80% diet and 20% exercises so whoever says diet alone wont work is silly . Just stick to diet and do what you can. Are you able to do some yoga? Not really, movement is generally painful right now. My whole pelvic area could only be described as "tender", the littlest things (sometimes even just bending over) hurt immensely and will occasionally send me into spasms. Right now, as you suggested, I am putting my main focus on diet, I know that may be all that I can do for a while (hopefully not forever). Just gotta keep moving, thanks for the encouragement!
signature: My life in a nutshell: So I and whenever I can. I spend a lot of time in and occasionally I spend a lot of time . This is my life and I take it one day at a time.

contents: I have thrown out my back and had problems with my discs in my lower back. My doc wants me to do physical therapy and build up my core. I am terrified of working on my core for fear that it will flare up my bladder and bring on spasms. BUT - I have to do this. Is there anything I can do to help myself? Is there an antispasmodic I should take after PT? Or do I come home and hold ice on my tummy?

I have had to do core exercises for years because of my back. After my initial fear, I just dove right in and did them. The physical therapist can help you find exercises that can help but that will not put too much pressure on your poor little bladder. Good luck, Sheila. Let us know how you do!

contents: I have put on 90 lbs in the last 18 mos. I desperately need to lose some weight but I am not sure what exercises I can do that won't make the IC worse.

I found that I had also put on weight with the IC. I started eating smaller meals (not a drastic change, but every bit helps). I also started walking everyday. Walking is low impact on your joints, it doesn't seem to jostle the bladder too much, and you have all the control as to how fast you go and how far you go. I started with just going around the block. It has also helped me with other medical problems as well. I have also found that I am eating less since I started the walking routine. I am now walking about a mile a day, but it did take time to get there. You would be surprised what a little walking can do for you. I hope this helps and I wish you the best of luck!

Walk, Walk, Walk!!! I find - in my brain only maybe - that the more I sweat - the better I feel. AND I always know where the nearest bathroom is. I'm also a BIG fan of 100 calorie packs, rice cakes (they come in chocolate now), and bottled water. Frozen fruit, yogurt, and anything else I tell myself is healthy for me.

The elliptical. Good low impact cardio. I think A lot of the meds help put on weight. I've gone up and done the last year about 10 pounds. Eat less. You can't be as physical as a healthy person. I was a cross country runner before I got sick, now I don't really work out, but many a day I am too miserable to eat. losing weight is a ***** when you can't do 90 percent of the work outs, and no one wants to do anything while in pain, or sedated from the medication. I find I didn't gain more than two or three pounds the months I was taking hydroxyzine, but it made me too tired , just started amitriptalyn (can't spell) so we'll see how the weight goes. Not much advise, but you are understood.

This is a very hard question! But walking is good, drinking plenty of water, eating 5 or 6 small meals every 3 hours, eating small amount of protein with each meal. I did have to stop taking the Elavil. I put on a lot of weight with that. I also wear a pedometer, which helps remind me to keep moving all day long! Good luck!

contents: I have just returned to Weight Watchers after a hiatus (in which I gained 26 pounds - ugh) due to a new job and way too much stress. The plan is fairly IC friendly in that you can eat the IC diet, just within the points range per day/week. Yes, it's a drag that many of their recipes don't work for us, but if you set that aside and just use it for what we can do - tracking our meals and exercise, it can work quite well. I choose the meeting carefully that I go to. Some leaders push their recipes and snack products and that is frustrating for me, so I go to a group led by a woman who lost 70 pounds, runs 1/2 marathons, is in her 50s, and talks mostly about whole grains, proteins, and veggies/fruits. (Yeah, I have to ignore most of the fruit talk too.) One very helpful thing she had me do when I talked with her about the IC was to take the booklet that listed foods & their points and to highlight everything I CAN eat. That was very positive and reminded me that I will in fact, not starve, while I take care of my bladder with a gentle diet... Best wishes to you with weight loss. It is hard with IC, but it can be done.

contents: In terms of exercise when I first started up again the Elliptical was by far the best for my bladder. It mimics walking/running but without any sort of impact. I eventually transitioned into swimming again (I was a competitive all american swimmer in high school) but I know that isn't for everyone. The biggest problem with outdoors exercise is the bathroom so the gym works best for me. I too am trying to lose weight, total I am 30 lbs heavier than when I first started my symptoms. Part of my problem is Lyrica and stress eating. It's nice to know there are other people out there with the same weight issues with IC.

Running,jogging as well walk that is called the constitutional walk better for the health even for the loosing the weight from the body so need to continue it for ever until or unless not stable as per the body requirement so this is very hard task to achieve it within the period.
signature: Conway

contents: The elliptical and walking has worked best for me. I invested in an elliptical. It's pricey, but when you add up gym costs over the years it makes fiscal sense. And you don't have to suffer the embarrassment of using the bathroom all the time at the gym. Good music helps, too: both to make time go by fast, and to keep your mind off your bladder. Good luck!

SPINNING/CYCLING!!!!! I honestly find that this has the least impact on your bladder, compared to walking. I fell in love with this class. You sweat your butt off and can barely notice your bladder during the exercise. If you have nerve issues, I can understand how biking might not be a possibility. However, if you only suffer from IC, I highly recommend Cycling. I have been down in the dumps the past few months because I can't run anymore. When I had minor IC, I could still hike, jog, walk, everything. But until, I can get everything under control again. My soloution is a bike. If you cant afford to go to spinning classes, just hop on your bike and go for a ride! :-)

I hate to break the news to you but the Elavil you just started increases your appetite !! I have gain 12 lbs in 4 months....it's worth it though because my pain was awful. 30 days of post Hydrodistension pain.....yuck......I work out at least 3x week...helpful...but we all know you need to decrease your intake if you want to loose weight....exercise helps! Good Luck! Belle
signature: Readers Digest Version of my Urinary History:Urinary Tract Infections since age 19 VCG x 2 IVP x 1 Ultrasound of kidneys x 3 (currently have kidney stone) Dilation of Uretha x 2 Diagnosed with IC 2008 age 48 Cystoscopy with Overdistension of Bladder x 4 2008, 2009, 2010 and 2011 First Overdistension of Bladder had great results, last one I had pain >10 for 30 days ............................................................... Current Medications Elmiron, Elavil, Lyrica, Klonopin, Tagamet, Macrobid (PRN) for infections, Glucosamine with Chondroitin, Fish Oil, Calcium with D, Acidophillus, Baking soda 1 tsp w/water PRN, Prelief, Estrace Cream, Combipatch, Fiber tabs, Diastat (for flares)................................................................. Did Not Work for Me Detrol, Vesicare, Toviaz, Goldenseal, D-Mannose, Quercitin

contents: Hi there, I was wondering whether anyone had tried to use a vibration plate for muscle tone and slimming? I am a little worried that the vibration might irritate the bladder and aggravate my IC which is more or less under control most of the time. I have a friend who has had spectacular results with such a machine and am very tempted but loathe to spend the money in case I can't use it. Any comments most welcome. Thanks and hope you are all having an IC free day Kathie

Vibrations is a big aggravtor of my symptoms. This is why I don't do well riding in cars the slight vibration I get there bothers my bladder big time. I also went to to concert feeling pretty good, our seats were in bleacher style seats that vibrated with the bass in the music, I loved the concert but I could barely walk by the time it was over. Everyone is different, I would be afraid to spend my money on this. I am sure you know your bladder best though. MG
signature: My are with you all. May you all find a way to peace and joy in your lives.

contents: I agree with Mothergoose. Vibration can be a big trigger for me. Can you try your friend's machine first to see how you do before you buy it? I would hate to discourage you if it could help, but hate to have you spend the money if it causes a flare. Let us know what you decide!!!

contents: I started walking a month ago. My son has to get up every morning and be at the high school for Weights and Conditioning. I've been using this opportunity to FORCE myself to walk on the track. It takes me about an hour to walk 3 miles and I feel like DEATH afterwards. This is always in the mornings - like 9 AM. I always feel like I have cement in my shoes and molasses in my veins and every step is a struggle. I've recently been walking in the evenings with my friend Amy. We drive a few miles to this Sunflower Nature Center and we talk and walk and sweat. Once around is 2.3 miles. We walk it twice is LESS than an hour, so 4.6 miles. I always feel GREAT afterwards!! I feel like I could do a 3rd lap around! That would be 6.9 miles. So, WHY is it I can walk so much faster and feel so much better walking this trail than walking on the high school track??? I just don't get it.

Perhaps its the scenery and/or having a friend walking with you. It could also be that evenings just jive with you better. Regardless, keep doing what youre doing. Maybe you could cut out the mornings and add that distance to your evening walk.
signature: (\__/) (o.O ) (> < ) This is Bunny. He's on his way to world domination.

contents: How lucky to have a beautiful place to walk & a friend who walks it with you! My walking buddy moved away & I now don't feel safe walking alone at the lake near my house.... Are you pushing yourself to walk faster in the morning to "get it over with"? You could be tensing muscles to do that... Or are you better hydrated by evening? If you, like me, restrict water at night to reduce bathroom trips, and then don't drink water when you get up to make up for it (because you're going walking), you could be starting your morning walk dehydrated... I also find walking on a track or treadmill tedious and the best solution is my ipod... (But if walking on the street, I only put one earbud on, so I can still hear the environment). I think it's great you're exercising, I got out of the habit this past year and I'm really hating getting started again. It's easier to continue than re-start, so keep up the good work!

contents: I think you could BOTH be right. Maybe I'm not a morning person. I do restrict water in the evenings. I am always borderline dehydrated - all the time. I know, shame on me. Maybe I can get Amy to do a 3rd time around with me at the nature park. We've had to stop because of the heat but it is supposed to be cooler this upcoming week. And Amy had another kidney stone, and then before that we both had ugly blisters. LOL. I'm ready to just suck it up and go again.

I've never been a morning exerciser, ever. A slow walk is ok but going to the gym or doing a strenuous walk in the morning leaves me feeling like crap. By noon I'm fine and raring to go though. I never could figure out why this is. No wonder no one wants to talk to me in the morning if I'm at the gym- Ha I'm also really cranky early in the morning. I'm wondering if it may have something to do with blood sugar levels in the mornings?
Does anyone have a protein powder suggestion that is safe? Thanks.

contents: I work full time as a CNA at a nursing home. This means that Im lifting, tugging, pulling, and pushing people all day long. Im also on my feet the entire shift. I rarely have a flare due to work. A few weeks ago, I bought a jogging stroller. Thought I would lose some weight. I didnt jog. I just went power walking. After a week of power walking, it threw me into the worst flare Ive ever had. I ended up getting my first ever set of 3 instils. I also got put on a much stronger pain med to take PRN. I waited a week and felt great. No pain meds! I went for a short (1 mile) power walk. Threw me back into another bad flare! Ive been in this flare since Monday. Im very frustrated! My son and I enjoy the walks. Also, Ive found when Im flaring, if Im just sitting around, Im fine. Once Im up and moving, Im back in pain. Could this pain be my PFD acting up? Im not currently on my maintenece drug (elavil) due to breastfeeding. Ive been off it for 2 years. Is there a chance that once I wean my son and get back on this med that I will be able to power walk? I want to take advantage of this nice spring we are having. And lose some weight!
signature: Meds: Percocet PRN; Pyridium PRN. First symptoms Dec 2005. Diagnosed in March 2006. I am the proud mom of a two year old boy! I was lucky enough to be in remission my entire pregnancy. If you have any questions regarding my pregancy, delivery, breastfeeding, or pumping, feel free to contact me! Im on Facebook! Just tell me you are from the ICN. Look me up under Kim Wayne. I love working as a CNA in a nursing home. Started school in August part time. Going for my LPN!!!!

contents: Elavil is a miracle drug for me, so it wouldn't surprise me if you felt better again when you're back on it. How about just walking (no power!) with the stroller for a short distance to see if you can use it that way, and then build up to longer distance rather than faster? Or, since you probably got the stroller due to the difficulty of exercise with a little one, how about the Leslie Sansone Walking videos? (I can do the Miracle Mile one and two mile dvds in my living room. You can start with just 1/4 mile or 1/2 mile and turn it off, try a little more the next day...) Some exercise is better than none! (Says me, I'm in the worst physical condition of my life. I really don't recommend getting as out of shape as I have this past year - it's not fun to re-start.) Best, Kadi

signature: 28 yrs old, I have little to no problems now with my bladder, unless I am very stressed out, I forget to take Elmiron for a few days, or I eat or drink something that I know is a trigger...(i.e) like eating a tomato or drinking tea.... Me in my graduation gown!

I have missed 2 years of her swimming lessons bc of this, and we now have a 1 year old who also adores the pool, but I am scared to try it again..
Why is this? Does the water enter the bladder? Does it just irritate the vagina?
I can not go swimming in pools, I get a huge flare but I do fine with swimming in the ocean!
signature: Diagnosed in July 2003 Meds and supplements: Desert Harvest Aloe Vera Prelief as needed Megafood multi Fish oil (Nordic naturals) Probiotic (Mega food mega flora brand) Vitamin D (Carlson's solar gems 4000 IU) Favorite Quote: "Dance as if no one were watching, sing as if no one were listening, and live every day as if it were your last."

contents: This is interesting (and sad). Swimming is my favorite form of exercise. In the last few years, however (I'm 55 and have IC since age 18), exercise makes me flare especially the vulvodynia part, which is more recent. I can never tell if it's the swimming itself that gives me a delayed reaction flare (like the next day) or if it's the chlorine or bromine. Where I live, the health club has an ultraviolet filter and low-chlorine. Another pool I use occasionally in another city uses bromine and maybe chlorine at the regular strength without the ultraviolet filter. I feel worse from both pools. I do freestyle for 20-30 minutes, without flippers since the flippers made it worse. There are no other pool options to experiment with. The ocean where I am is very very cold so that's out. This is all so depressing. I guess the only true test would be to swim for the same amount of time in the lake this summer.

I have been struggling with the ups and downs of IC since last July. Like everybody, I have tried different treatments and hopefully, have found a combination of diet, medication and instills that work for me most of the time. I'd been doing ok, but was still depressed, so in January began volunteering at a therapeutic riding center. I owned hunters for many years and used to ride a lot, but have not been involved with horses for about 12 years. Anyway, I noticed that I never had any symptoms at the barn, no matter what I was doing, so I volunteered for more time, and began a certification program that will allow me to be an instructor. This program requires that I pass a riding test. My urologist cleared me to try riding, just to take it slow. I rode comfortably several times with no problem. However, I began flaring two days ago and it seems to be getting worse each day despite my taking the meds that usually work. I haven't ridden for several days, and the flare didn't start on a day I rode, but it did start on the day my husband of 30 years decided to move out. This disease has been very hard on our marriage, and it was a mutual decision, so it's not like I was blindsided or abandoned. I don't know how people figure these things out, but I'm hoping this flare is stress related and not due to the horseback riding, as I feel I'm going to really need the involvement with horses to get through this next period in my life. So-finally-my questions. 1. Are there people out there who ride successfully? 2. Does anyone know if horseback riding can actually damage the bladder wall and cause the disease to progress? I can manage the flares, but I sure don't want to make this thing permanently worse. So far, I don't have any pelvic floor involvement, and that doesn't seem to have changed. 3. For people who flare when riding does it usually start right away, or in the next day or two? 4. Can stress cause an immediate, pretty severe flare? Thanks in advance to anyone with enough patience to read this loooong post and respond. I'm feeling pretty low, and could use some encouragement. Debbie

horse back riding Hi Debbie, I was sorry to read about your marriage, and about your flare. I haven't ridden in years, so I'm not sure whether the riding can cause a flare,but I recall that the last time I did ride, I had bladder discomfort(pre-IC), and actually ended up with a UTI. I wonder if you can put something on the saddle or under it, that would ease the impact on your bladder just in case that is part of the cause of your flare. Certainly, and I speak from personal experience here, stress can cause a flare. I can sometimes actually feel my bladder getting sore when I get very upset about something. I guess the only way for you to judge what is causing your current flare up is to stay away from riding for a while, after you take your ride for certification, and see how your bladder feels. As for the stress, try meditation, soaking in a tub, walking, being around the barn,(sweetest smell I know), and the horses, whatever is relaxing for you, and see if that helps. In the meatime maybe use some extra heat, baking soda, strict diet to calm your bladder. I think what you're doing(theraputic work), is wonderful. I hope things work out well for you. Keep us posted, Laurie

I was an A-circiut dressage competitor for years when my IC was in remission. Now, 5 minutes of riding puts me in a flare within a few hours. It has been such a loss, that after I moved to TN with my horse, I sent him back to FL with my friends since he was getting so depressed that I was not riding him anymore. (He's a show horse, and loves the lifestyle.) It was very hard for me to not have a horse, since I have been riding since childhood. I feel like IC has taken everything from me, and fight to get thru everyday. Yesterday was my 11th anniversary, and my husband and I have not been able to be intimate for 9 months now. I always wonder when he is going to get up and leave. I wish you luck in your riding goals. I would tell you to try a little at a time, and hopefully you can get back in the saddle. If you cant, at least keep doing the volunteer work, as just being with they horses can be a great stress relief. And from the sound of your situation with your husband, you could use some stress relief. I still kept my 2 donkeys, and they can be alot of fun to be around. I wish you luck and hope that things get better for you.
signature: I have basically done every treatment for IC except cyclosporine and botox. Everything has failed. I live on pain meds all day. My diet is so strict that I now have multiple vitamin deficiencies. I have also tried numerous alternative therapies. I would do a cystectomy, but doctors seem to think living in pain is better. Currently taking Synthroid, Elmiron, Percocet, Toradol, Desert Harvest aloe. Interstim done 4 times, what a waste. Also newly diagnosed with Hunners Ulcers on cysto. Now disabled due to IC. A body of life, a bladder of hell.

contents: Hello, my friends. I LOVE to exercise. I have been feeling well. Went to the gym & exercised today (elliptical, recumbent bike, treadmill, free weights for my arms, and exercise ball to work on my abs). I am not a high intensity exerciser, I take my time. If I am hurting or it feels like it is pulling around my bladder area & causing discomfort, I back off & take it a little easier. Sometimes after I exercise I have urgency that is moderate. Am I flaring from the mechanics of the exercise OR could it be from the breakdown of muscle (metabolism/lactic acid production) with those waste products filtering through & irritating my bladder? Does anyone else experience this? I had to take some Azo tonight. Maybe I should take some prelief with my exercise to combat the metabolic waste products that may be irritating my bladder? Any thoughts? I am so glad I have you all. Looking forward to hearing from you.
signature: Diagnosed: November 2011 Current treatments: Elmiron 100mg 3xday; Azo as needed, Prelief as needed, IC diet, ICN support, my faith.

contents: I believe it's the mechanics of the exercise irritating your pelvic floor. The most important thing is to keep your hips level so avoid stair master and fast running on the treadmill. How about rowing... I love doing that.. and its such good exercise! No idea about if prelief would help with exercise.. doubt it but that's just my guess. Jill

contents: Everyone's body is different. Personally, for me, my PT told me not to ride a bike or do any ab work. I do a lot of dance classes - zumba, hip-hop, cardio dance - and feel that even if I am flaring when I start the class I will usually feel better for a bit afterwards. My PT says it might have to do with the increased blood flow to the area and to keep dancing.

signature: lucky to live in hawaii but having a hard time and have a lot of questions. vesicare detrol la pyridium ditropan buspirone ?(new medications) chinese herbs: golden form with rehmannia triggers: alcohol, coffee, soda, chocolate, citrus, sex, spicy food, soy, running/jumping, pms, birth control pills, stress, and lack of zzz.

contents: Dance classes don't necessarily make me flare. If I am flaring before I start the class and do it anyway I almost always find that after the class the flare stops. It's as if the area is a bit numb and then it feels fine for hours afterward. My PT thinks it is a good thing because perhaps it has to do with the increased blood flow to the area. Sometimes, however, if I am having a good string of days and take a dance class it will make me flare afterwards. Then again, there have been days where I felt good and took it easy and I flared anyway. So, my theory is that I don't know what the @#$%^&@# makes me flare anymore and since dance doesn't necessarily make it worse and most of the time makes it better then I'm going to dance. Dance classes bring me absolute and utter joy and I'll be damned if I'm going to give them up. But, everyone's body is different. I was very careful to show my PT the dance moves during one of my sessions and get her approval. It's trial and error for all of us. But, I would be very mindful of lower body strength training, ab work and biking if I were you. Your PT would know best.

contents: Zumba I'm really thinking that the ZUMBA drove the Nail right in my coffin. I teach dance and that never really bothered me until after the ZUMBA! Ive been in a non stopped flare since Nov of 2011 and I'm sick of it. IDK what to do next. Last edited by angelakarson; 02-26-2012, 12:56 AM.

Wow - it just goes to show how different we all are. If I'm flaring and I take a Zumba class I can calm it down. My PT thinks it might have something to do with the increased blood flow to the area. I was so nervous to take the first class after my diagnosis, but I got to a point where IC robbed me of so much that I wasn't going to let it take away my dance classes too. Zumba will either make me feel better or I'll stay the same and at least I enjoyed myself. But our differences is one reason I think a good PT is a great resource.

Those 2 operations (tuck and birch) failed in 2004, I hope to get them re-done this year 2012. Riding on a riding lawn mower, on a bicycle, 4-wheeler, and even sometimes in a car my bladder is more irritated. I am unable to move faster than a normal walking speed or I will have bladder pain. Getting down on my hands and knees to garden causes me bladder pain and tight fitting clothing causes me bladder pain. Sorry i am not much help here

That's me but I do not know if my bladder has prolapsed. How does that happen? I have noticed that the jumping up and down is not good and I live on the worst road in the country so that really does it. I also ordered one of those cushions to put in the car hoping that helps. I am currently taking Triple Flex (glucosamine, chondroitin, MSM), Desert Harvest Freeze Dried ALoe Vera, and Colostrum. Im hoping something will help. Ive started drinking NEO smarter water which is Alkaline and antioxidant water and it tastes great.

some Causes of a Prolapsed Bladder Causes of a Prolapsed Bladder The following factors are commonly associated with causing a prolapsed bladder: Childbirth: This is the most common cause of a prolapsed bladder. The delivery process is stressful on the vaginal tissues and muscles, which support a woman’s bladder. Menopause: Estrogen, a hormone that helps maintain the strength and health of muscles in the vagina, is not produced after menopause. Straining: Lifting heavy objects, straining during bowel movements, having a long-term condition that involves coughing, or having long-term constipation may damage the muscles of the pelvic floor. Symptoms of a Prolapsed Bladder The first symptom that women with a prolapsed bladder usually notice is the presence of tissue in the vagina that many women describe as something that feels like a ball. Other symptoms of a prolapsed bladder include the following: Discomfort or pain in the pelvis Tissue protruding from the vagina (The tissue may be tender and may bleed.) Difficulty urinating A feeling that the bladder is not empty immediately after urinating (incomplete voiding) Stress incontinence (urine leakage during sneezing, coughing, exertion, etc) More frequent bladder infections Painful intercourse (dyspareunia) Low back pain Some women may not experience or notice symptoms of a mild (grade 1) prolapsed bladder.

i exercise daily by walking. if i try to hike, i notice pain afterwords. somehow i am fine during my walk most days though. i might notice slight pain, but nothing out of the ordinary. it's not intense or going to make me lose weight super fast, but i just want to be healthy and not jiggle

contents: Originally posted by icnmgrjill I believe it's the mechanics of the exercise irritating your pelvic floor. The most important thing is to keep your hips level so avoid stair master and fast running on the treadmill. How about rowing... I love doing that.. and its such good exercise! No idea about if prelief would help with exercise.. doubt it but that's just my guess. Jill Rowing machine like this? http://www.amazon.com/dp/B001A0ZT2I/ref=asc_df_B001A0ZT2I1917009?smid=ATVPDKIKX0DER&tag=hyprod-20&linkCode=asn&creative=395093&creativeASIN=B001A0ZT2I We have that at home. I like the hip abductor and hip adductor and leg press.

contents: I am a fitness instructor that also teaches Zumba. i have noticed that when i feel bad prior to class, then the pain subsides during class. i just figured that it was due to the endorphine rush that came from exercise. i also teach step and yoga and have noticed the same thing. so even when i'm depressed, i push myself to go to class (instead of subbing it out) i usually feel better for a little bit after wards.

Has anyone had Restylane injections for wrinkles? I am assuming because Restylane is hyaluronic acid that it should not bother my bladder. One thing that concerns me is their website states that it should not be used by people with previous bad allergies, particularly to microorganisms known as gram-positive bacteria. So is there gram positive bacteria in it? Could it cause a bladder infection. I guess that would be a question for the doctor but still would like to know if anyone has had the injections for wrinkles. Thanks
Has anyone successfully done squats (200+ lbs), push press (300+ lbs), or ab work without flaring? If so, how? I really miss working out my legs and abs, but I'm afraid to start again.

I am not gonna say I have. I have not worked out in ages to begin with. I deal with degenerative disc disease and walk with a cane often. Oh! I did notice you had put 200+ and 300+ lbs. OK. I probably could not squat 200+lbs even if I did not have IC, DDD, pain every single day, more tiredness, and so forth.(as I was a weak dude even before) (although I must figure you use to)Geez! It would be nice to be able to workout again.(and play golf)(and get employed again) (no IC)(no DDD) I have been denied disability twice by now. I realized I could not answer your question, but I also realized you had not gotten a response so far. You may end up getting more responses as well.

Ab work tends to bother me if I hit it too intensely. Exercises that involve leg lifts and such (instead of, say, crunches) tend to do better. I do a lot of yoga/pilates and I have to be careful with anything that requires me to lay on my stomach (like a lot of back/shoulder work). Squats, lunges, and leg press only bother me if I'm already in pretty bad shape. Usually I'm fine with them.
Thanks guys! I appreciate the help.

If you have IC I totally recommend that you find a physical therapist who can evaluate you and perhaps see if you have pelvic floor issues. That PT will become an invaluable resource, not just for IC, but for exercise questions. For example, mine has me doing leg presses. But, they could be a big mistake for others. I have also been advised to not do any crunches or moves that will pull up on my pelvic floor. I was at the gym 6 days a week before IC, but now I am back to 3 days a week. I even showed her my dance moves and got her blessing to go back to those classes. I just think it's best to have a PT advise you so you don't end up doing any more damage.

contents: Hi I'm a backpacker, or I should say I was. I haven't done any serious trips since being diagnosed. I'm sure you could search around on the web for all natural backcountry backpacking food. I'd stay away from the Lipton processed meals. You could also dehydrate fresh foods before you go. As far as the water goes, it is very pure and safe as long as you filter. (I've hiked in that part of Maine) And definitely bring all of your pain medicine. I hope you're not afraid of moose! You'll see plenty of them. One more thing, don't go during black fly season, May/June. Last edited by vanilla; 01-15-2011, 12:03 PM.

Hope you have a great hike. We live up "here" in the great state of Maine. As you know it is so beautiful, maybe you can forget about the IC for just a bit. Just bring some TP and some old plastic grocery bags and you'll be cool. Clean up after yourself and all will be well. That is great, what a wonderful thing to look forward to! You obviously know the hiking routine. Know hub Bob who has the IC can pretty much pull over whenever he has to (which is often) and nobody cares. We are pretty loose up here, and just a couple of words to explain your condition does it. How exciting for you, and yes, hope you are not planning it during fly season. Should be a gorgeious spring and summer, but the flies will be flying with all the frozen stuff on the ground now. So excited for you, it is a great trail. Stay well, let us know how it goes. Jill, wife of Bob

contents: I used to hike. The most memorable for me was a five day trek into Idaho's Frank Church- River of no Return Wilderness area. The entire first day was switchbacks up a mountainside so vertical that I feared I wouldn't make it. If your IC can handle them, sardines make a pretty good compact protein source. One of the best things I brought along was little plastic bags of homemade pancake mix, -flour, salt, baking powder, sugar, powdered milk, then just add water to the bag and squeeze to mix. We found plenty of huckleberries which when slightly mashed made a great topping!
signature: "The happiest people don't necessarily have the best of everything; they just make the best of everything they have."

contents: I used to hike a lot, but having more down days than good put an end to that. My last hike was a 5 day hike up to a lake called Berg lake because it is full of icebergs all year round. Where my kids went to school it is a right of passage to do this hike at the end of grade 7. It is 2 days pretty much straight up, a day of hiking around once there, 2 days hiking down, with 20 -13 year olds. It's about 30 miles round trip, not for the faint of heart. When my oldest 2 did it I had a little one at home and felt I could not leave him that long. When the youngest one turn came I was determined to go on this trip, I was in the throws of prediagnoses of IC. I went and loved it, it was a challange and hard on my bladder. I drank the water, it is all freash glacial water and I had no problems with it. My biggest problem was the second day where there is no water for a considerable uphill streach, and I had 2 1 liter bottles of water and drank it all, it was hot and I always drink alot (I just peed off the side of the trail and bagged my TP) a paramedic father on the trip saw I was in trouble and he gave me some of his water, and some of the kids shared their water. (for me IC causes me to sweat lots, when I am flaring) anyways I made it through. I premade most of my meals and then vaccumed sealed them in bags, then you only have to put the bag in a pot of boiling water. Your trip I am sure will be longer so I amnot sure if that will work for you. I packed as light as possible, opted to smell rather than have more cloths than needed, just packe layers. Marenio wool claim to fame is you can wear it for a week and not smell, it is also light and warm for it's weight. I made my own trail mix with nuts I can eat, and white choc chips. This is heavy but the trade off is I am hypoglycemic and prone to sugar lows and it gave me a qucik pick me up. But if your husband is a hiker I am sure he knows all these things. You can do this with IC, it just takes a bit more prep. What we did with one of the very skinney kids, his pk was chaffing his waist, so we duct taped a piece of thinsilite (the dense foam you can buy to sleep on)to his waiste band. If I go again I am going to do something like this to put infront of my bladder, because the waist band goes around your hips and can press on your bladder. A tip a friend gave me was to put a few wraps of duct take around my water bottle, it identifies your bottle as yours and comes in handy. Also works well as a blister pad if you get hot spots. I am envious I have always wanted to hike that trail or part of it. MG
signature: My are with you all. May you all find a way to peace and joy in your lives.

contents: I don't do big hikes, though I would like to. But for me, IC on day hikes hasn't been a problem. Usually after the first mile, my bladder gets used to the jostling and I can relax. The scenery distracts me from the pain, so that's nice. And while I'm drinking enough to stay hydrated, I'm peeing less because the water isn't just going straight through me while I'm on the couch. And I don't mind at all peeing in the woods. I like not having to worry about finding a bathroom! It's the ICers paradise - pee wherever you want, when ever you want.

Hiking Update Just wanted to let everyone know that I made it out alive! Maine was gorgeous. We flew (sea plane) in to Monson and started our hike into the 100 mile wilderness. Maine looks even better from the sky. It was tough going. My PFD flared up, IC flared up, but I kept hiking. It's the toughest miles I have ever hiked, especially Barren mountain. What a beautiful state. I'm looking forward to returning next year.

contents: Could anyone post if they are able to either of these with IC. I am getting quite depressed at the limitations of this disease. What is every one doing for exercise? Praying for a cure!! Suzi

Are leg presses a bad idea if you have pfd? I think by just using the machine your pelvic area tightens up. I thought I was being smart by just avoiding abdominal machines. I get the feeling if you have pfd you have to stick to cardio machines and arm workouts. Anyone have any thoughts? I appreciate your feedback. Thanks

That's pretty much what I've found - carefully selected cardio*, arm exercises, and stretches are what work for me without flaring (or flaring too much!) from exercise. *Walking, some elliptical trainers, some treadmills, rowing machines are the best for cardio for me.

Apparently God thinks I'm a bad-ass" ~Author Unknown Source - Pinterest " Current treatments: -IC diet -Elavil 50mg at night -Continuous use birth control pills (4-5 periods/year) -Heparin/Marcaine/Sodium Bicarb home instills at night 3-4x per week, more often if needed -Pyridium if needed, -Pain medicine at bedtime daily, as needed during the day several times per week -Antibiotic when doing an instillation to prevent UTI -Colace & SmartFiber to treat chronic constipation from meds, Fleet enema as needed -Dye Free Benadryl 50 mg at bedtime -"Your Pace Yoga: Relieving Pelvic Pain" dvd, walking, treadmill at gym -Managing stress= VERY important! -Fur therapy: Hugging the cat!

contents: Leg Presses My physical therapist has included leg presses as part of my exercise regime for my pelvic floor. But, I would strongly recommend seeing a physical therapist who specializes in pelvic floor disorders before trying to figure it out on your own. You don't want to hurt yourself or make things worse. My program is tailored for my issues.

contents: I recently had a bad flare, but it has calmed down finally and I've been feeling pretty good for the past 2 months. I decided to get out my Pilates DVD for people with pelvic pain (the one in the ICN shop) and do a couple of the workouts. Well...I did the warm-up and the arms Monday night, and the warm-up and legs Tuesday, and it put my in a horrible flare! I thought it was just the muscles reacting, but I took a muscle relaxer and it didn't help that much. The following night, I did a bladder instillation, and that did help. I was surprised that this caused actual pain, and not just muscle pain. Has anyone else had this experience. I'm going to stick with walking and stretching for now. Wish i could do more though. I miss my Denise Austin DVD's and pilates that I used to do before the IC

contents: I am curious if anyone here has had success bike riding without causing pain and flares. signature: ----- Kara 33 years old Symptoms began 12/10 - I had BV that went undiagnosed for awhile and developed urinary symptoms that did not go away after BV was treated. Visited the ER on 12/28/10 with extreme pelvic and lower back pain. Had cystoscopy with urogyn soon after which did not show any problem with the bladder. He did not think I had IC but I gave up cycling and tried the diet anyway. Symptoms initially improved some with diet modifications. I've had a few stretches of time where I felt that this whole nightmare just went away. I could drink and eat more freely and did not experience any issues. But eventually the pain keeps coming back. Most recently I had a flair start at the end of August. I was DX with a yeast infection. It was treated and I felt a lot better for a few weeks. Symptoms returned in September but the tests all came up negative. (??) Currently getting very bad flairs from sex, but not until days later (???). I'm now wondering if I have Vulvodynia, PFD or pudendal neuralgia (PN)/ pudendal nerve entrapment (PNE) Treatments I take Advil and Xyzal as needed and HYDROXYZ nightly. None of this is enough at the moment but I am very reluctant to take Elavil (slows you down, makes you gain weight etc.) and Elmiron is ridiculously expensive without prescription coverage. I also do pilates and other forms of exercise daily. It seems to help a lot but I still live with a great deal of pain right now, especially sitting! My next step is to find a new doctor who is very familiar with pelvic pain disorders and can recommend a physical therapist to work with me. Here is my precious family!
