contents: Exercise helps reduce my pain too. Also, the endorphins that are released during exercising are our body's natural pain killer. I am currently taking dance classes at my gym-sooo much fun.
signature: current meds- ditropan 5mg xl, neurontin 300 mg. zanaflex for pelvic floor spasms Most meds, IC meds included, cause me bladder pain previous med- elmiron( nothing after 8 months), atarax ( to sleepy), ditropan ( retention), Cymbalta, cystoprotek( worsened flare) has helped many others, aloe vera ( worsened flare), lyrica( irritated bladder. interstim implant (reduces my ic pain)January,2007

contents: I agree that exercise is good. I also mostly walk for exercise --- in good weather, outdoors, in ALL weather at the mall and in summer, I swim. I'd like to offer a small precaution: If you haven't exercised for a while, begin slowly, maybe even for as little as five minutes at first, gradually increasing time until you get to an acceptable regimen. Hugs, Donna

contents: I've been walking & increasing my time, and it has really made me feel better. For the past two years I've been following Weight Watchers. The first year I lost 30 lbs, but this 2nd one has been more problematic for me. I've finally gotten back on track & have shaved off 15 in the past month! I was running in occasional spurts during my walking time, but I noticed it was increasing my pain for the day so I've stopped for the time being. If I continue to lose weight & my IC is more under control I'd like to start running again eventually.
signature: (Me & my husband) IC- June '09- Elmiron, occasionally Urelle & Sanctura XR, also take joint supplement Vulvodynia- Aug '09- Elavil, Calcium Citrate & occasionally Lidocaine gel Semen Allergy- Nov '09- just preventive measures IBS- Jan '10- mild case, so just high soluble fiber diet Still have unidentified pain, possibly from PFD. Also migraines & hypertension. Follow IC diet when flaring, otherwise hasn't proven very helpful. Also follow some IBS diet guidelines. Exercise (2 miles walking per day, some yoga & mild kettlebells) and stress relief have also helped much! Lover of all animals! Visit my blog about living with IC & VVS: http://icthereforeican.blogspot.com/

contents: I do 60 min on the elliptical machine and I too feel much better after working out for that long. I don't lift weights though. I find that doing crunches sometimes irritates me.
signature: In memory of my beloved best friend in the whole world! Timmy (West Highland White Terrier) 11/24/04-9/5/2011 God Bless, Anna

contents: I can't walk or do the elliptical anymore without pain. Fortunately, I joined the local Y that has a pool and so far the water isn't bother me. I make sure to take clothes and whatever I need to shower off immediately after getting out of the pool. I have had some issues with irritation but it feels so good to move again. Sandra PS I am working up the courage to try the basic yoga class.

contents: I believe exercise helps me to feel better too. My guess is both the increased blood flow to clear tissues and the endorphins have positive effects. Also the toning and/or increase in muscles probably plays a supportive role in lessening strain on the bladder from daily activity. (Kind of like how after I began doing exercises specifically aimed at my back muscles I found I seldom experienced back pain anymore) I think exercise also has great psychological benefits. Vicki
signature: "The happiest people don't necessarily have the best of everything; they just make the best of everything they have."

contents: exercise I am glad to hear that exercise helps. i desperately want to lose weight but I do need to start slow. Thanks everybody for sharing.

Any Yoga fans My doc and Pt Doc said no running ,lunges,squats or bikes!! Yoga is an option along with walking. Has anyone tried Yoga. I did order the pilates ( sp) dvds off the site, just got them today anyone else try them? Sending good thought your way! C-Rella

I am a HUGE fan of Pilates--it has really helped me to recover from an issue with my lower back. It can make me flare if I'm already a little irritated though because there is so much core work. Just start slow!

i love doing yoga on the wii board. i'm really not into finding your center or anything like that...i do it for the stretches, but i've finding i really have a good time doing it when i get the chance!
signature: Current: -pelvic floor therapy, -weekly uracyst instills

contents: Originally posted by Stella1609 I am a HUGE fan of Pilates--it has really helped me to recover from an issue with my lower back. It can make me flare if I'm already a little irritated though because there is so much core work. Just start slow! Are you using the Pilates off the IC site? Thanks since you posted such a positive answer in help with the lower back pain I am going to try it. My lower back and right side really hurt. I need to do something. You also bring up a great point regarding core work. I know not to do direct sit-ups/crunches, but other exercises can be replaced and are actually better. Have you tried anything else? I have tried a few machine but I feel like the ball really is better on my back and I do not get as irritated. I will go slow and thank you so much for your post Stella. Wishing you a pain free day. Crella

Originally posted by an0bgobeloba i love doing yoga on the wii board. i'm really not into finding your center or anything like that...i do it for the stretches, but i've finding i really have a good time doing it when i get the chance! I have never understood the center thing. I just know that due to pain I am not flexibale at all. In fact I would like it to be a goal to have my nickname as rubberband Maybe then I could find my center an wish away my pain. I can ignore it most days but I have to keep busy as soon as I slow down it seems that is all I can think about. PAIN What parts of yoga do you think are especially fun? Well, I hope you have a fun a pain free day. I will look forward to your comments. Take care. Crella

Pain It is the same for me. As long as I am busy and in pain but I seem to be able to make it but as soon as I slow down I get slammed with the pain. Today I took meds at the first sign so I did not go into a major flare (my fingers, toes, and eyes are crossed ) For me the stretching in yoga helps if I don't over do it. I try to work at my own level. My problem is finding the time to be consistent and do it everyday. I seem to find time for everyone and everything else, but I am working on changing that. I think that is a must.

Pilates--watch out My PT says Pilates is almost never taught correctly, meaning you need a certified person in Pilates, a small class about 4, and the instructor has to constantly check your position and help you position not just act out at the head of a class. She also said the reason Pilates makes IC flare is the constant contraction of the core muscles that end in the pelvic floor. You need a every contraction of a muscle to be followed by the opposite or stretch in other direction. Also regretabley, I discovered that use of the Eliptical Trainer caused IC discomfort and my trainer said it causes sciatic nerve irritation. I asked my PT about this and she said any pelvic or IC problem is irritated by exercises that spread the legs wider than hip. Eliptical Trainer moves the legs on seperated foot pads, uses a stride that is longer than most average height females would stride, and keeps hips fixed. In natural movement your stride is right for body height, legs are closer together when you walk, and hips naturally undulate with a stride. Same goes for stair climbers. I do Yoga, weights, and walking.
signature: Sammi Meds: Melatonin 3mg @ bedtime if needed. Estrogen 1.5 mg troche and 0.1 mg Estrace cream. Diagnosis: IC, PFD (both in remission)

contents: Especially now that my symptoms have calmed just a bit since taking Aloe and Cystoprotek, I really notice that when I exercise I feel better. While I'm at the gym, my symptoms dissipate by about 70%. But once I get home and wind down, the need to go feeling starts up again really strong.
signature: Update! Feeling much better these days and no longer on any meds! Pelvic pain began July 2008. Urinary frequency began in November of 2008. IC Doctor says I'm one of the worst frequency patients he has had. Things that helped me the most: IC Diet, Elavil (30mg), Elmiron since June '09 (500mg/day). Previously tried and quit: one series of six instillations, Neurontin, Ditropan, Oxytrol, electric stimulation to the pelvic floor, Desert Harvest Aloe, Cystoprotek and Flomax, Hydroxyzine (50mg), Alesse Birth control (this helped my period flares when my symptoms used to be bad), PTNS (I think this helped). [/COLOR][/SIZE][/FONT]

I have wanted to start exercising for a while. I feel so lazy lately especially being a normally very active 26 year old with a 5 year old boy! For about the last six months though I can barely walk down the street with out getting very bad achiness and pain. I have read a post that described it as "labor pains" and that is EXACTLY what it feels like! I have endo and IC, so really not sure which one is causing it! Would anyone have any suggestions? I am starting to put on the pounds by not being active and taking the Elavil. Thanks again!
signature: Been suffering for 10 years, was recently finally diagnosed with IC and Endometriosis 05/09. Medications: Quit taking due to Pregnancy! Elavil 25mg Elmiron 100mg 3xday Pyridium as needed Birth Control pills for Endo Due 09/09/2010!

contents: It seems like you need some pain relief so you can exercise if the pain is that bad. I have been on a variety of rx meds and instills, but nothing knocked out the pain like Cystoprotek and when I added aloe it was even better. But I don't think my pain was as severe. I have frequency mostly and developed pain too, but the pain seems to be under control now. But if it's endo that's another story. I hope you find some relief. We all deserve it!

contents: I've noticed that when I ride my bike for a long distance or hike that those things seem to help my IC. You think that biking wouldn't because of the vibrations but it makes me feel better. I noticed that while I was doing physical therapy my biofeedback results were significantly better when I rode my bike a day before or something. It is helpful for working out those bladder muscles.
signature: I'm Taddy & here is a glimpse into my world & how I deal with this ridiculous and mysterious disease. My symptoms started in Feb of 2008, while in college at Penn State during which I was filming Season 2 of A&E's "Paranormal State." As one of the original cast members, I learned early on how difficult it was to travel and spend so much time in haunted, abandoned buildings with no bathrooms! I went to doctors with symptoms that felt like a UTI even though there were no signs of infection present. At the time I was prescribed all of the standard antibiotics for UTI's, plus I was drinking a ton of Cranberry juice...which only made things worse! After that a urologist put me on various medications like Vesicare and Deterol for overactive bladder. Those did nothing. In March of 2009 I underwent a bladder biopsy and found out that I have a mild case of IC. My urologist's explanation was that being on all of those different antibiotics caused my bladder to become inflamed. Diagnosed with mild IC by a bladder biopsy in March 2009. Before my diagnosis I was put on the standard antibiotics prescribed for UTI's. After they did nothing I was prescribed medicine such as vesicare & detrol for overactive bladder. After those did nothing, the result of IC came back... the doctor thought that being on all of those antibiotics made my bladder inflamed. Today I am still struggling with frequency and urgency. Physical therapy has been the only thing to help me with the pain. Yoga also helps my symptoms too. Figuring out what to eat has been a constant struggle for me. If anyone lives in the Pittsburgh area I'm looking to start a support group of ICer's. Anyone PLease feel free to message me about anything!!

contents: I have a book I am not using that I would be willing to pass along to someone else. It turns out I have other pelvic floor disorders but not IC. I had gotten this book, but never used it. It's called "Cystitis - A Time to Heal w/Yoga and Acupressure." Description is: An 8-week exercise program with special information on Interstitial Cystis & Urethral Syndrome." Written by Dawn R. Mahowald, Certified Yoga Instructor, and Dr. Emmey A. Ripoll, MD Here's an Amazon link so you can see more info, including some user reviews. http://www.amazon.com/Cystitis-Accupressure-Exercise-Information-Interstitial/dp/1403388709/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&s=books&qid=1258302482&sr=8-1 If anyone is interested, send me a PM w/your address, and I'll mail it to you.
Thanks twogirlsmom !
Twogirlsmom ~ Glad to hear you don't have IC!:woohoo: Were your symptoms similar to IC and that is why they thought you had it? How long were you misdiagnosed?
signature: Diagnosed Sept. 2009 based on symptoms Current Medications: Elmiron 100 mg 3x per day (started Sept. 09) Glucosamine w/MSM 1000 mg per day (started Oct. 2009) Added Glucosamine w/MSM and Chondroiton in Jan. 2010 Elavil 12 mg at bedtime (started Dec. 2009) Upped my dose to 25mg on Dec.14th) Lowered dose to 12 mg again on Dec. 28 due to the feeling that I was having slight retention IC Diet Ativan as needed .5 mg (this really seems to help with my symptoms) Update as of Jan. 6th 2010 Stopping Elmiron and weaning off of Elavil Started using 10 mg vaginal valium

contents: lttlwun - I was having excrutiating pelvic and abdominal pain for almost a year and also bladder control issues. I also have multiple sclerosis. A urologist I saw sent me for the IC testing (can't remember now what it's called, but the stuff with the catheters and the heparin, I think). Anyway, she said it might be IC but was inconclusive and just wanted to give me a bladder sling operation to deal with the bladder control issues. So I went to a good urogynecologist who did further testing and said I didn't have an IC bladder and sent me to pelvic floor physical therapy. It turns out that I had weakened muscles in the pelvic floor probably caused from childbirth and also from my multiple sclerosis, so the PT helped me tremendously. But I just feel for people with IC because of having spent so much time in pain, so I wanted to pass along the book if it could help someone. I will probably look into donating it to the site like the other poster mentioned if I don't get any responses. But thanks everyone for the "welcomes" and the support!

I have been in remission for a long time now. Recently, I started walking on my treadmill while I watch TV. The past 2 days my urine has been like acid. Ouch! I know I can't run anymore or use the elliptical (they both caused me terrible pain). So now, how do I live without exercise?! Is there anyway for me to keep walking and NOT have terrible acid urine. I hate IC.

Hi Sheila, Is there anything else that could be causing your flare? New food, sexual activity, tight clothing, stress? If not... Increase water to dilute the urine? Take TUMS (plain white ones) to reduce acidity? Drink baking soda in water to reduce acidity? (Don't do this if you have to follow a low sodium diet!) I hate IC too. I hope you find something that works,

contents: I agree with Kadi....maybe something else is bothering you and it is a coincidence as far as the walking is concerned. In addition to some of the things Kadi suggested, try breaking up your walk into smaller segments. If you have been walking for an hour, try to walk three, twenty minute periods instead. I hope you find something that works!

contents: Are you drinking more water to compensate for the hydration you lose while exercising? Maybe that could be the problem. I know when I exercise, I drink lots of water. I can't get through any exercise without lots of water. Sandra
signature: Link to the patient information, everything from What is IC? to Disability http://www.ic-network.com/patientlinks.html American Urological Association Clinical Guideline Diagnosis and Treatment of Intersitial Cysitis/Painful Bladder Syndrom http://www.auanet.org/content/guidel...ent_ic-bps.pdf

contents: You have been given some great advice, I agree with all of it. Up the fluid intake, break up your exercise time into a few workouts a day, make sure your incline is straight (starting out). Hope it works out for you Peace Blue
Thank you, everyone. I will try all of those suggestions.

I really want to get serious about trimming off the 15-20 lbs. I've gained since I got my promotion last year. My boyfriend used to talk about how skinny my waist was, and now he just talks about how big my butt is (in a positive way, but not something a girl really wants to hear). I'm on day three of a serious diet, and I want to try and get some exercising in too. I'm a sales manager at a department store so I'm always running around at work--makes it hard to get motivated to exercise at home. Anyways, is anyone else trying to take off a few pounds? I'd really like to have a friend to chat (or whine) with . My boyfriend told me tonight he doesn't think I can stick with the diet (which is fair, since he's seen me try and fail to diet in the past ). I REALLY want to prove him wrong, and get my awesome, curvy figure back! Thanks, Laura

Laura I'm in but need to know what diet you plan to use. My Daughter just lost 50 pounds by going on a no fat diet. I was surprised at the many foods she was able to eat. In the past the Atkins diet is the only one that really worked for me. I never was hungry and started to really hate food but ate anyway. Since IC hit me I'm thinking that is not the way to go. Maybe a low carb within reason would take the pounds off without feeling deprived. Strangely I find it is the people who work the hardest have the most problems taking weight off. Little time to think of themselves I guess. Hugs, Ziggy

I'm counting calories, with a goal of 1500 per day (averaging about 1600 so far, which is only day 4, lol). I'm not a big fan of no carb--I believe our bodies need balance. I know the only way I can stick with this is if I can still eat the same things my boyfriend is eating (we live together), just less of them. Like I had Sonic for dinner the other night, but I got small/medium everything and a grilled cheese instead of popcorn chicken. I've also stopped eating at the food court at work--I'm taking a granola bar and some crackers instead, plus lots of water. I really want to keep this up for the long haul, because I've lost lots of weight before and not been able to really maintain it. I'm at 182, a really heavy 12 (sometimes 14), 42-35-44, and my goal is 160, which will put me at a very light 12, almost 10, and about 39-30-40. This is a weight I was for years, and I know I can maintain healthily if I can get back in the habit. Right now, I'm in that awful initial period where your stomach is adjusting to less food and I'm HUNGRY all the TIME! I also want to exercise in some fashion at least three times a week, with a focus on things that will trim my waist (I have several Pilates and Bellydancing videos, so maybe one of each of those plus a nice, brisk walk with my dog each week). I have a spreadsheet made out and dated for me to fill in my calorie consumption, and with my goals for each week highlighted. I find I do better if I have a really careful plan. I just need someone to chat with, maybe to share each day's success with, even if she is doing different things. What are your goals??

If I lost 10 pounds I would be happy. My problem is that all my social events seem to be surrounded by foods. Tons of food. Usually each lady brings a wonderful dish. It's hard not to eat it. I am unable to work out. I have MS on top of IC. I get fatigued just walking through a grocery store. If you stick to 1500 calories a day you will loose weight. I wonder if there is something like crackers that are low cal and could take away that hungry feeling. Some of my best friends swear by Weight Watchers. It takes time but works. Many just go on line and not to meetings. I think I'm going with a reasonable low carb but include salad & veggies. Since I only have 10 ibs. to drop I think it may help me. Plus I would need to watch all my carbs in the future so all the weight does not jump back. It is hard when dealing with a mate. My Husband is on a very low fat diet. We would be eatting totally different foods. His foods make me sick to look at. I had to tell him to go buy what he needs and I would cook it for him. I'm not wise about low fat foods. Drives me crazy trying to read all those labels. Even if I lost 20 pounds I believe my IC belly would still stick out. Do you have that problem? I must wear shirts that cover that up. I often wear maternity jeans to make me more comfortable. IC is a stinker for sure. You sound like you have a good goal plan. I hope you can over-come the hunger problem. Maybe someone on here can give us some good advice. Keep in touch. I'll be thinking positive thoughts for the both of us. Hugs, Ziggy

I overate a little bit yesterday--we had a fashion show that my work was sponsoring and we ended up eating McDonald's for dinner and there was a whole mini buffet in the room where the ladies were changing and we had to wait with the clothes. I ate about 1980 calories, but I did my Pilates for weight loss video which burns around 200 calories, so I think I netted 1780 which isn't *TOO* bad! I'm weighing every morning, with goals for every week, and I've lost 3 lbs. in just these five days. I'm sure it was really water weight, but it's still nice to see the number change. Honestly, I remember when I was smaller (160ish) I could tell when I had some belly bloating, but now I can't really tell. Sometimes it feels a little swollen (harder instead of softer), but not too bad. IC-wise I think I'm starting to do a lot better--I think it might be the Elmiron kicking in (about half-way through month three). I want to talk to my doctor about stopping Elavil (20mg) when I go in on 11/16--I know it's not helping my weight loss and it makes me have crazy dreams! It does wonders for my IC/back pain though. Low carb with lots of veggies sounds nice and healthy! I'm terrible about eating salads and fruit, although I do like a lot of beans and broccoli. I don't really do any cooking since my boyfriend and I both work about 50 hours a week. I did find some TV dinners I really like that I'm doing for lunch at home sometimes (Michelina's). I don't like to take anything that needs to be heated up to work because the microwaves and fridges in the employee lounge is pretty yucky. People just don't take care of things that aren't theirs. I did already have a talk with Mat and tell him some nights he'll have to fend for himself, since I can't go pick up McDonald's or Wendy's every night anymore. He told me I should do low fat instead of low calorie--but I've done low calorie before and can understand it and can work with it. I've heard good things about Weight Watchers too! I think it's basically just what I'm doing--controlling food intake. I can't imagine having MS, let alone MS with bad IC . I'm sure it makes it harder to drop those last few pounds if you don't have the luxury of just jumping up and going for a brisk walk! Are you able to do any gentle, low-impact stretching? I know that makes me feel better, when I make myself do it! Anyways, thank you for letting me talk, and giving me a place to make myself accountable . I hope you are having a WONDERFUL day!

Laura 3 pounds is fantastic! Even if it's water weight, it's a darn good start. When I had a career and worked many hours a day I would do the same. Fast foods was all the strength I had left in me. Worst kind of food possible. Every Doctor I have heard on TV say that weight loss all comes down to the amount of calories we intake each day. Lately they are pushing for low fat diets as a life style. Cholesterol builds up plaque in the arteries. As we grow older that can cause strokes and heart attacks. Less fat, longer life. I guess I'm in denial as I'm not kidding, I hate to read labels. I don't understand them. Just when I think I've bought a correct food for my Husband he looks at it and says too much trans-fat. I feel like throwing the darn food at him. "Buy your own food" problem solved with him. Now the Holidays are upon us. It will take a lot of fortitude to get through that. Have you found anything that can make you less hungry between meals? I've seen "power" bars that say they help with hunger. I don't know much about them. Have a great week-end. Hugs, Ziggy

I've been taking One-A-Day weight loss vitamins and they help for a while, but they make me nauseous if I take them on an empty stomach (might not work for you at all if you are sensitive to vitamins). I'm taking the lowest dose of SlimQuick too--I took it in the past and when I went from 2 a day to 4 a day I felt really awful emotionally. Something about some herb they include doesn't agree with me if I get too much of it. So far, carrot sticks and Lipton Cup-of-Soup (45 calories a cup) have been good for my in-between cravings. If I'm REALLY hungry, a fun size Snickers bar is about 80 calories and really cuts the hunger (I guess because of all the protein). I like reading labels in general, but I've just never been good at eating low fat . . . I suppose I'm not doing TOO bad right now, but normally I eat so much fried food, which I know is not good for me. My body also just craves complex carbs--all kinds of bread, pasta, rice, etc. I eat more carbs than anything else. Maybe it was being raised in the south where we had a starch with every meal! It is frustrating trying to feed someone else. Mat expects me to bring home dinner, but never knows what he wants (unless I want something, then he wants something else, lol). I also do all of our grocery shopping. Hope you have an awesome day!

NOTE FROM DIET FORUM MODERATOR, KADI-- Most of the things Stella mentions are not ok for all IC patients. Vitamins, especially B & C, can be very irritating to an IC bladder. Cup of Soups contain preservatives & onion powder that can be a trigger also. Chocolate is a definite IC trigger food. I don't know what's in SlimQuick, but it likely contains added vitamins & minerals as well as preservatives. Unless you know these things are ok for you, please do not try these methods to lose weight. I am on Weight Watchers & there are lots & lots of healthy low calorie, low fat foods we can eat that will not trigger our symptoms.

contents: Ditto what Kadi said! I'm not really diet sensitive, except for really irritating drinks like alcohol/juices.

Hey, I'm trying to drop some pounds too. I have noticed that after 40 years old its harder to drop the pounds. My hubby just bought me the WII fit Plus. Its lots of fun. But I have been sick for the last couple of weeks and not been able to workout on it. I thought about buying diet pills but.. no telling what's in them that would harm the bladder. DIAMIC65

I'm really cautious about diet pills in general, regardless of IC or not. I always carefully read the ingredients, and avoid the hard core ones like Xenadrine, Hydroxycut, etc. I like ones with green tea--they help me with losing excess water weight at the beginning of a diet. I wouldn't take anything long-term, though--too much caffeine! I'm asking for a Wii Fit for my birthday . I love video games and hope that will encourage me to exercise, which I DO NOT love!

STELLA1609, You have no problem of drinking or taking anything with Green Tea? I thought that green tea was a no-no on the IC diet list? My sister in-law drop bunch of weight over the summer and she was drinking lots of green tea. DIAMIC65

I also have to agree with Kadi, a lot of what is mentioned is total IC flare material....also green tea, a HUGE IC flare..... Coming from not only a fitness enthusiest, but also one who works in the industry, eat a nutrition balanced diet from the canada food guide, and have a balanced exercise routine to follow. Not only do you need to eat a balance nutritious diet you need to also exercise. Eating a healthy diet and staying well hydrated, exercising, keeping a positive attitude and lowering stress in your life all contribute to a great weight loss program. If you are under stress your body holds onto weight....so deep breathe relax and do some yoga for part of your exercise regime. Try to fit in walks through the day if you can not fit in a full 30 minute walk. Fitting in little walks through the day will also benefit. Try to walk after a meal, it helps the diegestive system digest the food better, and burn more calories. Drink a glass of water if you are craving a "NO NO" food....sit down, relax and then decide if you are really needing that NO NO food, or if it is another underlying issue that is making you reach for that NO NO food. SOMETIMES we have other underlying issues that cause us to eat....deal with that so that you know what your emotional FOOD TRIGGERS are. CELEBRATE your food, enjoy prepping your food, eat it slowly, sit down and eat with every snack or meal. Do not settle for a bite here or there....make sure when it is meal or snack time you can enjoy it, eat it, taste it...NOTHING worse then eating on the go, and you have no idea what you inhaled.....?? (you will end up just eating again...) just a few pointers... Peace Blue

As I said, I'm not personally diet sensitive (with the exceptions I mentioned). I know how my body loses weight, and what my hang-ups are. I was just looking for the accountability of someone else going through the same thing, and not giving diet advice to anyone else (which is why I started the thread here, and not in the diet forum).

Hi everyone. I was hoping someone out there could help me out. I have 10 pounds that will not budge and I was wondering is anyone out there new of an excersice that wouldn't cause pain? At the moment, I do 20 minutes of yoga twice a week, and 30 minutes on the recumbent bike 2-3 times a week at resistence level 2-3. Thanks, Illnesses: Endometriosis IC Pelivc Floor Dysfunction Celiac's (gluten intolerant)
I have added weight training-a little at a time-and that is nudging my weight loss along. Good luck
The elliptical trainer (at lowest resistance & no arms) is fine for me. I also like the rowing machine at the gym. Walking is also really good for me.

contents: My hubby bought my the new WII FIT PLUS. I tried it a couple of times and I could feel the burn the next day. But couple of days later I came down with the H1N1. Read Eat to Live by Joel Fuhrman, it's wonderful. A sane helpful way to lose weight and keep it off that's helped me immensely, as many meds I take are associated with weight gain. It's not about willpower, it's about knowledge. This gave me the information I needed to cope with the increased appetite I have from my meds and now I'm slowly losing weight and feeling fabulous. I hope it helps you like it helped me. Amazon.com http://www.amazon.com/Eat-Live-Revolutionary-Formula-Sustained/dp/0316735507/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&s=books&qid=1256832034&sr=1-

contents: Dieting with I.C. Using the elliptical machine is great! I would step it up to 4-5 times per week at 30 minutes and add weight lifting 2-3X per week, starting at 20 minutes per session. Weight loss needs to address appetite control as well as metabolism. I eat alot of vegetables and fruit to appease my appetite. I eat 3 pears a day but you can also eat apples and blueberries or other tolerated fruits. I eat probably 6-8 vegetables a day for fiber and bulk. Eat them lightly steamed and avoid added fats. If you use a food journal, you can track your loss! I'm sure you can win this battle! Good luck!

signature: Formerly lorenab

contents: I'd start with yoga classes labeled "restorative yoga." These are very gentle classes that emphasize relaxation and stretching. As you get used to things, you can try other types - then you will need to decide if you would prefer to hold poses for a long time, or if you prefer flowing fairly quickly between different poses. Iyengar yoga will provide you with the first type, and anything called Flow or Vinyasa yoga will provide the second. I do Ashtanga yoga, which is power vinyasa-based, but I would avoid that and anything else labeled "Power Yoga" at first, until you know how your body will react, as it is a very active and athletic type of yoga - it's designed to create muscle heat and a copious, purifying sweat (and I can tell you that I end up absolutely dripping with sweat during class ).
signature: **** Jen *Diagnosed with severe IC in 2004 *Also diagnosed with PFD, fibromyalgia, chronic myofascial pain, IBS, migraines, allergies/asthma, dermatographism *Kept trying a million different treatments for all these things until I found what works, and I am doing okay these days with the help of a cocktail of medications and the InterStim, which was first placed in 2007. [I have had 2 revisions - one in 2010 when my battery died and had to be replaced, and one complete replacement (lead and generator) in 2012 after a fall on my stairs caused my lead to move.] *Current meds include Atarax (50mg at night), Lyrica (150mg twice a day), Xanax (0.5mg at night and as needed), Zanaflex (4mg at night), hydrocodone (10/325, every 6 hours as needed), Advair, Nasonex, Singulair (10mg at night), oral contraceptives, home instills containing Elmiron and Marcaine (as often as I need to do them). **I am not a medical authority nor do I offer definitive medical advice. contents: Reply to bowling with IC comments Thanks for the info everyone. I have become more confident that I can live with this. I'm not going to let it get me down. I have continued to bowl and so far so good no flares. I have changed my approach so that I don't bend down as low and I don't throw my ball as hard. The main thing is I'm having fun and my girls on my league are great mood boosters. I don't feel so isolated when I'm with them. I've a great family and that helps. The diet I feel has been a great help. I'm not on any medication at this point and hope I can continue. I see the Doctor next week so that he can touch base with me on my progress. Last time he liked that at this point I'm controling it with diet. When I feel stress coming on I breathe deep and count my blessings and decide if this problem is something to let bug me. God has been a good listener, when I worry I give that to him. I keeping my positive attitude it could always be worse. Live in the moment. Thanks again!

contents: I used to go run a few miles around the track nearby often. Now, I can't walk a mile without having a problem. Does anyone have any advice on how I should start to ease back into exercising? I am ready to try just about anything. I'm only 19 and I was a cheerleader last year and lifting about 125 pounds. This is a HUGE difference for me and I REALLY miss being active! Exercise is the main way I relieved stress and now I don't know what to do. Please please please help!! THANKS!

When I was first dx with IC, I completely stopped exercising. I was an avid runner and triathlete. However, since my IC has gotten better with Glucosamine MSM and Elmiron, I am now running again. I started really slowly, just walking around the block(in case I needed the bathroom). I gently stretched and started doing yoga. It took about 6 months, but I gradually added little distances of run/walking. I am now up to 3 miles with hopes of at lest keeping that us. Good luck to you. I know running really puts me in a great mood!

I can't run at all; it's just too painful, but I do take brisk walks. I spend time almost every day on my treadmill so it's a constant speed for long enough to help. My doctor recommends 30 to 45 minutes daily for me. Donna

contents: Thank you so much! I will try to ease back into it. Have you tried ab workouts? I have been too scared and worried what it might do to me. I will definitely start back to walking everyday and hopefully get involved in Yoga programs back at college. It is SO new to me since I have had gradual symptoms and then the Spasms kicked in about 6 months ago. Before that, I was running, cheerleading, and attending fitness classes daily! I am just glad for the HOPE that something will work! Thank you both VERY much!!!!:woohoo:

I can't take running, -too tough on the old joints. But I do aerobics, free weights, and abs. As long as my bladder is happy at the onset, I've never had a problem with it causing an IC flare. -Not even the abs. But if I'm in the slightest of flare to begin with, any exercise only seems to make matters worse so I take days off until my bladder feels completely quiet again. It's funny how much you can miss an activity once you get away from it! I agree, start off slow, a little at a time to get a feel for what you can do. Then gradually increase. Better safe than sorry! Vicki
signature: "The happiest people don't necessarily have the best of everything; they just make the best of everything they have."

contents: I can relate to the feeling of lack of exercise. When first diagnosed with IC I could do nothing. I went from personal trainging clients, teaching aerobics, running, teaching yoga....etc...to nadda for a few months. I continued my personal yoga practice and my own walking fitness routine until I learned more about IC and started on the IC diet to begin my road to healing. It has been a year for me, and in the last month I started slowly back into running (something I THOUGHT I WOULD NEVER do), I have been back to teaching yoga. As far as the running goes, I do not run outside anymore,and I run on my treadmill. My treadmill has extra padding on the belt so it helps with impact. I also do not run my full 3 miles. I may run one lap, then power walk the next lap, run another lap, then do an incline hill on the next lap. I mix it up through the entire 3 miles. I find I am actually getting my "running fix" while doing it. PLUS this type of exercise is really a greaty way to get the body in shape as it is interval training. Hang in there, respect your pain and your discomfort of IC, I defintitely DID not run or do interval training on the days my bladder was sore...I respected my body and would only take the dog for a walk...etc...on the days I would feel good I would put a better workout in. Peace Blue
Be sure to take it slowly if you are planning any exercise that can "jar" the pelvis. I strongly recommend talking with your doctor about starting a sensible exercise program. Good luck! Donna

contents: Running and ab work send my pelvic area into spasms! I hate that. I love to run and workout, it really makes me feel great and have lots of energy (plus burns off stress & calories). Just follwo the process, firts walking, then running 3 min, 5 minutes, and so on. I comletely avoid abs, even with yoga.

contents: Originally posted by Angeles I do yoga, it flared me the fist time but not it doesn't anymore. I'm glad I saw this. I used to do yoga for my back & since being diagnosed I decided to try it again, but the first time I did it HURT! So I didn't try it again... Maybe I need to ease my way back in. I was running in spurts during my walks, prior to finding out I had IC, but I noticed they made flares worse, so I've stopped for now. I've been walking (level 5 most of the time) to try to lose weight & feel better. If I can take more weight off & control the IC I'd like to start running again. I never thought I'd enjoy running, especially as someone who's BMI puts her in the "grossly obese" category. (Ick.) But it really made me feel good & I actually liked it. Good luck with the increased exercise, Manders!
signature: (Me & my husband) IC- June '09- Elmiron, occasionally Urelle & Sanctura XR, also take joint supplement Vulvodynia- Aug '09- Elavil, Calcium Citrate & occasionally Lidocaine gel Semen Allergy- Nov '09- just preventive measures IBS- Jan '10- mild case, so just high soluble fiber diet Still have unidentified pain, possibly from PFD. Also migraines & hypertension. Follow IC diet when flaring, otherwise hasn't proven very helpful. Also follow some IBS diet guidelines. Exercise (2 miles walking per day, some yoga & mild kettlebells) and stress relief have also helped much! Lover of all animals! Visit my blog about living with IC & VVS: http://icthereforeican.blogspot.com/

contents: I have found gentle yoga: Iyengar, Hatha, Kripalu,etc. to be great. Some days,it helps me for a while. Other days, it just prepares the rest of my body to combat the pelvic pain. I also do not have problems with Pilates, but I do not have PFD. I think one thing that's been hardest for me to accept with my case is that there are really no rules. Exercise can sometimes flare me, other times, not. IC is unpredictable for a lot of us, so my feeling is, it's better to get into some physical condition if possible.
with having ic is it ok to go swimmin in a pool.

I don't do well in a clorinated pool. We have a pool and use Baquacil. I know there are some public pools with salt filters --- you might check to see if there's one in your area. We also live near a lake and I can swim there with no problem, but usually just go in our pool at home. It wasn't terribly expensive --- we have an above ground one with a pool house we built so it will get warm enough to swim in. Donna

contents: We have a salt water system pool and I am able to swim in this without any issues, but I do shower and dry off immediately after swimming. We recently had a trip to Florida and the house we rented did not have a salt water system and I was terrified to go into the pool in case I would flare. BUT it was so hot...obviously...I went in the pool, I started off with only a few minutes, then would come out shower and dry off immediately. By the end of the trip I was in the pool swimming about 20-30 minutes. I would not stay in any longer because I found the water beginning to irritate around the 20 to 30 mark. As crazy as it sounds, I would even try to empty my bladder after each swim in the pool to avoid any urinary issues. BUt the biggest thing is start in small steps, go for a few minutes, then come out...shower immediately (get out of your wet clothes)....etc. Hope this helps. Peace Blue Originally posted by liljen211436 with having ic is it ok to go swimmin in a pool.
I believe swimming every day in a chlorinated pool is what contributed to my whole IC issue.

IC and Pools For me, the chemicals are a killer. I took a kayak polo class and about 3 days into it I had some of the worst flairs of my life. Just depends though on your individual triggers. It's so hard to say, "try it out" b/c trying it out often means pain. Good luck, and keep us in the loop

I think the best thing is to try it out. I am really diet sensitive and at a pretty bad place for my IC with pain all the time. But I can swim in a chlorinated pool. So maybe someone with moderate symptoms can do it too. I guess it just depends on what really bothers you. For me it is mostly food.

contents: Exercise makes my bladder symptoms worse, but I really need to find a form of exercise that does the least amount of damage. Walking and the ellipse machine (as well as stair stepper and rowing machine) kills me. A regular bike puts to much pressure/pain on my pelvis and so I was wondering about the recumbent bike. I tried it months ago and it made my urgency/frequency worse (just like all other cardio machines). My urgency/frequency is better lately, but I deal with considerable pain at times and exercise makes that worse. I have not tried the recumbent bike recently and I think I might give it a go. Anyone able to use this form of cardio? I love weight lifting and I'm trying to find a way to do that (resistance bands...? medicine balls...? etc...?). I am just wondering about others and the recumbent bike. I got some DVD's for kickboxing, yoga, and pilates, and I hope one (or more) of those will work out too!

contents: I don't like the recumbent bike. I get a sore bottom, sometimes I get flares, plus it doesn't give me a great cal burning work out. I use elliptical machine where you can also move your arms with. I don't have any problems when using that machine. I sometimes use rowing machine. I also do push ups on the wall. I want to try other cardio work outs like kick boxing or even some dances like zumba or salsa. I did try yoga but I didn't like it. I need something that will make me burn a lot of calories.
signature: In memory of my beloved best friend in the whole world! Timmy (West Highland White Terrier) 11/24/04-9/5/2011 God Bless, Anna

contents: Yes - found something that works! We bought one at Sears for $250. It's not a fancy one, but the wheel in the front has a smooth motion, and the chair does not tilt back too far. I am very short, so equipment that actually fits me is hard to find! I have a treadmill, stair stepper and ab machine. Can't use any of them, except ab machine. I need to keep my trigylcerides down and my good chol. up, so the aerobic bike is a life saver! I can get on it for an hour without it causing problems. I'm glad that you are active - it really helps us in life, and especially as we get older (as you can tell by the name - I'm not a young'un!). Go - feel great - live life!!! Grandma V.
signature: Live well - be HAPPY! GrandmaV Taking: NOTHING!!! I have done so well on the diet that he took me off all meds and I feel great! Hours of Prayer per day One day at a time Time to be grateful for what I have Philippians 4:13 for Pete's sake! Fur therapy and grandchild hugging therapy (Highly recommend) I am so thankful for my friends on the IC support network. My success has been made possible by YOU!

contents: cycling with ic After being disabled with ic I couldn't find an exercise that work without causing a flare, well let me tell you about the benefits of cycling. Most of us can't handle the pressure of the bike seat, well I've been working on this for sometime and I'm now again enjoying life. I have a special seat that works wonders!!!! It's a seat used for people who have problems with the pressure points of the average bike seat, the link is below: Adjustable Dual Pad Bicycle Seat - Easyseat http://www.bicycleseats.org/easyseat-bicycle-seat.htm?source=google I wish everyone the best, another site that may benefit others interested in cycling is why cycle? under cycling with disablities:http://www.whycycle.co.uk/index.php Thanks Marshmel

IC exercise I have a lot of trouble exercising as well - walking on a treadmill is especially bad for some reason. I recently joined a gym that offers workout program consultation. I told the trainer about my issues w/ high-impact workouts and he introduced me to this nifty machine called the Nu Step. Think recumbent stepper/elliptical if you've never seen it. I had never tried it before. But it's great! you can get a gentle aerobic workout without pummeling your feet and pelvis.

Hi everyone. My main symptom is frequency. I also have PFD, but have improved with that, but not 100%. My question is this, does anybody else improve during or after mild exercise? If I walk around, I often (but not always) can hold more urine. Sometimes if I am having a good day, and then I go do the elliptical machine at the gym, my symptoms get even better (but still not normal) about 15 minutes into the workout and last until an hour or so afterwards. On the other hand, I tried doing the breast stroke in the pool, and I got much worse. But it seems walking and the elliptical improve my symptoms some of the time, although temporarily. Why would this be? Does anybody else experience this?

Hello, I wear incontinence pads and need them daily; I experience stress incontinence and urge incontinece. They work well for fitness walking and other land based activities. At the beach, I don't worry since no one call tell. At the pool, I am worried about that pool urine dye showing up leakage. Does anyone know what to do without wearing an adult 'swim diaper' which is more for fecal incontinence? I had thought of putting a towel inside my bathing suit, even if it just masks the leakage. I cough and pee in the pool a bit. I am also running to the ladies room every 20 minutes. Last winter I would soak through an incontinence pad just while walking quickly although I went to the bathroom before exercise.
I think the pool urine dye is an urban myth but I could be wrong.
Her is a site that supports the idea of it being a myth http://www.snopes.com/science/poolpiss.htm
Have you talked with your doctor about your stress incontinence? I used to have a problem, but had it repaired. It's such a relief to not have to worry about it. Warm hugs, Donna

contents: Swimming in pads I have the same problem. Its hard to know what to wear when you go swimming. Im in a wheelchair and have trouble changing in public dressing rooms and in some places you cant get a wheelchair in a place to change. I used to wear Depends Fitted Briefs under shorts to swim in when they were less absorbent and came in a smaller size. I still wear them at the lake and less crowded areas. They still work and stay together good as long as you dont mind the bulkiness but they can be noticable around other people once you have been in the water for a while. I swam in these from the time i got out of pampers (around 10-12 years old) until I started using youth disposable training pants about 12 years ago. I found that its easiest to wear something similar to Goodnights training pants under a pair of shorts. They fit low in the waist and as long as you are careful about the kind you wear and get the snuggest fitting ones you can wear, they don't swell up enough to notice through the shorts and they don't restrict your movement. I wouldnt wear the name brand Goodnights though. The waistband comes apart too easy. I like SleepWells or SleepPants training pants. Some pads will also hold up (for urinary use) for swimming if you wear them in snug fitting underpants. I would test whatever you are going to use before you use them at the pool. Hope this helps.

Although I wasn't there I have heard about one of my friends sister peeing in the pool and the water around her turning purple. Not all pools are like that though, it might be best to test the waters... so to speak so you know that is some leakage does occur it won't go noticed.

Pool dye There is no pool dye, its a myth to scare people so they wont pee in the pool. It would cost them more than its worth plus it adds more risk of health issues than urine would. Thats also an advantage of wearing a disposable diaper while swimming, the urine doesnt go directly into the water. Depending on how snug it fits the urine stays in the diaper and gradually comes out when you start moving or the current carries out what doesnt go into the padding.

the hospital sends out a little build a healthy community pamphlet every few months, offering classes and free seminars on things like stress reduction, coping with illnesses, new baby, nutrition etc. They partner through a local yoga studio and offer a class in Gentle Yoga. It says gentle stretching strengthening and relaxation. Excellent for seniors and those recovering from illness. They also have a workshop for moving with chronic pain. I was wondering if anyone had tried yoga and if it helped or caused flares? They also have a stress reduction clinic or MBSR From their website Almost 30 years ago Jon Kabat-Zinn began offering a program of stress reduction to patients and physicians at the University of Massachusetts Medical Center. His program combined stress medicine and psychology with meditation, gentle yoga, experiential education, group dynamic exercises, and aspects of Buddhist psychology. Download our essay about MBSR He dubbed this 8-week 30 hour training Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction Training or MBSR. The 8 week program is described in detail in Jon's landmark book Full Catastrophe Living. A rich background of scientific research and subsequent books by Jon and others has made MBSR one of the most effective and respected methods of helping people though the most stressful challenges of life: heart disease, cancer, fibromyalgia, anxiety, depression, asthma, multiple sclerosis, sleep problems and much more. See the bibliography. Download our MBSR research summary. MBSR is a way of systematically, directly and thoughtfully working with the catastrophes of life and learning to accept and take charge even in the face of these challenges. It's a way of finding your inherent wisdom to face stress, chronic pain, and illness. Since that first program tens of thousands of people around the world have taken the course. Two decades of published research as well as tests and evaluations at our Clinic indicates that the majority of those who have taken the course report: * Reduction in pain levels * A lasting ability to cope with stress * Lasting decreases in physical and psychological symptoms. * A greater ability to relax * Greater energy I was thinking of maybe trying this as well. The Yoga is a 10 week session starting June 17, the chronic pain workshop is in August and then the MBSR clinic runs starting either July or October. Thoughts?

contents: I am a yoga practioner and have been practicing yoga for over 10 years, and have been teaching just as long. Yoga has saved me, and has given me my life back. If it were not for my yoga practice IC would be ruling my life. If I can give you some advice, I would say take the gentle yoga flow, you will be truly taken in by its healing. Just be careful of postures that place pressure on the abdomen, I find if my IC is in a happy place I have no issue with pressure of the belly postures, but if my IC is a bit aggrivated then belly postures are best not done. If I can offer advice or any info in regards to Yoga please let me know. Peace Blue Originally posted by Bluheaven the hospital sends out a little build a healthy community pamphlet every few months, offering classes and free seminars on things like stress reduction, coping with illnesses, new baby, nutrition etc. They partner through a local yoga studio and offer a class in Gentle Yoga. It says gentle stretching strengthening and relaxation. Excellent for seniors and those recovering from illness. They also have a workshop for moving with chronic pain. I was wondering if anyone had tried yoga and if it helped or caused flares? They also have a stress reduction clinic or MBSR From their website I was thinking of maybe trying this as well. The Yoga is a 10 week session starting June 17, the chronic pain workshop is in August and then the MBSR clinic runs starting either July or October. Thoughts?

thank you both! I think this will be good for me. I used to do yoga a few years back and was in the best shape and health ever. Now I have no muscle tone or core support and everything makes me sore.. from sitting or sleeping too long to standing to walking around the block. I'm going to talk with my phys therapist about doing the yoga and the practitioner at the studio about my issues so maybe we'll be able to modify things for me a bit if needed. Best part is it will get me out of the house and doing something.

contents: My PT told me that yoga would be fine, so long as it wasn't "power" yoga....which I have no intention of doing. My concern is that I might being doing poses incorrectly and do more harm than good. Any thoughts? Advice? thanks Sandra

contents: Hi there Sandra, if you can find any KRIPALU HATHA yoga DVD you will be fine. These type of dvd's focus on slow moving postures and breathing. Also You do not need to dig deep into your pocket to pay to go to a yoga class if you can find a good dvd then you are all set. It truly is unfortunate that these studios are expensive, as yoga is suppose to be a an offering to a client that is meant to be affordable. I wonder have you tried calling the studio and expressing your desires to go, but the "tightness of cash"...they may give you a rebate/deal. I know that my classes are not overpriced and even though there are clients of mine who can not afford the price I charge, I help them further by reducing the rate (confidentially). (just a thought...) Also if you look to the website http://www.kripalu.org/find_a_teacher/ you can do a search in your area to find a trained qualified KRIPALU Yoga teacher. I myself am a part of this data base along with many qualified instructors. You may also find a few dvd's on this site....if I can write that, they may edit this...if so you can email me and I will provide you with the info.... If you need any other guidance with reference to yoga I would be more then happy to hep. Peace Blue Originally posted by sailawaygrl My PT told me that yoga would be fine, so long as it wasn't "power" yoga....which I have no intention of doing. My question to those of you that have experience with yoga, do you think I can do something with a DVD at home or do I absolutely need to learn from someone in person. My concern is that I might being doing poses incorrectly and do more harm than good. Any thoughts? Advice? thanks Sandra

This sounds like a fantastic idea. I think where you have done yoga before your body will love you for bringing it back into your life. The great thing about yoga is that you need no muscle tone nor core strength. Yoga is so forgiving and you can build your core strength and your muscle tone through a very gentle yoga practice. I think your body will blossom once you start yourself on a program. I know when I was first hit with the pain of IC, the only thing that helped me though the pain was certain yoga postures...downward dog helped take the pressure off my bladder (lifting all my inner organs up, allowing the bladder freedom), it felt soooo good. Let me know how it goes, or if I can be of any more help.... Peace Blue Originally posted by Bluheaven thank you both! I think this will be good for me. PLUS you do not pay for the yoga flow!! HORRAY!! Hope his helps!! contents: IC is making me fat. Like...Orca fat. I was doing a little better and then with the diagnosis I am struggling with eating healthy foods - the healthy foods I really enjoyed aren't IC friendly. Food tastes blech and my constant flaring isn't compatible with exercise. It's been a rough couple of months. I also have sleep apnea which is impossible to treat with nocturia. Sleep apnea puts your metabolism at a standstill. I have a slow sculpting DVD. It's my stomach that is getting fatter but if I can't do the ab exercises I might as well do the other stuff. The pounds are quickly creeping on and I'm starting to panic. Any words of inspiration or advice?

My best advice is to take it slow in the beginning, and set realistic goals so that you can achieve them! I always had a tendency to jump in way beyond my level which usually meant I got hurt. IC hurt, muscle ache hurt, etc. Then it's easy to say I can't do this and quit. Or I expected big change in a short amount of time -that always leads to disappointment! I use a sculpting DVD every other day. I began with light weights and only did half of the reps, gradually increasing the weight and reps as I could handle it. Even if my IC is flaring I find I can at least do many of the arm routines. Good for you for wanting to exercise! There are so many other advantages to it beyond weight loss! Vicki

contents: That was helpful Ladies. I think maybe I should just be nice to myself. A five minute walk is better than no walk at all and it is a start as Donna said. I can always do 1/2 mile walk around our neighborhood. Easy. When I started pushing for more I gave up. I think that's where your advice came in Vicki. I go too fast too soon and then where am I? I walked 1/2 mile today. Go me!!!!

That's great! I really think it's a wise move on your part to start gentle. When you do decide to bump up the distance or level of difficulty, do it in small bumps. In no time at all you'll be amazing yourself with how much you've improved over when you first started! Vicki

contents: I love the ellipticals at the gym but when I asked my physical therapist about going back to the gym and using one she said flat out no. Not sure why she told me no but I was told to work up to 15 minutes on flat surface with no pain then 30 with hills and flat at a moderate training pace and then we could talk about the elliptical. Just thought I would share. I think you guys are in better physical shape than I am, I haven't worked out since this hit me in Dec '07. Sandra

contents: I used to do Taibo, Aerobics, Step Aerobics, and Jazzercise, and now I can barely walk for 45 mins straight. I am in the worst shape ever, but I try and keep as active as possible, but I do feel weaker than I ever was. My right knee is pretty ify though now as I pounded on it for years. The thing is is that I'm sure I would feel better all the way around if I could get an exercise program going. The endorphins themselves are great pain relievers.

I always preferred the elliptical to the treadmill because it was easy on my knees--lower impact. And I was able to read on the elliptical, but not so well on the treadmill. I too would love to have my own elliptical machine at home. I had a great gym not far away with free parking, NO annoying dopey music, friendly anti-spandex people and good rates, but it closed. Now I do a lot of dogwalking. Good for the dog, not as good for me.
OMG Goldfinch LMAO anti-spandex people!!!! I do the dog walking thing, but not enough much to my pooches dismay...

I've been doing the Wii Fit on the Nintendo Wii this last month and it's been helping me some. I don't think it's improved my IC condition but It has helped with some of the pain and with the depression. They have the Yoga exercises that will help you to align your pelvis and teach meditation. Bye Garth
I LOVE our Wii! I haven't done too much on the Wii Fit yet, but it has some very low impact exercises that I will be trying over the next weeks. Donna

contents: Wii Fit is definitely a revolutionary game console that has helped me enjoy exercising more often. Why pay a trainer to work with you when you have the Wii Fit male or female personal trainers available to you anytime. They also encourage me... LOL. My balance is not very good. Oh my Wii Fit age has improved since I've started doing it. I fluctuate between two or three pounds and when I weigh myself the Wii Fit board asks me what do I have to blame for my weight gain. I usually choose "Late Night Snacking".. LOL Which is true usually.

I really love my Wii. I play as often as it is possible because it always makes me happy. I love all games but my absolute favorite one is "We Ski". I love to go skiing in the winter and therefore it is the perfect game for me. Additionally I always enjoy playing with my friends and family.

I did two segments of an exercise DVD I was trying out yesterday. I'd tried one segment before & wasn't sure if it was ok for me, so what was I thinking trying two? Duh. Flared all night & today too. Dumb, guess I should stick to walking, elliptical trainer on easiest setting for 10min only & physical therapy exercises for now. Ow. Hope I'm better for work tomorrow.

contents: Kadi, I know how you feel! At times, I try and do what I used to do in the "glory days" of optimal health. IC struck me down when I was at the peak of my health... running 5-10 miles/day, 2 hours of lifting weights 3 days/week. I worked out wicked hard and loved it. I hate this sedentary life now. I am so unhappy (exercising is a great mood enhancer). I gained weight due to this sedentary lifestyle. Sigh... I guess we just have to take it one day at a time and then go slowly... and if we overdo it, pamper ourselves. I've learned that pampering is necessary some days! Feel better!

contents: Tomorrow I turn 26 and I am about 20 pounds overweight. It's not a good feeling knowing that you are on the road to 30 and already overweight. I have been trying to diet but it's not working, I haven't lost any weight. The last time I tried to exercise was about a year ago and I had a HUGE flare up. It was easily one of the worst I've ever had. I'm been scared to exercise again because of it. I want to know what other people do to exercise that doesn't irritate their bladder.

The main thing is to start out super slow. Try walking first, flat ground, short amount of time, not fast. Then increase time, then speed. When walking becomes easy & you're ready for something more, you might try starting out really small at the gym. Elliptical trainer-5 minutes, easiest setting, lowest tension, then increase time over weeks, not days. Rowing machine - 5 minutes, same idea as the elliptical. When I started back to exercising, I had a consultation with a physical therapist who evaluated me, gave me home exercises & designed a gym workout for me. It was super helpful & I'm going back for 2 refresher appts during my Christmas break. It is possible to exercise with IC, just maybe not the same type or intensity as before IC... (I've lost 22 of the 45 pounds I gained with IC & inactivity. Really wanting to lose the last 23 pounds, so have re-joined www.sparkpeople.com It's a free diet site where you log your food & exercise, has tons of informational articles & motivational tips. It's how I lost the 1st 22 pounds! Want to join with me?)

contents: I dogwalk most days and it makes me feel better. Level is definitely easier than up and down. If I'm on a rocky trail the uneven surface, especially a steep downhill, can be punishing to a tender bladder. Sometimes, after at least a half hour's walk, my bladder actually seems a little lighter or more relaxed, and less "there." The elliptical trainer is good because it is relatively low-impact--your feet don't leave the pedals.

Walking will help with the pounds and is not at all hard on the bladder. As your stamina increases, try adding in some low-impact exercises--like those mentioned. If you can find a comfortable bike seat, cycling is a great low impact exercise as well. Good luck! Diana

contents: Walking and swimming are the two things I can do without pain. I have a treadmill that I haven't been able to use due to a strained hip and back, but hope to be back on it soon. Donna

contents: Look, lose weight is very possible. First can I ask if you are taking medecine that can influence that? When I was 20 I fell into depression (again) and my weight was about 71 kg (and i'm only 165cm tall!). I had psychological counceling, and that helped to go out of my depression and also release some kind of psychological wall that I had when thinking about my figure. You know, I didn't even want to go out because I was ashamed of my appearance!! So, at some point (after eating the biggest ice cream ever to seal a promise with myself) I bought a book on nutrition and started to learn. Made a plan and stuck to it. Wow, I lost exactly the amount expected every week.. I was very pleased of myself. I used to do relaxation exercises for 30m a day. It seems silly, but that was definitely the winning strategy for me. Those techniques help the will to maintain the strenght and be constant. I couldn't do much of exercise myself so I decided to do at least 30m of quick walk every day. I did the diet for 3 months, then a little bit of relief than other 3 months etc.. for one year. I went down to 52 KG!! Now I'm 55-56 but never been as big as before :woohoo: I did complex calculations at that time, but now there are some cool tools. I now use Gyminee.com Try it out and maybe add me as your buddy! Good luck

Before IC I was an intense swimmer who had been on a team since I was four. Last year when the problems began I had to quit my college team. It took me a few months to adjust to this and try a different form of exercise. I found that walking on a treadmill or outside is quite easy on the bladder. I started out walking very slowly and then built up to fast walking. I absolutely cannot run on a treadmill or outside for more than 2 minutes, I've tried with bad results ( only like 10 minutes). I can, however, run on an elliptical probably because it is much smoother and there is no hard impact. I have also been able to take a fitness class called Zumba, which does have some medium impact moves. I modify it slightly though, and never do the jumping movements. I haven't had the guts to try lifting yet as it caused me problems when I first had problems. However, when I'm flaring I can't do any exercise it all seems to make me feel much worse.

Thank you Thank you to everyone that responded I am going to check out the websites suggested and start out with some low impact exercises and going it slow ... it's very encouraging to know that people are able to exercise with IC

Walking I would say walking is good, but not to fast and not for to long. A medium paced 30 min walk everyday should help you quite a bit. I got a little risky last week and I was feeling so great and had been walking that I decided to do the elliptical for 10 min...OUCH!!! FLARE!!! I will stick to walking. Walking is an excellent form of exercise and also some arm workouts to help with the upper body.
signature: As I think, so I shall be.... I have had IC for 4.5 years now. I also have fibro, sjogren's syndrome, and vulvadynia. I live in immense pain. I often daydream of life before this demon . When I feel good, I embrace it with all of my heart! I pray that someday I will feel 100% again, we all deserve it! Medications: Vicodin (as needed, so daily!) Percocet (morning & night) Klonopin (.5 mg at night) Zanaflex (2 mg each night) Thyroid med for low thyroid (hypo) leuko-stim Vitamin D (4000) Diagnosis: IC, CFS, Fibro (it is really bad it has taken so much from me and changed my life), Anxiety, Depression from it all, & Hypothyroid.

contents: I have been working out with IC for a long time. I like ellipticals, walking on a treadmill, weights, regular walking, and especially stretching/core work. Stretching and relaxing your pelvic muscles is the best thing you can do. One caution, it seems almost everyone commenting is getting flares. Here is my take on that ... if you have not been working out, your body will secrete lactic acid from your muscles after your workout. This will burn in your bladder and feel like a flare. It can be easily seen once you are aware of it because you will feel fine while you are working out than after ... 30 minutes or so you will start to feel the burn. The best way to get back into exercise is start out with a low enough intensity that your muscles are not stiff and you are not getting any burn in your bladder. Start very small, add to it very, very slowly after a few months you will be able to workout without any big problems.
signature: Bill

contents: Biking is out for me! I thought, "I can do this" and started slow just going around the block for 5 minutes. I am, 10 hours later, still in the worst flare I have had in months. I am disappointed but what can you do? I wish I could use my elliptical as it does not bother my bladder but does mess seriously with my peripheral neuropathy in hands/feet. Sigh. I guess I'll go back to walking.... Waah.

contents: Exercise and IC Not sure why exercise would cause IC to flare?? Unless you have simultaneous issues with pelvic muscles? I have fibro and IC and its the fibro that flares with some exercise. What works best for me is Curves or walking.

contents: Exercise and flares My first four years with IC were mild enough that I could do anything without flares. Now, that is not the case and I've had to work hard to find activities that don't cause pain, burning, urgency, and frequency. I know that vibrations -- riding in a car or airplane, or walking on a treadmill -- cause irritation to my bladder and cause a flare. I rarely sleep the night of a trip. Riding a bike is known to put pressure on the bladder and pelvic area so it is common that it will cause a flare and is known to cause impotence in men as well as other problems such as numbness and pain in those who do not have IC or other pelvic disorders. Surely was my worst exercise-induced flare ever over the weekend! Jarring exercises such as aerobics and dance seem to jostle and cause flares. Also, exercises that use my stomach muscles or thigh muscles when I try to go from zero to 40 cause light flares. For instance, if I have not done the elliptical in a long time, I can't just jump on and do what my body and endurance will allow -- flare! Wear out my thighs and my bladder hurts. Instead, I limit myself to 3 minutes for a few days, and then 5, and so on, until I am going 30-40-50 minutes at a time with no reaction. By doing that, I can do so without a flare. The same goes for lower-body and core pilates (specifically New Dawn for people with pelvic pain). So, I do believe that those of us who have got it bad and flare from exercise can inch our way in to some exercises. So, where I am successful - elliptical, New Dawn Pilates, Yoga for people with IC, walking, swimming, kayaking, boxing with minimal lower body movement or for short periods of time.

contents: Yoga I'm about to try yoga since I've lost most of my strength due to endo/ic/possible fibro, and I have some nasty back pain. My doc recommended either restorative yoga or yoga for back pain since it is a lot slower and gentle. I won't be attending a class (too expensive!) so I bought a DVD titled, "Healing Yoga for Aches & Pains". We'll see how that goes! I heard a rumor yoga can help with pain management too! Could be beneficial all around!!
signature: Kris :-) Diagnosed with: IC, Endometriosis, Vulvodynia, IBS, and Pelvic Floor Myalgia... what's next!

contents: yoga? I have been doing yoga classes and seem to be getting flares a few hours later that lasts for days. I have pelvic floor dysorder and a nerve problem. Anyone notice flares after yoga? Krisssy

Yoga The only yoga I do is the one sold for people with IC. I find that if I try to do the whole DVD without building up I get flares. You might try only doing 10 minutes for a while and see how you fare. You won't feel like you got anything out of it, but that's OK as you want to build up. If after 3-4 times of going 10 minutes you are OK, then do 15-20 minutes and so on. Build up that way and you might be able to tolerate it. Or, you might have to cut out moves that over-exert the back, stomach, or thigh muscles. Good luck!

contents: Yoga Hi there Ladies, you have to remember there are 100's of styles of Yoga, and you have to be careful as to which class you partake in, or type of DVD you purchase. You want to be looking for a class/or dvd that is hatha style (which is a slow moving meditative yoga, focussing on your breath and slow movement). Titles such as RESTORATIVE YOGA are good choices, or Yoga for backpain. If you go on utube, you can find some amazing yoga online...or check out the site below it is a good link to some restorative postures for Yoga.. I am thinking the network may block this...but if they do...oh well...if you are interested I can pm them to you.... Just make sure your Yoga is a gentle practice, it is about honouring your body, listening to your body and responding to it gently. Peace Blue Last edited by SharonA; 01-15-2009, 03:05 AM. Reason: deleted website address that linked to online sales

Everyone's different. GOtta find what works for you. When I started biking for transportation, I lost 20 pounds almost instantly and my horrible thighs. Swimteam is a guarantee shoe in for maintaining no matter what, especially during highschool and college. Swimming, biking, and hiking are things that never seem like exercise, but I enjoy, and then I end up working out around 2-3 hours a day without realizing it. When I cut out more junk (not dieting though), it makes a 10-pound difference for me on average. I hope you find what works for you. I am still considered overweight, though I keep fit enough and all that. A friend of mine is approx. 240 pounds (6 foot 4), but does triathlons and is training distances for a half-ironman, which he is doing in May. He's just built big and can't help it. It's that way for some people. A dietician or somebody might be able to assist too.

kimh Originally posted by UCSB I have been working out with IC for a long time. I like ellipticals, walking on a treadmill, weights, regular walking, and especially stretching/core work. Stretching and relaxing your pelvic muscles is the best thing you can do. One caution, it seems almost everyone commenting is getting flares. Here is my take on that ... if you have not been working out, your body will secrete lactic acid from your muscles after your workout. This will burn in your bladder and feel like a flare. It can be easily seen once you are aware of it because you will feel fine while you are working out than after ... 30 minutes or so you will start to feel the burn. The best way to get back into exercise is start out with a low enough intensity that your muscles are not stiff and you are not getting any burn in your bladder. Start very small, add to it very, very slowly after a few months you will be able to workout without any big problems. Likewise, I have been working out with this condition for over 12 years. I workout in the gym consistantly, 3 to 4 days per week for 90 minutes each day. I use weights, the elliptical machine and treadmill. I take a 45 minute spinning class 3 times a week and also take a pilates class. Biking seems to bother me the worst, but my discomfort has never stopped me from performing the exercises. My symptoms may be more typical of OAB than IC. I take 4 mg of Detrol LA, Cysta-Q supplement, use Azo for pain when needed, and take Pre-Lief as needed. These meds were just started Feb. 10th so I am very new with them. So at this juncture, exercise has not made me feel any the worse for it. In fact, exercise actually boost my morale and keeps me from getting too depressed:woohoo:
